TDD is a 'trap-door function': easy to create production code from tests, but virtually impossible to create comprehensive tests from existing code
If you lost all production code but kept tests, you could rebuild a better system; if you lost tests, the code would rot with no way to stop it
The quality of production code depends on tests, but test quality is independent of production code—a fundamental asymmetry
Every test can be reduced to three statements (Given, When, Then) and should be cleaned before being made to pass
money_quotes: The tests are the most important component in the system. They are more important than the production code.
TDD is a trap-door function. It's easy to go from tests to production code, but hard to go the other direction.
If we lose the production code, we end up with a better designed system that stays clean because it has tests. If we lose the tests, then the production code rots.
One of the goals of TDD is to be able to trust your test suite to the extent that, if it passes, you know you can ship!
We let them rot because we think our effort is better spent on the production code. We think that time spent cleaning tests is wasted. But, of course, that's nonsense.
No test ever needs to have more than three lines!
First make the test right. Then make the test work.
We should give our tests the highest priority. That is what 'Test First' really means. The Tests Come First!
posts.key_insight: Tests are more valuable than production code because they are the specification from which systems can be rebuilt, while the reverse—deriving tests from code—is nearly impossible.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob pushes back against the growing trend of programmers abandoning acceptance testing tools like Cucumber, FitNesse, and JBehave. He argues that acceptance tests were created to solve the fundamental problem of miscommunication between business stakeholders and developers - the single greatest cause of software failure. The fatal error was not the tools themselves, but that programmers co-opted them instead of having business people write the tests, and then compounded the problem by tightly coupling tests to volatile UI elements. The irony is that businesses already write formal test specifications - they just execute them with the most inefficient platform possible: human QA testers. Rather than abandoning acceptance testing, we should redirect QA effort from manual test plans to automated ones that programmers can execute.
key_points: Requirements miscommunication between business and developers is the single greatest cause of software failure
Acceptance testing tools were designed for business people to write formal, unambiguous specifications - not for programmers to write tests for themselves
When programmers write acceptance tests, it defeats the original purpose of bridging the communication gap
Coupling acceptance tests to the UI (via tools like Capybara) creates massive maintenance burden when interfaces change
The logical solution is to redirect QA's manual test-writing effort toward automated acceptance tests that programmers can execute
money_quotes: The most costly problems in software development are problems with the requirements.
Using these tools it is possible for programmers to know what needs to be done, and know when they are done.
It appears that the business already does write the tests; they simply use the most inefficient execution platform possible: Humans.
What is the point of having the programmers write the formal specification so that the programmers can then understand it?
When you abandon the unit tests in favor of acceptance tests, you are abandoning the tests you are supposed to write in favor of tests that you are not supposed to write.
The First Rule of Software Engineering: Don't depend on things that change a lot!
What changes more than the UI? Nothing! So let's couple all our tests to the UI. Oh, yes! Lets! What a good idea!
posts.key_insight: Acceptance tests fail not because the concept is flawed, but because programmers hijacked tools meant for business stakeholders and then blamed the tools when they didn't work as intended.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Object-Relational Mappers are fundamentally misnamed because objects and data structures are opposites—objects expose behavior and hide data, while data structures expose data and have no behavior. He traces the history of how OO programming became subservient to relational databases, forcing developers into a 'lock-step march' around database schemas rather than designing freely. ORMs don't actually create objects; they merely migrate data structures between disk and memory. The real problem is that developers design their business objects to mirror relational schemas instead of designing behavior-first applications that treat database tables as just another resource.
key_points: Objects are abstractions of behavior with hidden data; data structures are public data with no methods—they are exact opposites
ORMs don't create objects from relational tables; they simply migrate data structures from disk to RAM while changing their form
Too many applications bind their business object design in lock-step to their relational schema, letting database structure dictate behavior
If you design an application by grouping behaviors by responsibility without considering the database, you get an object model that looks nothing like a relational schema
Tables are resources that business objects use, not business objects themselves
money_quotes: An object is not a data structure. Repeat after me: An Object Is Not A Data Structure.
From the outside looking in, an object is an abstraction of behavior, not an abstraction of data.
How do you store an abstraction of behavior in a database? Answer: You don't!
Hibernate doesn't turn anything into an object. All Hibernate does is to migrate data structures that are stored on a disk to data structures stored in RAM.
Why should the message pathways of an application be bound to the lines on a E-R diagram? What does the behavior of the application have to do with the structure of the data?
Tables are not business objects! Tables aren't objects at all. Tables are just data structures that the true business objects use as a resource.
Design your applications to behave first. Then figure out a way to bind those behaviors to the data brought into memory by your ORM.
posts.key_insight: ORMs don't bridge objects and databases because objects are behavior abstractions while databases store data structures—design your application's behavior first, then treat the database as just a resource.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob contrasts his delightful experience assembling a well-packaged bunk bed from Walmart/Dorel with the frustrating software failures of healthcare.gov and Apple's OSX Mavericks upgrade. The bunk bed packaging showed meticulous care—perfectly fitted cardboard, shrink-wrapped labeled parts, clear instructions—while the software products displayed carelessness at every turn. Healthcare.gov had absurd password requirements, rejected valid date formats without explanation, and timed out his email confirmation within seconds. Apple's installer created a deadlock with its own updater, gave no time estimate for a 34-minute process, and bombarded him with context-free calendar popups. Martin argues these failures stem from prioritizing schedule over users, and challenges software professionals to choose whether they want to be associated with organizations that care.
key_points: Well-designed physical products demonstrate care through thoughtful packaging, clear instructions, and attention to the user's experience
Software failures often stem from disconnected teams—the programmer who didn't read the bureaucrat's date question, the testers who never checked common workflows
Organizations that ship broken software are prioritizing their schedule over their users' needs
The carelessness in these examples was preventable—these weren't complex edge cases but basic functionality that went untested
Software professionals must ask themselves what kind of organization they want to be part of and take action accordingly
money_quotes: From the moment I opened that box, it was clear to me that someone cared.
Upper and lower case, numbers and punctuation, DO NOT INCREASE SECURITY. What number do people use? They use the number 1. What punctuation do they use? They use a period.
The programmer did not write the questions! Some bureaucrat wrote the questions. The programmer never talked to that bureaucrat.
This is a classic deadlock! Both processes were waiting for the other to complete.
They cared about their schedule not about me.
Is that who we are? Is that how we want our industry to be viewed. Are we: The Careless Ones?
They released software that they knew did not work properly and had not been tested enough.
What kind of organization do you want to be associated with? One that cares? Or one that is careless.
posts.key_insight: The difference between excellent and terrible products comes down to whether the people building them genuinely cared about the user's experience or simply cared about meeting their schedule.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that the healthcare.gov failure represents a profound failure of the entire software industry, not just government or management. He draws a parallel to the Challenger disaster, where engineers knew of the danger but failed to stop the launch. The post contends that software professionals have an ethical obligation to refuse orders that will cause harm, similar to doctors following the Hippocratic Oath. He warns that continued high-profile failures like this could lead to government regulation of the software industry, which he presents as an undesirable but increasingly likely outcome if the profession doesn't self-regulate.
key_points: The healthcare.gov failure harmed millions of people and represents a failure of the software industry, not just government
Software professionals have unique knowledge that creates ethical responsibility - we cannot hide behind 'just following orders'
Like the Challenger engineers who knew but couldn't stop the launch, someone on healthcare.gov knew it would fail and didn't say NO loudly enough
Professionalism means being willing to refuse orders that do harm to others
High-profile software failures may eventually lead to government regulation of the industry
money_quotes: A critical public policy is being thwarted, not by the failure of the policy, but by the failure of a web site.
Government and management don't know how to build software. We do. We were hired because of that knowledge.
It's easy to blame the managers. It's appropriate to blame the managers. But it was the engineers who knew.
They failed to say: NO!, loud enough for the right people to hear.
I will create software for the good of my users according to my ability and my judgement and never do harm to anyone.
Professionalism is the willingness to refuse to obey orders that do harm to others.
A professional has the knowledge and responsibility to say: 'No!' as loudly as necessary to prevent harm.
How many more healthcare.gov debacles will it take before it does?
posts.key_insight: Software professionals bear ethical responsibility for the systems they build and must be willing to loudly refuse orders that will cause harm, or face the consequences of industry regulation.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob questions the software industry's approach of hiring masses of inexperienced developers to meet growing demand. He argues that the problem isn't a lack of raw manpower but a lack of skilled professionals. Using analogies to heart surgery and pilot training, he illustrates that some tasks require expertise rather than more bodies. The proliferation of coding bootcamps and the declining quality of CS graduates are symptoms of a flawed strategy. He suggests the industry is creating 'cannon fodder' rather than competent craftsmen, leading to the widespread crashing and burning of software projects.
key_points: The exponential demand for software has led to desperate hiring of underqualified developers
Coding bootcamps and weak CS programs are producing novices, not professionals
More developers doesn't mean better software—it often means more bad code
The industry has become desensitized to software failures, accepting them as normal
Complex work requires skilled professionals, not masses of barely trained workers
money_quotes: Perhaps, instead of hordes of novices, we need a small team of professionals.
If one doctor can transplant a heart in ten hours, can ten nurses transplant that heart in one hour?
Or do we want less code? Less code that does more. Much less code, written much better, doing much, much more?
Is our industry doing the equivalent of offering free rides to hopeful software developers, calling them pilots, and throwing them by the thousands into airplanes just to watch them crash and burn?
Like the frog in boiling water, have we become so inured to the fact that software systems crash and burn that we shrug it off and say: 'That's just the way software always goes.'
Do we really need to keep on recruiting and training cannon fodder to throw at software projects?
posts.key_insight: The software industry's crisis isn't a shortage of developers—it's a shortage of professionals who can write less code that does more.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob clarifies his earlier 'Hordes of Novices' post, emphasizing that he welcomes newcomers and coding bootcamps, but opposes placing untrained novices on critical projects without supervision. He argues that software development requires years of supervised probationary experience, similar to medicine, law, and skilled trades. The industry's youth—both as a 60-year-old field and in its workforce demographics—means there aren't enough experienced mentors to guide the influx of young programmers. Companies implementing proper apprenticeship programs will gain a competitive advantage in the coming decades.
key_points: Novices need careful supervision, code review, and continuing education before gaining full autonomy
A CS degree or bootcamp completion is not sufficient to produce a professional software developer
The software industry lacks experienced mentors because it's young and many veterans left coding for management
The median age of programmers has decreased as most hiring occurred in the last decade
Companies with solid apprenticeship programs will have a competitive advantage
money_quotes: What we don't need is to continue in the absurd belief that a degree in computer science, or the completion of a boot camp, is sufficient to produce a professional software developer.
Novices should not be able to check code into the main line.
The fact is that gaining competence in software development requires several years of supervised probationary experience.
Interns don't do heart surgery upon getting their medical degree.
Most programmers are young.
We sorely need some battle hardened leaders to guide the novices.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's rapid growth has created a dangerous imbalance between novices and experienced mentors, making structured apprenticeship programs essential for producing competent professionals.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on the 14th anniversary of Kent Beck's 'Extreme Programming Explained', arguing that XP succeeded precisely because its practices outlived the name and controversy that surrounded them. He traces how the 12 original XP practices—from the Planning Game to Coding Standards—have become mainstream despite the churn of Agile certifications, process fads, and project manager takeovers. The practices persisted because they're grounded in stable values: communication, simplicity, feedback, and courage. True success, he argues, is when an idea becomes so embedded in everyday practice that people stop thinking about it consciously—just as happened with structured programming before it.
key_points: The 12 XP practices, though once wildly controversial, are now considered mainstream ideals that teams aspire to achieve
The Agile movement was 'taken over by project managers who all but pushed the programmers out', leading to 'Flaccid Scrum'—adopting planning without the other 11 practices
Despite constant process churn (Kanban, Lean, certifications), the core XP practices have remained stable while only their names evolved
The practices persist because they're grounded in stable values: communication, simplicity, feedback, and courage
True success for an idea is when it outlives its movement and becomes invisible—simply 'what we do'
money_quotes: The implications were an Earthquake whose tremors haven't even begun to die down.

Indeed, it was so controversial that whole books were published describing how this couldn't possibly work... Ah, but I shouldn't let those old feelings overtake me… Because, after all, they're gone – and we're still here.
We saw the adoption of the planning game (i.e. SCRUM) without the other eleven practices; and we saw that strategy fail – becoming what Martin Fowler called: Flaccid Scrum.
The names change. The practices remain. The practices are persistent.
Amidst all the chaos of humans jockeying for position over one-another. Amidst all the messiness of human avarice, passion, and pride. Amidst all that politics, the practices persist.
An idea succeeds when it outlives the movement that spawns it and simply becomes part of our everyday lives.
Nobody even thinks about Extreme Programming any more, we are all just trying to do it.
posts.key_insight: The ultimate success of a methodology is not fame or adoption of its name, but when its practices become so universal that people practice them without even knowing their origin.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob and his son Micah visit Marion Correctional Institution to participate in a coding event with inmates. The piece chronicles their journey through security checkpoints into the prison, where they join other volunteers to pair-program with incarcerated men learning Java. Through multiple 40-minute coding sessions, Uncle Bob discovers enthusiastic, capable learners ranging from beginners to journeymen programmers. The narrative deliberately blurs the line between a normal coding meetup and a prison environment, with roll calls and tattooed knuckles serving as jarring reminders of the setting. The piece ends with a poignant contrast: doors that open easily for the visitors, but not for their new coding partners.
key_points: The inmates have severely limited resources—only 6 hours per week at donated computers, no internet, no Google, no Stack Overflow—yet they learn to code effectively
The coding session organically breaks down barriers: by the final retrospective, inmates and outsiders are randomly mixed around the circle rather than segregated
Uncle Bob evaluates his partners as he would any programmer, finding genuine talent: 'Were this an interview, he'd have scored very high. Mark has served 20 of a life sentence.'
The inmates are eager, grateful, and technically engaged—one has been reading about functional programming and wants to reorganize code around services and immutable data
The piece uses environmental details (fishbowl bathrooms, 'YOU WILL BE WRITTEN' signs, roll calls) to maintain tension between normalcy and incarceration
money_quotes: I forget where I am. We are coding. Life is good.
There is no internet. No google. No open source downloads. No StackOverflow.
Were this an interview, he'd have scored very high. Mark has served 20 of a life sentence.
As he drives, I look at his hands on the keyboard. P A I N is tattooed across the knuckles of one hand.
Doors. Doors that open easily – for Micah and me.
He's a young man on top of his game. He wants to do things right, and he wants to do them right now.
At it's conclusion, all thirty-seven of us knows each other's names.
posts.key_insight: The universal language of code can transcend prison walls, revealing capable minds and eager learners in the most unlikely places.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to a critique of TDD teaching methods by first refuting the argument that students should be taught to design up-front to avoid refactoring pain, then agreeing with it—but only when applied to architecture rather than domain design. He argues that learning to refactor is essential homework that cannot be shortcut, and that extraction and test maintenance are trivial when done correctly. However, he draws a crucial distinction: while TDD drives problem domain design through red-green-refactor, system architecture must be decided up-front because it exists in a different dimension entirely—the 'Domain Discontinuity.'
key_points: You learn complex things by solving problems, not by having solutions given to you—refactoring must be learned through experience
Tests should define behaviors, not mirror code structure; you don't rewrite tests when extracting methods and classes
Up-front design doesn't eliminate refactoring, it just postpones it until it becomes expensive
Architecture and problem domain are discontinuous—they differ in kind, not just level, and TDD cannot help with architectural decisions
System architecture is determined by desired user experience, not by problem domain, and must be decided before TDD can begin
money_quotes: You don't learn complex things by having the solutions given to you. You learn complex things by solving the problems that those complexities present.
I do not create a test for every method or every class. I create tests that define behaviors, and then I create the methods and classes that implement those behaviors.
We don't want the tests coupled to the code; and so we restrict the tests to operate through a small set of public methods.
Up front design does not eliminate the refactor step; it just postpones it until it is very expensive.
Architecture and problem domain are discontinuous – they do not form a continuum – they differ in kind not just in level.
TDD can't even be begun until we know the shape of the system that is to be created.
Like Shrodinger's cat, it lives in a superposition of states. It is right after all; except that it is dead wrong.
posts.key_insight: TDD drives problem domain design through iterative refactoring, but system architecture exists in a separate dimension that must be decided up-front based on user experience—this Domain Discontinuity explains why the same advice can be simultaneously right and wrong.

posts.summary: Martin argues that software projects lack the equivalent of a construction foreman - someone responsible for ensuring quality and correctness. He proposes that this foreman should be the sole person with commit rights, reviewing all pull requests and rejecting those with insufficient tests, dirty code, or poor quality. He acknowledges programmers would resist such oversight but contends this is standard practice in other industries. The absence of this quality gatekeeper, he argues, explains why software projects are delivered late, over budget, and with poor quality.
key_points: Software projects lack a dedicated quality enforcer equivalent to a construction foreman
The software foreman should have sole commit rights and reject pull requests that don't meet quality standards
Programmers resist oversight because it prevents them from claiming code is 'done' when it isn't
Other industries use this foreman model to deliver projects done, done right, and on time
The foreman needs both technical expertise to evaluate work and authority to reject substandard code and unreasonable demands
money_quotes: Where's the guy who makes sure all the tests are written. Where's the guy who makes sure that all the exceptions are caught.
He'd be the only one with commit rights. Everybody else would send him pull requests.
After all, how can you get done on time if the code has to be right?
If there's a guy who's actually going to look at the code, then there's no way to make yourself look good by saying that the code is done when it's not.
If you want to get a project done, done right, and done on time, you need a foreman.
He also has to have the power to say 'No' to the unreasonable demands of the customers and managers.
Is it any wonder that our floors squeak?
posts.key_insight: Software projects fail to deliver quality because they lack a dedicated technical authority with the power to enforce standards and reject substandard work.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob defends his controversial position that a technical foreman should hold ultimate commit authority on software teams. He argues that coaches, despite claiming egalitarian team ideals, already occupy a special non-peer role with unique responsibilities. Through a parable about 'Ron'—a developer whose personal crisis leads to degraded commits—he illustrates how unchecked trust in 'perfect teams' allows problems to fester until they cause serious damage. The post concludes that while good teams can operate with shared commit access day-to-day, someone must retain the authority to revoke that privilege when necessary, just as captains, coaches, and directors hold ultimate authority in their domains.
key_points: Coaches who advocate for egalitarian teams contradict themselves—coaching inherently involves authority and responsibility that peers don't share
'Perfect teams' are temporary states; human messiness inevitably requires corrective action
Silent, continuous code review by a responsible party catches problems early before they metastasize
The foreman doesn't need to exercise commit control in normal circumstances—the power exists for exceptional situations
New team members should earn commit privileges through demonstrated good work, not receive them automatically
money_quotes: Is there any way to be less of a peer, than to be the agent of 'growth'?
Good people sometimes do bad things.
In their blind faith in its own perfection, they are all but blind to the suffering of their team member.
In a well functioning team the foreman allows everyone to commit, and then simply, and silently, reviews the work.
But the foreman is the only one with the right to commit.
The truth about teams is that teams only function well when there is a competent leader that holds the commit rights.
Would you sail on a ship, or fly in an airline, without a captain?
posts.key_insight: Trust without verification is negligence disguised as egalitarianism—effective teams need someone with both the responsibility to watch and the authority to act.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that most software teams lack the mutual trust and shared values of an ideal agile team, and therefore need a 'foreman' model similar to successful open-source projects. He contrasts the perfect agile team (where everyone is effectively a foreman through constant pairing and shared values) with the reality of dysfunctional teams where members don't agree on quality standards. Coaching alone cannot fix fundamental value disagreements. Instead, teams with trust issues should adopt the open-source committer model: form a core team of trusted individuals with authority to enforce quality standards, accept that this will precipitate conflict, but use that crisis to quickly separate those who will adopt shared values from those who won't.
key_points: The perfect agile team doesn't need a foreman because every member already functions as one through pairing and shared commitment to quality
Successful open-source projects like Linux and git thrive using a foreman/committer model where a trusted core reviews all contributions
Coaching only works when team values are already aligned; it cannot resolve fundamental disagreements about quality and discipline
You cannot convince people to change through argument—only through being a role model, which eventually forces a crisis
The goal of introducing foremen is to accelerate an inevitable conflict, protect the code during transition, and ultimately build a team where everyone shares values
money_quotes: The question is meaningless because the team is composed of nothing but foremen.
Most often you can't convince people to change their behavior. At least not by talking.
Telling people that they are doing things wrong, and that you know how they should do them right, is not a good way to convince someone.
A coach, faced with a disagreement of values, must have the power to draw a line in the sand and say: 'Only things on this side of the line may pass'.
The team I am referring to is already in a state of distrust, and is already on a slow and painful course to destruction.
I want the crisis to come quickly. I want the period of divisiveness to be short.
The goal is to obviate the foremen because everyone has become a foreman.
posts.key_insight: Teams without shared values are already dysfunctional—introducing authority to enforce quality standards doesn't create conflict, it accelerates an inevitable crisis toward resolution.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to recent blogs suggesting TDD practitioners should think more before coding. He agrees that forethought is valuable but clarifies two misconceptions: TDD doesn't prohibit thinking ahead, and early thoughts aren't superior to later ones. He argues that thoughts during and after coding are actually more reliable because they receive feedback from reality, while pure forethought lacks the negative feedback that prevents ideas from drifting into impracticality. The key is to think continuously—before, during, and after coding—without privileging any particular phase.
key_points: TDD does not exclude or discourage forethought—this is a common misconception among both critics and enthusiasts
Later thoughts are almost always better than earlier thoughts because they're informed by actual coding experience
Thinking without coding lacks negative feedback, making it easy for ideas to drift away from practical reality
Keep forethought episodes short and drive them to code before they become too detached from reality
There is no preferred time to think—think before, during, and after coding
money_quotes: Your early thoughts are not better than your latter thoughts! Indeed, quite to the contrary, your latter thoughts are almost always better.
If you want to draw UML diagrams because they help you think, then draw the diagrams!
This happens because thinking without coding involves inadequate negative feedback.
Having been to La La land a few times in my career, I've learned a healthy distrust of too much forethought.
Don't give those forethoughts special status or special trust. In fact, I think healthy skepticism is the best way to treat them.
After all, those forethoughts are the least reliable thoughts you'll have. The most reliable thoughts you'll have will come long after you are done with the project.
There is no preferred time to think!
posts.key_insight: Forethought is valuable but deserves healthy skepticism—your most reliable insights come during and after coding when reality provides feedback on your ideas.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Holub and Binstock fundamentally misunderstand Agile by claiming it's a culture separate from practices. He contends that culture and practice are inseparable—practices are how cultures express their values, as demonstrated through examples from martial arts, medicine, and accounting. The true corruption of Agile, he argues, is not ritualism but the systematic elimination of technical practices like TDD, pair programming, and continuous integration. This stripping away of practices has fractured the movement, with project managers flooding in while developers flee to the Software Craftsmanship movement. Bob concludes that Agile is a culture expressed through practices, and abandoning those practices corrupts the culture itself.
key_points: Culture and practice are inseparable—you cannot have a culture without practices, and practices define and express cultural values
The fear of ritualism in Agile has been misplaced; the real problem is the elimination of technical practices, not their over-emphasis
Historical examples like sterile procedure in medicine and double-entry bookkeeping show that valuable new practices face resistance before becoming cultural norms
The Agile movement has fractured: project managers embraced a practice-free 'Agile' while developers moved to Software Craftsmanship to preserve technical discipline
Certification programs and the rise of soft skills over hard skills have diluted Agile into 'feel-good platitudes' rather than technical excellence
money_quotes: You can't have a culture without practices; and the practices you follow identify your culture.
Cultures express their values through their practices. It is absurd to imply that practice is irrelevant to culture.
It has been a shift away from excellence towards mediocrity, away from hard realities, towards feel-good platitudes.
While project managers have flocked into the Agile movement, developers have fled out of it.
Agile is a culture expressed through a set of practices.
Past success does not imply the ineffectiveness of new practices; nor does past success imply that the enthusiasm for new practices is inappropriate.
Under no circumstances, will I become the doctor who gets in the way of hand-washing.
posts.key_insight: Separating Agile culture from its technical practices doesn't purify Agile—it corrupts it by removing the very mechanisms through which Agile values are expressed and transmitted.

posts.summary: Martin draws a vivid parallel between TV hoarders who fill their homes with junk until they become unlivable, and programmers who accumulate cruft until codebases become unmaintainable 'legacy code.' He argues that the hoarder mentality—believing new acquisition is more valuable than organization, and eventually giving up because the mess seems insurmountable—mirrors how developers prioritize new features over cleanup. The post challenges the notion that legacy code is inevitable, arguing instead that professionals should leave code better than they found it, with quality improving over time rather than degrading.
key_points: The hoarder mentality believes acquiring new things (features) is more valuable than organizing existing things (refactoring)
Once mess accumulates, people rationalize that cleanup is pointless because no effort could 'make a dent'
Legacy code is the programmer's equivalent of a hoarder's home—a mess left for others to clean up
True legacies improve with age; professionals make their work better over time, not worse
The question isn't whether legacy code exists, but whether you're creating value or just building a hoard for others to clean up
money_quotes: Do you wander through dark passageways of floor to ceiling cruft as you attempt to add some new feature or fix some old bug?
We have a name for the result. We call it Legacy Code. The very term fills us with disgust and despair.
The legacy that a Hoarder leaves is a legacy of junk, trash, filth, and the huge effort required to get it all cleaned up.
A true legacy gets better with age.
Can you imagine hiring an artist, a mechanic, an engineer, a doctor, a lawyer, or anyone else, whose work products get worse the longer they work on them?
A professional team of programmers make their code better each day. The quality of the code improves with time. That's how you know they are professionals.
Is your work leaving a legacy of value, or are you just building a monstrous hoard for someone else to clean up?
posts.key_insight: Professional programmers leave code better than they found it—if your codebase gets worse over time, you're not building a legacy, you're hoarding.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to David Heinemeier Hansson's provocative blog post declaring 'TDD is dead' by dismantling his inflammatory rhetoric and defending the practice of Test-Driven Development. He argues that the 'stridence' in TDD advocacy is justified because the software industry consistently fails to meet professional standards. The core value of TDD lies not in debugging reduction or documentation, but in enabling fearless refactoring through a fast, trustworthy test suite. He challenges DHH's preference for integration tests, arguing they likely cannot achieve the speed and coverage necessary to support continuous refactoring. Bob concludes by offering to convert if proven wrong, but expresses strong skepticism that GUI-based integration tests can match TDD's effectiveness.
key_points: The inflammatory rhetoric comparing TDD to 'abstinence-only sex ed' and 'fundamentalism' undermines the credibility of arguments against TDD
TDD's primary benefit is enabling fearless refactoring through a fast, trustworthy test suite that keeps code clean and teams moving quickly
The 'stridence' in TDD advocacy is justified because the software industry consistently underperforms professional standards
Integration tests through GUIs likely cannot achieve the trustworthiness (coverage) or speed needed for effective continuous refactoring
If you can meet the two predicates of trustworthiness and speed with any testing approach, the specific method doesn't matter
money_quotes: As an industry, we suck. If you aren't doing TDD, or something as effective as TDD, then you should feel bad.

If you have a test suite that you trust so much that you are willing to deploy the system based solely on those tests passing; and if that test suite can be executed in seconds, or minutes, then you can quickly and easily clean the code without fear.
I have yet to see any test-driven developers flying airplanes into buildings while repeatedly hollering: 'Kent Beck is great!'
And that is power. Because if you can clean the code, you can keep the development team from bogging down into the typical Big Ball of Mud.
Yes! If I can keep the team moving fast, then I will find a way to trust my test suite, and I will keep those tests running fast.
There's lots of code in a normal system that deals with exceptions, errors, and odd corner cases that cannot be reached through the normal user interface.
posts.key_insight: TDD's true value is not in reducing bugs or creating documentation, but in providing the confidence to continuously refactor and keep code clean, which is the only way to maintain development velocity over time.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob addresses criticisms about TDD 'religiosity' by clearly delineating where TDD is impractical: at physical system boundaries requiring hardware interaction, in 'fiddling layers' like CSS that require human judgment, and in test support code. He compares TDD to double-entry bookkeeping but acknowledges software's unique challenge of controlling physical devices. The solution isn't to abandon testing but to keep these boundary layers 'humble' by extracting all logic into testable modules. He argues this separation of concerns actually improves design rather than damaging it.
key_points: TDD doesn't work at physical boundaries where software interacts with the real world - testing a bell rings requires a microphone or human ears
The 'fiddling layer' just before physical boundaries (CSS, Swing setup, configuration) can't be meaningfully test-driven because you discover correctness through iteration, not specification
Test support code (fixtures, Object Mothers, test doubles) doesn't need its own tests - the actual tests validate them
The Humble Object pattern solves the boundary problem: extract all logic from untestable layers into testable modules
This separation of fiddling code from testable code is good design, not damage - these concerns change for different reasons and at different rates
money_quotes: The act of stating every bit of logic twice, once in a test, and once in the production code, is very similar to the accounting practice of entering every transaction twice.
At the moment the software controls something that physically interacts with the world, automated tests become so impractical that manual tests are the best option.
As the tests get more specific, the code gets more generic.
Your eyes, and your mind, are the actual test.
It's not fair to load those layers with logic just so you can avoid writing tests for that logic.
We keep these layers humble by moving all the logic associated with them out into other modules for which we can easily write tests.
Those two domains of code will change for very different reasons and at very different rates; so it is wise to separate them.
posts.key_insight: TDD's boundaries aren't failures of the methodology but opportunities to apply the Humble Object pattern, separating untestable physical interactions from testable logic through good design.

posts.summary: Martin defends Jim Weirich's approach to decoupling business logic from Rails frameworks against DHH's critique that such refactoring constitutes 'test-induced design damage.' He argues that what Weirich demonstrated was the classic principle of separation of concerns, as articulated by David Parnas in 1972. Martin contends that separating business rules from framework code, GUI concerns, and database schemas is simply good design that happens to also make tests faster. The faster tests are a symptom of good design, not a distortion caused by testing obsession.
key_points: Jim Weirich's refactoring of Rails apps demonstrates separation of concerns, not test-induced design damage
Things that change together should stay together; things that change for different reasons should be separated
Business rules change at different rates and for different reasons than framework code, GUI, or database schemas
Slow tests are an indication of poor separation of concerns, not a necessary evil
DHH's critique points to architectural boundaries as 'needless indirection' but these boundaries are fundamental to good design
money_quotes: What I saw was a tightly interwoven mass of rails and business logic being teased apart by a master of his craft.
How do you separate concerns? You separate behaviors that change at different times for different reasons. Things that change together you keep together. Things that change apart you keep apart.
Putting those business rules into Rails controllers or Rails models therefore violates Parnas' principle.
If your tests run slowly, it is an indication that you have not separated concerns, and that therefore your design is lacking.
It seems to me that using good design principles that make your tests run faster is a noble goal.
Jim would have done this separation no matter how fast the tests were running.
posts.key_insight: Slow tests are a symptom of poor design, not a necessary cost of thorough testing—good separation of concerns naturally yields both better architecture and faster tests.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob defends his association of TDD with professionalism while acknowledging respected programmers who disagree, including Jim Coplien, Rich Hickey, and DHH. He draws a parallel to Ignaz Semmelweis, who faced decades of resistance when advocating hand-washing in medicine. Martin argues that TDD is not currently a prerequisite for professionalism but believes it will become one as software failures increasingly endanger society. He acknowledges he could be wrong, but points to mounting casualties from software failures as evidence that something must change. The piece balances conviction with humility, refusing to call current TDD skeptics unprofessional while predicting TDD may eventually carry the force of law.
key_points: TDD is associated with professionalism but not currently a prerequisite for it—that may change in the future
The resistance to TDD mirrors the 60-year resistance doctors had to Semmelweis's hand-washing protocols
Respected programmers like Coplien, Hickey, and DHH who reject TDD are still professionals—being wrong about a practice doesn't make one unprofessional in that moment of history
Software failures are mounting (Healthcare.gov, Knight Capital, Toyota) and society's dependence on software makes this an existential concern
If TDD proves as significant as hand-washing was to medicine, it may eventually carry legal weight
money_quotes: I plead guilty to claiming that the association exists.
Their only fault was that they were human.
But patients are dying out there!
Something must change… or we are headed for a catastrophe of existential magnitude.
It would not surprise me if, one day, TDD had the force of law behind it.
I won't call you unprofessional, and I won't think you are unprofessional. Because today, at this moment in our history, TDD is not the prerequisite of professionalism that I believe it will become.
If that is the case, so be it.
posts.key_insight: Professional standards evolve with knowledge—what seems optional today may become mandatory tomorrow, and the measure of professionalism is doing the best you can with what you know at the time.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob traces the intellectual lineage of the Single Responsibility Principle from Parnas's 1972 paper on module decomposition through Dijkstra's separation of concerns to his own formulation in the late 1990s. He clarifies that SRP is fundamentally about people, not code mechanics—a module should have only one reason to change because it should serve only one business stakeholder. Using an Employee class example, he shows how different methods serve different C-level executives (CFO, COO, CTO), and mixing these concerns creates organizational chaos when changes in one area break functionality another group depends on. The principle is ultimately about protecting both the software and the relationships with the people it serves.
key_points: SRP's 'reason to change' refers to people and business functions, not technical concerns like bug fixes or refactoring
Modules should be designed so that change requests originate from a single tightly coupled group representing one business function
The Employee class example demonstrates how calculatePay (CFO), reportHours (COO), and save (CTO) serve different organizational stakeholders
Mixing concerns terrifies customers and managers—breaking unrelated functionality destroys trust like a mechanic who fixes your window but breaks your starter
SRP is a restatement of cohesion and coupling: gather things that change for the same reasons, separate things that change for different reasons
money_quotes: This principle is about people.

When you write a software module, you want to make sure that when changes are requested, those changes can only originate from a single person, or rather, a single tightly coupled group of people representing a single narrowly defined business function.
Nothing terrifies our customers and managers more that discovering that a program malfunctioned in a way that was, from their point of view, completely unrelated to the changes they requested.
Gather together the things that change for the same reasons. Separate those things that change for different reasons.
The reasons for change are people. It is people who request changes.
Certainly the code is not responsible for bug fixes or refactoring. Those things are the responsibility of the programmer, not of the program.
posts.key_insight: The Single Responsibility Principle is fundamentally about people and organizational structure—each module should serve exactly one business stakeholder so that changes from one group never accidentally break functionality that another group depends on.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob examines the trade-offs of using mock objects in testing, arguing that both extremes—no mocks and too many mocks—lead to problematic test suites. Without mocks, tests become slow, incomplete, and fragile due to external dependencies. With excessive mocking, tests become slow (due to reflection), complicated (mocks returning mocks), and tightly coupled to implementation details. He advocates for a 'Goldilocks' approach: mock only across architecturally significant boundaries like databases and web servers, and write your own simple mocks rather than relying on mocking frameworks.
key_points: Tests without mocks suffer from slowness, low coverage of error conditions, and fragility due to external dependencies
Excessive mocking leads to complicated setup code, tight coupling to implementation details, and over-abstraction through unnecessary interfaces
Mock only across architecturally significant boundaries (databases, web servers, external services) but not within those boundaries
Writing your own mocks is trivial, forces better design, avoids reflection overhead, and promotes reuse
Good architectures are inherently testable—the right mocking balance correlates with proper separation of concerns
money_quotes: So, without mocks, tests tend to be slow, incomplete, and fragile.
Mocking the interactions between all classes forces you to create mocks that return other mocks (that might return yet other mocks).
The mocking structure become tightly coupled to implementation details causing many tests to break when those details are modified.
Mock across architecturally significant boundaries, but not within those boundaries.
The implication is that good architectures are inherently testable.
Find a way to test – design a way to test – your code so that it doesn't require a mock.
Those are the important boundaries of your system and they need to be managed, not just for tests, but for everything.
posts.key_insight: Mock sparingly and only at architecturally significant boundaries—this discipline not only improves test quality but also reinforces good architectural design.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that while frameworks like Rails, Spring, and Hibernate are indispensable tools, developers make a critical mistake by coupling their code too tightly to them. He observes that frameworks demand significant commitment from developers—requiring inheritance from base classes and adherence to conventions—while offering no reciprocal commitment in return. Framework authors, driven partly by ego and genuine desire to help, actively encourage tight binding through examples and talks. The solution is to keep frameworks at arm's length behind architectural boundaries, using them for peripheral subsystems while protecting core business logic from their influence.
key_points: Frameworks solve their authors' problems, which may overlap with but are not identical to your problems
The relationship between framework and user is asymmetric: you commit to the framework, but it makes no commitment to you
Framework authors are motivated partly by ego and self-worth tied to adoption, leading them to encourage ever-tighter coupling
As projects mature, frameworks increasingly get in the way rather than helping
Core business logic should be protected from framework dependencies through architectural boundaries
money_quotes: By accepting the framework into your code, you surrender your control over the details that the framework manages.
Despite the huge commitment you've made to the framework, the framework has made no reciprocal commitment to you at all.
Those problems may be similar to yours, but they are not yours.
A lot of my self-worth got tied up into the acceptance of my frameworks.
I surrender none of my autonomy to them.
The high level policies of my systems shall never be touched by frameworks.
Before you know it you find yourself engaged in all manner of unnatural acts, inheriting from its base classes, relinquishing more and more of the flow of control.
posts.key_insight: Protect your core business logic from framework dependencies by treating frameworks as peripheral tools behind architectural boundaries, not as the foundation of your system.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob revisits Bertrand Meyer's 1988 Open Closed Principle, arguing that while the original definition feels dated, its core insight remains vital: systems should be extensible without modification. He points to plugin architectures (Eclipse, Vim, Minecraft) as proof that OCP is not only achievable but represents the pinnacle of software design. The key is using object-oriented design to separate high-level policy from low-level detail, with dependencies pointing inward toward the core system. He concludes by dismissing critics who call OCP impractical, asserting that plugin architectures will be the most important aspect of future software systems.
key_points: The OCP states you should extend system behavior without modifying existing code—new features mean new code only, no changes to old code
Plugin systems (Eclipse, Vim, Minecraft) are 'the ultimate consummation' of OCP, proving the principle works at scale
Achieving OCP requires careful dependency management: plugins depend on the system, never the reverse
JARs, DLLs, and gems should ideally represent isolatable features, not tangled code bundles
A plugin architecture frees your core business rules from being bound to any particular framework, database, or interface
money_quotes: You should be able to extend the behavior of a system without having to modify that system.
Plugin systems are the ultimate consummation, the apotheosis, of the Open-Closed Principle.
The way you get a plugin architecture is to make sure that all dependencies inside the plugin, point at the system; and that nothing in the system points out towards the plugins.
When your fundamental business rules are the core of a plugin architecture, then you are never bound to a particular feature set, interface, database, framework, or anything else.
A strong plugin architecture is likely to be the most important aspect of future software systems.
We've known this was bad long before Martin Fowler wrote the book that labeled it Shotgun Surgery; but we still do it.
posts.key_insight: Plugin architectures prove that the Open Closed Principle is not theoretical idealism but a practical, powerful approach that liberates core business rules from framework and infrastructure dependencies.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explains the taxonomy of test doubles through a Socratic dialogue, distinguishing between dummies, stubs, spies, mocks, and fakes. He clarifies that 'mock' is often used colloquially to mean any test double, but formally refers to a specific type that verifies behavior. The conversation reveals a hierarchy where mocks are spies, spies are stubs, and stubs are dummies—but fakes stand apart with real business logic. Uncle Bob concludes by sharing his personal preference for hand-written stubs and spies over mocking frameworks, finding them simpler despite the tooling available.
key_points: Test doubles are the formal term for all test objects, like 'stunt doubles' in movies—mock became colloquial slang for the whole family
Dummies return null and should never be used; stubs return canned values; spies record what happened; mocks verify expected behavior; fakes have real business logic
There's a hierarchy: Mock → Spy → Stub → Dummy, but Fakes are fundamentally different and can become so complex they need their own unit tests
Spying on implementation details creates coupling and leads to fragile tests that break for wrong reasons
Uncle Bob prefers writing his own stubs and spies over using mocking frameworks, finding the syntax strange and setups overly complicated
money_quotes: They are called 'Test Doubles'. You mean like 'Stunt Doubles' in the movies? Exactly.
You pass it into something when you don't care how it's used.
The more you spy, the tighter you couple your tests to the implementation of your system. And that leads to fragile tests.
A test that breaks for reasons that shouldn't break a test.
Mocks know what they are testing.
The mock is not so interested in the return values of functions. It's more interested in what function were called, with what arguments, when, and how often.
We can say that a Mock is a kind of spy, a spy is a kind of stub, and a stub is a kind of dummy. But a fake isn't a kind of any of them.
I don't often write fakes. Indeed, I haven't written one for over thirty years.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the precise taxonomy of test doubles—dummy, stub, spy, mock, and fake—helps developers choose the right tool for each testing situation while avoiding the coupling and fragility that comes from over-mocking.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that a comprehensive test suite is essential for professional software development, using DHH's statement from the 'Is TDD Dead?' debate as a launching point. He outlines three key benefits of tests: they serve as unambiguous, executable documentation; they force beneficial decoupling in system design; and most importantly, they provide the confidence to change and clean code without fear. Martin contends that this confidence is what enables teams to maintain velocity, as clean code prevents the slowdowns that plague most developers. He concludes that because tests are critically important, they should be written first.
key_points: Tests serve as documentation that is unambiguous, formal, executable, and always in sync with the application
Well-designed tests force beneficial decoupling, though indiscriminate decoupling should be avoided
The primary value of tests is confidence - eliminating fear of change enables developers to keep code clean
Code flexibility comes more from a trusted, fast test suite than from design alone
When something is critically important, you do it first - hence test-first development
money_quotes: They are written in a language you understand. They are utterly unambiguous. They are so formal that they execute. And they cannot get out of sync with the application.
A well designed test suite with a high degree of coverage eliminates, or at least strongly mitigates the fear of change.
And when you aren't afraid to change your code, you will clean it. And if you clean it, it won't rot. And if it doesn't rot, then the software team can go fast.
Nothing makes code easier to change than a quickly executing suite of tests that you trust – nothing.
How irresponsible is it to have lost that control and to be afraid to make changes – afraid to clean?
When something is critically important, you do it first.
posts.key_insight: The greatest value of tests isn't verification but confidence—the freedom from fear that enables continuous cleaning and sustained development velocity.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on the 'Is TDD Dead?' hangout series, noting that while the participants agreed TDD's usefulness varies by individual, they neglected to discuss TDD as a team discipline. He argues that teams divided on TDD practices will inevitably fracture through attrition, as members seek teams sharing their values. This evolutionary pressure will sort programmers and companies into TDD and non-TDD camps, with natural selection determining the winner. Drawing parallels to past controversies over structured programming, objects, and Agile—all of which the 'better' faction won—he predicts TDD will similarly prevail.
key_points: The 'Is TDD Dead?' hangout focused on TDD as an individual practice while largely ignoring its role in team dynamics
Teams cannot function effectively when members hold fundamentally different values about testing practices
Divided teams will eventually sort themselves through attrition as members gravitate toward like-minded colleagues
Historical precedents (structured programming, OOP, Agile) show that controversial practices eventually win or lose through factional separation
TDD is a team discipline that requires shared values, not just an individual preference
money_quotes: If TDD were a dead topic, the brouhaha that we've all watched would not have happened.
Teams that don't enjoy a shared set of values are unstable.
If each member of the team does what is right in their own eyes, without considering the values of the team; then they don't actually comprise a team.
TDD is a team discipline, not simply an individual discipline.
Natural Selection and Survival of the Fittest, will determine which companies, and which values, thrive.
Structured Programming was a huge controversy in the '70s... Nowadays, however, we look askance at any use of goto. The factions separated, and the structured programming faction won out.
In 2014 I'm betting on the TDD faction.
posts.key_insight: TDD isn't merely a personal choice—it's a team value that will sort programmers and companies into factions, with evolutionary pressure determining which approach ultimately prevails.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob analyzes Stack Overflow data showing older programmers have vastly higher reputation scores, not because they're outdated, but because they possess more knowledge and can solve problems more calmly and efficiently. He argues the reason most programmers are under 30 isn't that older programmers fade away, but that the industry has doubled roughly every five years for decades, meaning there were simply far fewer programmers 40 years ago. This exponential growth creates a permanent immaturity problem: juniors perpetually outnumber seniors by huge margins, leaving most teams unguided and forced to relearn lessons repeatedly. His solution is for experienced programmers to actively mentor, pair with juniors, and lead from the trenches rather than isolating themselves or moving to management.
key_points: The correlation between age and Stack Overflow reputation proves older programmers have more relevant, current knowledge—not outdated experience
The industry's exponential growth (doubling every ~5 years) means half of all programmers have less than 5 years experience, creating a permanent shortage of mentors
The 'young programmers are superior' meme is foolish and responsible for many software failures, especially in startups
Senior developers should pair constantly, take fewer personal tasks, and act as sergeants in the trenches—not managers removed from the code
The ideal junior-to-senior ratio is 5:1, though demographics make even 16:1 difficult to achieve
money_quotes: Is the programming field the logical equivalent of Lord of the Flies? All kids. No adults?
Code doesn't have to pour out of all their orifices because they can solve problems calmly, quickly, and easily with much less code.
It means that the industry as a whole will remain dominated by novices, and exist in a state of perpetual immaturity.
I want the kids on my grass. I want to teach as many young developers as I can.
Think of them as Sergeants not Lieutenants; squad leaders, not company commanders.
That leader can temper and curb the youthful enthusiasm that leads to premature decisions about frameworks and architectures.
That leader can help to instill the value of refactoring and clean code, as a counterweight to the youthful thrill of gettingittowork.

There is a meme in the software industry that says it is better to hire young programmers because they are cheap, energetic, and produce more code than older programmers. But as the above analysis showed, that's a very foolish perspective.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's exponential growth creates a permanent crisis of inexperience that only aggressive mentorship by senior developers—paid and positioned as technical leaders in the trenches—can address.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob uses a Socratic dialogue to push back against the notion that Design Patterns are outdated relics of the 1990s. He argues that while frameworks like Rails, Angular, and Node may come and go as 'glitter,' the underlying Design Patterns they embody are timeless. The post emphasizes that patterns provide a shared vocabulary and canonical forms that allow professional developers to communicate efficiently and write recognizable code. He concludes by listing all 23 patterns from the Gang of Four book, making clear that modern developers still need to learn them.
key_points: The Design Patterns book remains one of the most important software books of the last 20+ years despite its age
Frameworks are 'glitter' that sparkles and fades, but the design patterns within them are enduring ideas
Design Patterns provide named, canonical forms that enable efficient communication between professional developers
The fundamentals of software have changed very little in the last four decades
Knowing patterns saves time because designers can communicate using shared names rather than explaining concepts from scratch
money_quotes: The Design Patterns book is one of the most important books, if not THE most important book, written about software within the last 20 years.
The fundamentals of software have changed very little in the last four decades.
The ideas aren't glitter. The frameworks are. Most of the ideas in those frameworks are old. They've been around for decades. The ideas are… Design Patterns.
A Design Pattern is a named canonical form for a combination of software structures and procedures that have proven to be useful over the years.
By following the canonical form, you relieve everyone else of the work of having to decode what you have done.
Social Networks: The font of all wisdom and knowledge.
posts.key_insight: Trendy frameworks come and go, but the Design Patterns underlying them are timeless fundamentals that every professional developer must learn.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that slow-running tests are unacceptable and represent a fundamental design flaw. He recounts advising a client whose C++ build times were slowing their test feedback loop, emphasizing that no testing tool's benefits justify slow execution. The core problem is that slow tests get run less frequently, leading developers to avoid refactoring and allowing code to rot. He advocates for decoupled architectures that enable fast test doubles, using FitNesse as an example where 72,000 lines of code can be tested in seconds during the red/green/refactor loop by stubbing slow dependencies.
key_points: Slow tests are run less frequently, causing developers to skip refactoring and allowing code quality to deteriorate
No testing tool's features justify slowness - if it slows you down, replace it immediately
Keeping tests fast is a design challenge requiring decoupled architectures and strategic stubbing
Well-designed systems allow slow operations (databases, web servers) to be stubbed behind architectural boundaries
You only need to test slow external systems like databases once to know they work - testing them repeatedly wastes time
money_quotes: If your testing tool is the reason it takes a long time to run your tests, then it's not the best tool for the job – let alone the best tool out there.
Anything that gets in the way of fast test times is forfeit, no matter what other wonderful benefits it may provide.
The primary benefit of TDD is the ability to refactor without fear, and without cost. The slower your tests run, the higher the refactoring cost.
Don't let the tests get slow!
Keeping the tests running very fast is a design challenge. It's one of the design constraints that well heeled craftsmen put upon themselves.

After all, how many times do you need to test the database to know that it works? How many times do you need to test the web server to know that it works? The answer to both questions is pretty close to one.
A slow running test suite is either a freshman error, or just plain carelessness.
posts.key_insight: Fast test execution is not a nice-to-have but a critical design constraint that professional developers must actively architect for, because slow tests inevitably lead to infrequent runs, skipped refactoring, and rotting code.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob recounts the architecture of a 1984 telephone voice response system (CDS) that he designed, describing its components: a RAM-based key-value database, field-labeled data format, and a finite state machine driven by a state transition table that invoked command-line processes. He then reframes these 30-year-old design choices using modern terminology—micro-services, message bus, BPEL engine, NoSQL, and JSON—to demonstrate that today's 'innovative' architectural patterns are essentially rediscoveries of older ideas. The post serves as a reminder that fundamental software architecture concepts remain constant even as terminology evolves.
key_points: The 1984 CDS system used a RAM-based key-value store (3DBB) fronted by a message-passing process—essentially a NoSQL database
Field Labeled Data (FLD) was a binary tree structure with JSON-like text encoding, predating modern data interchange formats
A finite state machine read from a configuration file and invoked command-line processes—an early form of BPM/BPEL orchestration
Multiple FSM processes ran simultaneously handling different phone lines, achieving concurrency through process isolation
Modern architectural buzzwords often describe patterns that have existed for decades under different names
money_quotes: For CDS I had a radical new idea.
Nowadays we'd call this JSON or XML. It had it's own text encoding scheme that, quite by accident, looked a lot like JSON.
The actions of the FSM were command lines.
We used a Micro-Service Architecture, communicating through a message buss, driven by Business Process Model (BPM) interpreted by a BPEL engine, backed by a NOSQL database holding values in JSON.
The more things change, the more they stay the same.
posts.key_insight: Modern software architecture patterns like micro-services, message buses, and NoSQL databases are often rediscoveries of fundamental design principles that experienced programmers have been applying for decades.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob examines the microservices trend and questions whether it offers advantages that aren't already available through well-designed jar files, DLLs, or shared libraries. He argues that the core benefit touted for microservices—independent deployability—has always been achievable through proper modular design using existing packaging mechanisms. The post acknowledges real costs of microservices: communication latency across sockets and loss of debugging capabilities like stack traces and single-stepping. While not dismissing microservices entirely, he warns against adopting them as a fad without first trying simpler approaches like plugin architectures with jar files.
key_points: Independent deployability, the main selling point of microservices, is already achievable through well-designed jar files, DLLs, gems, and shared libraries
Most developers have forgotten that jar files are dynamically linked at runtime and can be deployed independently, just like IDE plugins
Microservices introduce significant costs: socket communication latency (50ms connection setup plus transmission time) and loss of debugging capabilities
The industry tends to chase architectural fads that leave broken systems in their wake
A plugin architecture using jar files should be the first approach before considering service boundaries
money_quotes: Most people have forgotten this. Most people think that jar files are just convenient little folders that they can jam their classes into any way they see fit.
So if you design your jars well, you can make them just as independently deployable as a MS.
If you'd like proof that jars can be independently deployable, just look at the plugins you use for your editor or IDE.
Freedom's just another word for…
You can't single step across a REST call, but you can single step across jar files.
I don't want to see a big fad tearing through the industry leaving lots of broken systems in it's wake.

Don't leap into microservices just because it sounds cool. Segregate the system into jars using a plugin architecture first. If that's not sufficient, then consider introducing service boundaries at strategic points.
posts.key_insight: Before adopting microservices for independent deployability, master the art of well-designed jar files and plugin architectures—they've always offered the same benefits without the overhead.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that micro-services are a deployment option, not an architecture, and should be treated as such. He contends that a well-designed Clean Architecture keeps components ignorant of their deployment model, allowing architects to choose the appropriate deployment strategy based on actual needs rather than anticipated scaling. The post walks through a 'scalability scale' from micro-services down to simple JARs, noting that Clean Architecture works at any level. He pushes back against the notion that non-micro-service systems are 'monolithic,' calling this marketing spin, and recommends starting with the simplest deployment model and scaling up only as needed.
key_points: Micro-services are a deployment option, not an architecture - the deployment model is a detail that should be deferred
Clean Architecture components should have 'forced ignorance' about how they're deployed and how they communicate with other components
There's a scalability scale from micro-services to threads to JARs, and good architecture works at any level
Calling non-micro-service systems 'monolithic' is marketing baloney - well-designed JAR-based systems can be just as decoupled
Start with the simplest deployment (JARs/DLLs) and walk up the scale as actual needs arise, rather than anticipating massive scaling
money_quotes: The code within the components of a Clean Architecture has no idea whether it is in a micro-service running on an independent server communicating with other independent servers over the internet, or in a simple Jar or DLL communicating with other components using polymorphic function calls.
That means that there is no such thing as a micro-service architecture. Micro-services are a deployment option, not an architecture.
The job of good system architects is to create a structure whereby the components of the system have no idea how they are deployed and how they communicate with the other components in the system.
Breaking that forced ignorance is a good way to over-engineer a system.
Too often I have seen systems that have adopted huge three-tiered architectures in anticipation of scaling, only to discover that they never need to be deployed on more than one server.
A well designed system following the Clean Architecture is about as far from a monolith as you can get.
Don't leap to the top of the scale in anticipation of massive scaling. Keep that option open by conforming to the Clean Architecture.
posts.key_insight: Micro-services are a deployment detail, not an architecture - good architects keep deployment options open by ensuring components remain ignorant of how they communicate and are deployed.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob humorously laments how the software industry has hijacked perfectly good technical terms and changed their meanings. He specifically targets 'responsive' which now means mobile-friendly rather than fast, and 'design' which now refers to graphic design rather than software architecture. Through his curmudgeonly 'get off my lawn' persona, he argues that these linguistic shifts cause real confusion in professional communication and have displaced important technical concepts from their rightful terminology.
key_points: The term 'responsive' has been hijacked from meaning 'fast system performance' to meaning 'mobile-compatible'
This terminology shift causes real confusion in professional meetings when discussing system performance
'Design' has similarly been appropriated from software architecture to mean graphic/UX design
These changes diminish the professional identity of software designers and architects
Words should respond to users as stimuli, not to screen formats as IO devices
money_quotes: Consarned kids, can't keep the definitions of words straight.
In what way does adapting a GUI to the size of a screen make the system responsive? What the hell is the system responding to? The screen? Crimeny!
Couldn't he have called it 'adaptive', or maybe 'mobile friendly'. Why in tarnation did he pick a word like 'responsive'; a word that already had a perfectly good meaning?
Everyone turned and looked at me like I was a neanderthal.
I was a designer dammit. I designed software systems. Now I tell people that I design software systems and they think I'm a UX guy!
You respond to a stimulus. A screen is not a stimulus. A screen is an IO device.
Consarned kids! Get Off My Lawn.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's careless appropriation of established technical terminology creates confusion and erodes the professional identity of software practitioners.

posts.summary: Martin reflects on the declining participation of women in software, prompted by a Planet Money episode showing women's representation in computing peaked around 1984 and has declined since. He connects this trend to his own stumbles—three incidents where his humor at women's expense drew criticism. Through examining why jokes comparing Java to estrogen, belittling Grace Hopper's 'little hat,' and likening framework users to concubines felt funny to him and male audiences, he recognizes a pattern: using women's suffering or status as the butt of jokes is demeaning. He questions whether this normalized laughter in male-dominated rooms is the force driving women away from software careers.
key_points: Women represented about a quarter of programmers in the early 1970s but their participation has declined dramatically since the mid-1980s
Martin recounts three personal incidents where his attempts at humor were criticized for being at women's expense, each time apologizing but initially not understanding the deeper problem
The critical insight came from feedback that 'the suffering of women should not be used as the butt of one of your jokes'—treating people as things to be used for a goal is demeaning
He connects casual, normalized sexist humor to more extreme harassment like that faced by Anita Sarkeesian during GamerGate
The laughter that feels harmless in male-dominated rooms may be the force driving women away from software careers
money_quotes: When someone like you says something like that, it doesn't help women like me.
The suffering of women should not be used as the butt of one of your jokes.
In general, the act of using a class of people as the butt of a joke, is demeaning. You treat those people as things to be used in pursuit of a goal.
Why did I think my 'estrogen' joke was funny? Others did too, because there was laughter in the male dominated room.
Perhaps you got the joke too. Perhaps you felt a smirk trying to drag up a corner of your face. If so, you're probably male.
Ask yourself if your laugh is at someone else's expense. Ask yourself if the laugh is truly harmless.
Could the women be fleeing from the laughter in our male dominated room?
posts.key_insight: The casual laughter at women's expense that feels harmless in male-dominated tech spaces may be the very force driving women out of software careers.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to Erik Meijer's provocative conference talk 'One Hacker Way' with biting sarcasm, treating it as an elaborate hoax rather than serious advice. Meijer's talk advocated for skipping testing in favor of deploying to production and rolling back failures, quitting companies that practice TDD, organizing software teams like military hierarchies, and treating developers like professional athletes who should code intensely from ages 22-32 then retire. Uncle Bob systematically dismantles each argument by sarcastically agreeing with the absurd logical leaps—comparing the Catholic Church's longevity to the OSI stack, and equating programmers to world-cup soccer players. He concludes that the only explanation for such concatenated illogic is that Meijer brilliantly hoaxed the audience, who uncritically accepted the nonsense.
key_points: Meijer claimed TDD is waste and developers should deploy directly to production, rolling back when things fail
