The talk advocated military-style hierarchical command structures for software teams, rejecting Agile
Meijer suggested programmers should work intensely only from ages 22-32, like professional athletes, then retire
Uncle Bob frames the entire talk as an elaborate hoax because he cannot accept anyone seriously believes these positions
The audience's uncritical acceptance of the arguments is presented as evidence of weak critical thinking
money_quotes: All this TDD crap? Forget about it. If your company does TDD what do you do? Leave! You quit! You hand in your resignation today!
Writing tests is waste. TDD is for pussies.
The church has been around for 2,000 years. No company has been around for 2,000 years. Why can the church be around so long? Because it's a layered architecture!
He's brilliant because he's pulled off the perfect hoax.
Listen as their weak minds uncritically seize upon his endless train of ridiculous confusion and nonsense.
Dr. Meijer filled forty-five minutes with absolute drivel, and made everybody love it. And that takes brilliance.
posts.key_insight: When someone advocates abandoning testing, embracing rigid hierarchies, and treating programming as a young person's game, the ideas are so absurd they must be satire—and the real indictment is of audiences who accept them uncritically.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that programmers have accumulated enormous, largely unrecognized power over modern society since virtually nothing functions without software. He warns that society will eventually wake up to this reality, possibly after a catastrophic failure, and that programmers must preemptively adopt a code of ethics rather than wait for government regulation. Drawing parallels to the military's ethical obligations despite holding weapons, he proposes that programmers should pledge to cause no harm, write quality software, and behave with honor. The post concludes with an adapted oath from the Order of the Engineer, calling programmers to accept their obligations as reluctant rulers of the world.
key_points: Society has become completely dependent on software without fully realizing it, giving programmers unprecedented power over civilization
High-profile failures like Healthcare.gov, Knight Capital, and Toyota are warning signs that society keeps ignoring
Programmers must proactively establish their own code of ethics before government regulation is imposed upon them
The military provides a model: those with great power must adopt sworn duties to use it only in service to society
A programmer's oath should include commitments to cause no harm, write quality software, and maintain professional honor
money_quotes: We programmers. We rule the world. We write the rules that make our society work.
Software is a set of rules.
At some point, probably very soon now, our society will realize, to their horror, just how much responsibility, and just how much power, they have placed in the hands of programmers.
With great power comes great responsibility.
To protect our society from the power they have unwittingly ceded to us, we should adopt a code of ethics that describes our sworn promise and duty to humbly use our power to serve.
We go home every night, look in the mirror, and are proud of what we accomplished that day. We never feel like we have to run home and take a shower.
We are the rulers of the world. It's not a position we wanted. It's not a position we anticipated. But here we are.
posts.key_insight: Programmers have unwittingly become the rulers of modern civilization and must adopt a professional code of ethics to responsibly wield this power before society forces regulation upon them.

posts.summary: Martin argues that TDD practitioners often make the mistake of writing tests for core functionality first, which he calls 'grabbing for the gold.' The gold is always surrounded by an invisible 'hedge of thorns'—exceptional, degenerate, and ancillary behaviors that will entangle programmers who skip them. He advocates writing tests in a specific order: exceptional behaviors (invalid inputs), degenerate behaviors (inputs that produce 'nothing'), and ancillary behaviors (supporting functions) before tackling core functionality. This approach clears the thorns away systematically, making the core implementation much easier when you finally reach it.
key_points: Core functionality ('the gold') is always protected by invisible thorns—exceptional, degenerate, and ancillary cases
Write tests for exceptional behaviors first: invalid inputs that should never reach core functionality
Handle degenerate behaviors next: inputs like empty strings, zero-length arrays, and blank values that cause 'nothing' to happen
Ancillary behaviors often prove surprisingly useful to core functionality in non-obvious ways
Only approach core functionality after surrounding the problem with passing tests for everything else
money_quotes: The problem is that The Gold is protected by an invisible hedge of thorns that will entangle any unwitting programmer who, dazzled by The Gold, incautiously grabs for it.
I've learned that if you grab for The Gold too early, that invisible hedge will thwart your progress and tear your efforts to ribbons.
So the strategy I've learned to follow is to keep my eyes averted from 'The Gold', while probing for the hedge and clearing it away.
Before I approach the core functionality, I write as many tests as I can that ignore the core functionality and deal instead with exceptional, degenerate, and ancillary behaviors; in that order.
I often find that if all the ancillary behaviors are in place before I approach the core functionality, then the core functionality is much easier to implement.
I shy away from any tests that are close to the core functionality until I have completely surrounded the problem with passing tests.
Indeed just the day before yesterday I wasted four hours entangled in thorns that I had missed, and had not cleared away properly. In the end git reset --hard was my only escape.
posts.key_insight: In TDD, systematically test exceptional, degenerate, and ancillary behaviors before approaching core functionality—the thorns must be cleared before you can safely reach the gold.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Object Orientation and Functional Programming are not mutually exclusive paradigms but complementary disciplines that can be combined. He defines OO as fundamentally about disciplined polymorphism (managed function pointers), not about state, while FP is about disciplined assignment (immutability). Since these disciplines address orthogonal concerns, programmers can and should use both. He dismisses the tribal warfare between OO and FP advocates as ignorant, asserting that design principles like SRP and patterns like Strategy apply regardless of paradigm.
key_points: Objects are bags of functions, not bags of data - when objects are used as data structures, it's a design smell
OO's core contribution is disciplined polymorphism through managed function pointers, enabling dependency inversion
FP's core contribution is disciplined assignment through immutability, enabling safe concurrent programming
Polymorphism allows source code dependencies to be inverted from runtime dependencies, enabling plugin architectures
Design principles (SOLID) and patterns apply regardless of paradigm - FP doesn't magically make them irrelevant
money_quotes: Objects are bags of functions, not bags of data.
Is there really so much difference between f(o), o.f(), and (f o)? Are we really saying that the difference is just about the syntax of a function call?
The overriding difference between a functional language and a non-functional language is that functional languages don't have assignment statements.

For all the hi-falutin' rhetoric about OO and 'real-world objects' and 'programming closer to the way we think', what OO really comes down to is that OO languages replace function pointers with convenient polymorphism.
You can't have concurrent update problems if you never update anything.
Everybody thinks their way is the best. Everybody is wrong.
The fact that you've decided to use a language that doesn't have an assignment operator does not mean that you can ignore the Single Responsibility Principle.
posts.key_insight: OO and FP are orthogonal disciplines - OO manages polymorphism while FP manages mutation - and combining both yields robust, safe systems.

posts.summary: Martin describes TDD as a hierarchy of four nested cycles operating at different time scales. The nano-cycle (second-by-second) follows the Three Laws of TDD, writing one line of failing test then one line of production code. The micro-cycle (minute-by-minute) is Red-Green-Refactor, separating the goals of correct behavior and correct structure. The milli-cycle (every ten minutes) ensures code becomes more generic as tests become more specific, preventing 'getting stuck.' The primary cycle (hourly) checks architectural boundaries to ensure TDD drives toward clean architecture.
key_points: TDD operates at four time scales: nano-cycle (Three Laws), micro-cycle (Red-Green-Refactor), milli-cycle (Specific/Generic), and primary cycle (Boundaries)
Our limited minds cannot pursue correct behavior and correct structure simultaneously, so we separate these concerns in the RGR cycle
As tests get more specific, production code must get more generic—violating this leads to getting stuck and having to backtrack
Refactoring is continuous minute-by-minute activity, not a scheduled task at the end of a project
Architectural boundaries require stepping back hourly to see the big picture that nano- and micro-cycles obscure
money_quotes: You must write a failing test before you write any production code.
The philosophy is based on the idea that our limited minds are not capable of pursuing the two simultaneous goals of all software systems: 1. Correct behavior. 2. Correct structure.
Getting software to work is only half of the job.
As the tests get more specific, the code gets more generic.
Programmers make specific cases work by writing code that makes the general case work.
Refactoring is not something you do at the end of the project; it's something you do on a minute-by-minute basis.
There is no task on the project plan that says: Refactor.
posts.key_insight: TDD is not a single practice but a hierarchy of four nested feedback loops operating from seconds to hours, each serving a distinct purpose in producing both correct and well-structured software.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Java's 'interface' keyword was a lazy workaround to avoid implementing proper multiple inheritance, a problem solved decades ago in languages like C++, Ruby, and CLOS. Through a Socratic dialogue, he demonstrates that interfaces and abstract classes with only abstract methods are functionally identical, differing only by compiler-enforced keywords. The 'Deadly Diamond of Death' problem that supposedly justifies this limitation has known solutions. The real harm comes when developers need legitimate multiple inheritance, like implementing the Observer pattern cleanly, and are forced to either violate separation of concerns or duplicate code.
key_points: Interfaces and fully abstract classes are functionally identical - the distinction is purely a compiler enforcement mechanism
The 'Deadly Diamond of Death' multiple inheritance problem was solved decades ago in C++, Eiffel, Ruby, and CLOS
The interface keyword forces classes to know whether they're implementing an interface or extending a class, breaking encapsulation
Without multiple class inheritance, implementing patterns like Observer cleanly becomes impossible
Developers are forced to choose between violating separation of concerns or duplicating code - both bad options caused by the language limitation
money_quotes: The deadly diamond of death is a problem that was solved decades ago and it isn't deadly, and does not lead to death.
The keyword interface was invented as a way to prevent multiple inheritance of classes.
I can't explain it any other way than laziness.
Why should a class have to know that it is implementing an interface? Isn't that precisely the kind of thing you are supposed to hide?
Either you'll have to violate the separation of concerns, or you'll have to duplicate code. And it's the language forcing me into that situation.
That's the Observer pattern – done correctly. But it won't compile because you can't extend more than one class. Yes, and that's a tragedy.
posts.key_insight: Java's interface keyword was a shortcut that avoided solving multiple inheritance properly, and this design decision continues to force developers into code duplication or broken separation of concerns.

posts.summary: Martin recounts his formative 1967 experience watching someone program an ECP-18 computer, which set his life's course toward programming. Through correspondence with another early programmer, he discovers Judy Allen, a remarkable woman who wrote a two-pass assembler in binary machine language while managing a household. Martin reflects on how programming was once 'women's work' - from Ada Lovelace through Grace Hopper - until the late 1960s when society recognized that computers manipulated symbols, not just numbers, conferring real power. Once programming became associated with power, it became 'man's work' and women were driven out. He ends questioning whether the technician who inspired him might have been a woman like Judy Allen.
key_points: Programming was historically considered 'women's work' - drudgery beneath men who built hardware - from Ada Lovelace through the 1960s
Women like Annie Jump Cannon, Grace Hopper, and Judy Allen did groundbreaking work in computing when it was undervalued
The shift occurred in the late 1960s when popular culture (Star Trek, 2001) revealed computers as symbol manipulators with boundless power
Once programming was perceived as powerful rather than clerical, men invaded the field and drove women out
Martin's own certainty that his inspiration was male reflects the cultural assumption that power must come from men
money_quotes: This woman was Bad ASS! She wrote a M_____ F____ two-pass assembler, in binary machine language, in 1024 words, in a couple of months, using nothing but the front panel switches.
To some extent programming was considered to be Women's Work.
Computing was Women's Work. Indeed, they called the women: Computers.
These machines manipulated symbols. And if you can manipulate symbols, you can do anything! If you can manipulate symbols, you have power!
Power has a gender, and that gender is Male.
When programmers became powerful, programming became Man's Work.
You couldn't absorb that kind of power from a woman, could you?
posts.key_insight: Programming shifted from 'women's work' to 'men's work' precisely when society recognized that manipulating symbols meant wielding power.

posts.summary: Martin describes building a PDP-8 emulator for iPad as a training tool, contrasting two programming modes: MODE-A (the slow, deliberate process of programming on 1970s hardware with hour-long feedback loops) and MODE-B (modern rapid iteration with 10-second edit/test cycles). He argues that while MODE-B is vastly faster, many programmers sabotage themselves by accumulating technical debt, dependencies, and complexity until their development loops become as slow as MODE-A. The paradoxical solution: maintain MODE-B speed by applying MODE-A's careful, deliberate behavior to prevent the mess that slows you down.
key_points: Programming ancient machines like the PDP-8 strips away all magic and reveals computing's cold, hard reality—valuable for training new programmers
MODE-A programming (1970s style) required hour-long feedback loops through paper tape editing, multi-pass compilation, and manual debugging, forcing extreme carefulness
MODE-B programming enables 10-second edit/test cycles, but TDD rhythm applies even when not writing formal tests—knowing what you want to see IS your test
Programmers self-inflict MODE-A conditions by accumulating mess, frameworks, dependencies, and fragile builds that stretch loop times from seconds to minutes
The secret to staying in MODE-B is adopting MODE-A behavior: deliberate, careful work that prevents the technical debt accumulation that slows everything down
money_quotes: It seems to me that there is no better way to understand what a computer really is, than to touch a real computer and program it at the bit level, in machine language. Once you have done that, all the magic disappears and is replaced with cold, hard reality.
In MODE-A you take nothing for granted. You do everything deliberately and carefully. Because that's the only way to go fast.
Why would anybody do anything that increased their loop time? Why wouldn't everyone defend their loop time with their lives?
The secret for staying in MODE-B is to use MODE-A behavior.
Every mistake costs you an hour or so. So you spend a lot of time going over the details, making sure your code is right.
Even though I wasn't writing tests, it felt like I was—it felt like TDD.
Isn't avoiding MODE-A a guilt-edged priority? Shouldn't we all do everything we possibly can to keep our development cycle in MODE-B?
posts.key_insight: Modern developers destroy their own productivity by letting technical debt accumulate until fast feedback loops become impossibly slow—the cure is applying the deliberate carefulness that constrained environments once forced upon us.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to a question about whether readability, hackability, and abstraction can coexist in complex programs. He argues that while simple programs can tolerate disorganization, complexity demands structure. Using the analogy of a messy desk versus an organized library, he demonstrates that learning an organization scheme is an investment that pays off as systems grow. The uncomfortable truth is that organization schemes become obsolete as programs scale, but refusing to organize leads to code hoarding.
key_points: Simple programs can tolerate disorganization because dependencies are limited and a linear search through the codebase is feasible
As complexity increases, disorganization becomes suicidal - what works for a messy desk fails catastrophically for a large library
Learning an organization scheme requires investment, but proficient users can navigate complex systems in seconds
Organization structures that work at one scale become obsolete at the next - a thousand-line structure won't work for ten thousand lines
The alternative to investing in organization is becoming a code hoarder
money_quotes: As programs grow, parts start to depend on other parts. The connections become ever more tangled. And the program begins to turn into a system. And a system is always harder to understand, harder to read, and harder to change, than a program.
Relatively simple things can tolerate a certain level of disorganization. However, as complexity increases, disorganization becomes suicidal.
The organization structure that works for a thousand line program, does not work for a ten thousand line program.
You were asking whether the time required to learn the organization scheme of the system is worth the bother.
As the program grows you must invest time, effort, and practice into an organization scheme that is bound to become obsolete.
If you decide that organizing your system isn't worth the effort, you'll wind up as a Code Hoarder.
posts.key_insight: Organization is an investment that becomes increasingly essential as complexity grows, even though every organizational scheme will eventually be outgrown.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on the absurdity of running a 1969 Lunar Lander game through five layers of programming languages on his iPad, which prompts him to question why the software industry has created so many programming languages. He argues that the proliferation of languages stems from our collective dissatisfaction with all of them, as we perpetually search for a 'perfect' language that doesn't exist. The post playfully suggests, through modified song lyrics, that the industry should stop creating new languages and settle on one or two that work well enough. Despite the whimsical tone, the underlying message is that this endless search is futile and complicates our work unnecessarily.
key_points: The tower of abstractions in modern computing is remarkable - five languages stacked to run a simple 1969 game
The reason so many programming languages exist is that we don't truly like any of them
The search for a perfect programming language is as futile as searching for Neil Young's 'heart of gold'
The industry would benefit from settling on one or two languages rather than constantly creating new ones
Even when we find a 'perfect' language, we'd probably just use it to run old programs anyway
money_quotes: The reason there are so many computer languages is: We don't like any of them.
And what would we do with the perfect language if we found it? We'd write PDP-8 Emulators and run FOCAL so we could play Lunar Lander written by a High School student in 1969!
Get over it. Stop searching. There is no perfect language. We've searched everywhere. We've looked high and low. We've looked in and out.
Maybe we need to stop writing new languages and just settle down and pick one or two that work really well.
That would make life a lot simpler for us wouldn't it?
posts.key_insight: The endless proliferation of programming languages reflects our inability to be satisfied with our tools, but the search for perfection is futile - we should settle down with languages that work well enough rather than constantly chasing new ones.

posts.summary: Robert Martin recounts how IBM's 1401 FORTRAN compiler from 1960 used what he calls a 'microservice architecture' - 63 small programs (phases) of about 150 instructions each that ran sequentially, each transforming the source code held in memory. Rather than the obvious approach of loading a full compiler and making multiple passes that required punching and reading intermediate card decks, IBM's engineers kept the source code in memory and swapped in tiny executable phases from the card reader. Each phase progressively transformed the source into smaller intermediate representations until producing the final binary output. Martin marvels at the ingenuity required to decompose compilation into 62 distinct programs with such tight constraints.
key_points: The IBM 1401 had only 16,000 bytes of memory and an 88khz clock, yet ran a FORTRAN compiler
The naive multi-pass approach would require operators to physically shuttle punch cards between passes, taking potentially an hour for a 1000-line program
IBM's solution kept source code in memory and loaded 63 tiny 'phases' (~150 instructions each) sequentially from the card reader
Each phase transformed the source into smaller intermediate forms, freeing memory for symbol tables as compilation progressed
Martin argues this 1960s approach was essentially a microservice architecture, proving there's nothing new in software
money_quotes: The machine addressed it's memory in decimal.
If you had 8000 bytes, and had to write a FORTRAN compiler, how would you do it?
Almost all the memory in the computer was used to store the source code. They held back a few hundred bytes for the executable phases.
Each compiler phase averaged 150 instructions.
Imagine breaking down the problem of compilation into 62 different programs, each of which can run only once.
I have just one word for that. Incredible.
Which just goes to show that there's nothing new under the Sun.
posts.key_insight: Elegant architectural solutions to resource constraints are timeless - IBM engineers independently discovered microservice-like decomposition in 1960, decades before the pattern was named.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob tackles the common belief that Singletons are untestable by demonstrating simple techniques to mock them. He argues that tests trump encapsulation, meaning private variables should be made accessible when testing requires it. By making the instance variable package-scoped or public, Singletons become trivially mockable. He concludes that while Singletons are useful in public APIs to enforce single-instance constraints, within trusted team code you can simply create one instance without the pattern's ceremony.
key_points: Tests trump encapsulation - no test should be denied access to a variable just to maintain encapsulation
Singletons can be easily mocked by relaxing the private access modifier on the instance variable
The 'Singletons are hard to test' criticism stems from misunderstanding, not inherent pattern flaws
Public APIs justify Singleton usage for enforcing constraints; internal code can just create one instance
Trust your team - protective patterns are less necessary when you control all the consumers
money_quotes: Tests trump Encapsulation.
That means that tests win. No test can be denied access to a variable simply to maintain encapsulation.
What good is encapsulated code if you can't test it?
Do you have bad people on your team?
Well, if this were part of a public API, I'd agree with you. But if this is just code that's used by our team then… We trust our team?
Well, then, I simply create one.
posts.key_insight: The supposed untestability of Singletons is a self-imposed constraint - relax encapsulation for tests, trust your team, and the pattern becomes unproblematic.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to criticism of his previous post about the Singleton pattern, defending the use of global variables and Singletons when appropriate. He argues that global variables are not inherently sinful and that Singletons can be useful for managing globals and protecting public APIs. He addresses concerns about distributed environments, documentation value, and the role of trust in small teams. The post concludes with a defense of learning design patterns, comparing them to essential knowledge in other professions like electronics or sailing.
key_points: Global variables are not inherently bad - most systems legitimately need a few globals for data structure roots, factories, and resources
In small trusted teams, implicit rules and conventions provide efficiency gains that outweigh explicit documentation
Learning patterns is valuable, but hunting for places to use them is counterproductive - patterns should fit naturally
Pattern names provide automatic documentation for teams who share that vocabulary
The Singleton pattern's usefulness depends on context, including whether 'singular' means system-wide or process-wide
money_quotes: Despite Martin Fowler's oft referenced imitation of me, global variables are not a sin.
In small trusted teams implicit rules are a huge efficiency gain that I am loathe to abandon.
It's a good idea to learn patterns. It is not a good idea to hunt for places to use patterns.
If you know the patterns well, then you will find places in your systems where they fit naturally.
If I see a class named ReportVisitor, I immediately know what the author's intent was, and what the structure of the code is.
I'm not sure I'll ever understand what people have against learning things.
posts.key_insight: Design patterns are professional vocabulary that should be learned thoroughly so they can be applied naturally when appropriate, not dogmatically avoided or forcefully inserted.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that programmers are trapped in an endless cycle of creating new languages and frameworks, each promising to be the magical solution to software development's problems. He contends that nothing fundamentally new has emerged in programming since the late 1970s and early 1980s, with modern innovations being mere rehashes of old ideas. The root cause is that programmers believe in 'magic' - they don't understand what's happening at the machine level. His prescription is learning assembly language to demystify computing and writing simple versions of frameworks before adopting them, which empowers developers to make rational decisions about dependencies rather than being seduced by magical thinking.
key_points: Every framework is essentially a complaint that 'my language sucks' - an attempt to compensate for perceived language deficiencies

No genuinely new ideas have emerged in programming languages or frameworks since the late 70s/early 80s - everything since is rehashing Assembler, FORTRAN, C, Smalltalk, Lisp, Prolog, Erlang, and Forth
New generations of programmers are condemned to repeat history because they don't remember it, rewriting the same languages and frameworks with slightly different syntax
Learning assembly language destroys the 'magic' and reveals that all higher-level abstractions are just expressions of machine instructions
Before adopting any framework, you should be capable of writing a simple version yourself - this removes the framework's power over you and enables rational cost-benefit analysis
money_quotes: Every framework you've ever seen is really just an echo of this statement: My language sucks!
Those who do not remember the past are condemned to repeat it.
But there is no magic. There are just ones and zeros being manipulated at extraordinary speeds by an absurdly simple machine.
And once the magic is gone, you have a different perspective on things. You look at a language like C or Java or C# as just another expression of machine language.
You don't need someone else's framework.
Never buy magic! Before you commit to a framework, make sure you could write it.
Lines that you didn't write. Lines that you don't control. Lines that you probably shouldn't be putting a whole lot of trust in.
posts.key_insight: Understanding computing at the machine level destroys the illusion of magic in languages and frameworks, empowering programmers to make rational choices about what abstractions they truly need rather than chasing the next shiny thing.

posts.summary: This post uses a Socratic dialogue to explain the origins and meaning of Structured Programming. Martin traces the history from Bohm and Jacopini's 1966 mathematical proof that GOTO was unnecessary, through Dijkstra's famous 1968 letter 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful,' to the eventual adoption of structured control flow in modern languages. The key insight is that Structured Programming wasn't a new technology or invention—it was a discipline that identified something programmers shouldn't do. The battle was ultimately won not through persuasion but through language design that simply removed GOTO as an option.
key_points: Bohm and Jacopini mathematically proved in 1966 that GOTO was unnecessary, but few read their technical paper
Dijkstra's 1968 letter argued programs should use only three structures: Sequence, Selection, and Iteration
The controversy wasn't resolved by argument—it ended when languages stopped including GOTO
Structured Programming is not a technology but a discipline that restricts what programmers can do
Modern programmers practice Structured Programming without knowing it, simply because their languages enforce it
money_quotes: He didn't invent anything. What he did was to identify something we shouldn't do. That's not a technology. That's a discipline.
It made things better by taking away a damaging tool.
It ended when people stopped making and using languages that had GOTO statements, and started using languages that didn't.
That's what we were used to. We'd never even thought it could be different.
The more things change, the more they stay the same.
Structured Programming imposes discipline upon direct transfer of control.
posts.key_insight: Progress in programming often comes not from new tools or technologies, but from disciplines that identify and remove harmful practices.

posts.summary: Martin recalls his 1976 experience at Teradyne Central where programmers directly answered customer support calls via WATS lines, diagnosing and resolving issues in real-time. He contrasts this with modern development teams that are insulated from customers by layers of QA, DevOps, and customer service departments. Martin argues these departments exist because developers have failed so badly in these areas that companies created defensive structures. He concludes that true software craftsmen should never be insulated from customers, operations, or quality assurance—specialization is fine, but insulation is not.
key_points: At Teradyne, programmers personally answered customer calls and stayed on until problems were resolved, signing as 'Responsible Engineers' for their changes
Modern development teams are insulated from reality by QA, DevOps, and customer service departments that exist because developers failed in these areas
Software craftsmen cannot claim that title if they don't directly experience customer problems and operational nightmares they create
There is an important distinction between specialization (acceptable) and insulation (harmful)
Developers at Teradyne would ride along with telephone repairmen to understand how their system was really used
money_quotes: We felt responsible for the system. We wanted to know what our customers were experiencing. We didn't want a layer of people insulating us from the problems that we were creating in the field.
Modern software development teams are often highly insulated. They live in a world free from the distractions of customers and their 'petty' problems.

And why do these groups exist? They exist because each of these are areas where software developers have failed so badly at that companies have had to defend themselves by creating whole new departments and management structures.
How can you be a software craftsman if you don't communicate with your real customer? How can you be a software craftsman if you don't directly experience the nightmares you are creating for devops?
There's nothing wrong with specialization. There is a lot wrong with insulation.
One way or another we stayed on the phone with the customer until the problem was resolved.
posts.key_insight: True software craftsmanship requires direct accountability to customers and ownership of the real-world consequences of your code, not insulation behind organizational layers.

posts.summary: Martin responds to the Volkswagen emissions scandal, where software was designed to detect and defeat emissions tests. While the VW CEO blamed 'a couple of software engineers,' Martin argues the real issue isn't corporate scapegoating but that programmers actually wrote the fraudulent code. Regardless of orders from management, the programmers had an ethical responsibility to refuse. Martin calls for a true profession with the authority to hold members accountable for such violations.
key_points: Corporate executives can now use programmers as convenient scapegoats, setting a dangerous precedent in congressional record
The public is becoming aware that programmers can cause harm, which may lead to increased regulation
Regardless of whether they were ordered to do it, the programmers who wrote the code are ethically culpable
Programmers have a professional responsibility to know when they are being asked to commit fraud
The lack of a real software profession means there is no mechanism to hold unethical programmers accountable
money_quotes: It is now a matter of congressional record that software developers can be used as scapegoats.
Some programmers did, in fact, do this.
That they did it without authorization is neither likely, nor relevant. They did it.
It doesn't matter that their bosses told them to do it. They did it.
I think that argument is even more asinine than Michael Horn's. They knew. And if they didn't know, they should have known. They had a responsibility to know.
If we had a real profession, those programmers would be brought before that profession, investigated, and if found guilty, drummed out of the profession in disgrace.
posts.key_insight: Programmers cannot hide behind orders or ignorance—they bear personal ethical responsibility for the code they write, and the profession needs mechanisms to enforce that accountability.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to an article claiming 'Agile is the new Waterfall' with pointed disagreement. He argues the author fundamentally misunderstands Agile history and disrespects the significant contributions of its pioneers. While conceding one valid point—that teams should use minimal necessary process—he systematically defends each of the twelve Agile practices, explaining how different teams may need different levels of each. He concludes that the original author likely only experienced 'Flaccid Scrum' and wrongly generalized that to all of Agile.
key_points: The battle against waterfall is far from over—many organizations still advocate for it or try to mix waterfall with agile
Changing organizations is hard, and while some Agile consultancies are better than others, denigrating the entire effort is ignorant
Not every team needs every Agile practice, but most teams need at least some of them—choose wisely
Individual code ownership is deeply dysfunctional; no single person should be able to hold a team hostage
The author's exposure to 'Flaccid Scrum' led him to wrongly conclude that's all Agile offers
money_quotes: Anyone who thinks that the battle against waterfall is over simply hasn't been fighting in the right trenches.
Changing an organization is hard! Those companies that try to change, and hire help to make that change, are courageous.
As far as I'm concerned any team that has individual code ownership is deeply dysfunctional.
No single individual should be able to hold the team hostage.
Software projects are marathons, not sprints. You dare not run at a rate that you cannot sustain for the long term.
If we learned anything in the '90s it is that over-design is suicide.
Ignorance is no excuse. If you are going to impugn the character of good people and good ideas, you'd better do your damned homework.
posts.key_insight: Agile practices should be adopted selectively based on team needs, but dismissing the entire movement because of poor implementations reveals ignorance of its history and value.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to Christin Gorman's critique of 'future proofing' code, agreeing that no system can be completely protected from change, but defending strategic decoupling. He argues that while gratuitous abstraction is harmful, placing frequently-changing elements outside code and using interfaces to isolate volatile modules is sound practice. He pushes back on Gorman's dismissal of single-implementation interfaces, arguing they serve legitimate decoupling purposes. He also defends hand-written mocks over mocking frameworks, finding interfaces essential at architectural boundaries.
key_points: The Open-Closed Principle cannot apply to every element - some code must change when requirements change
Anything that changes frequently should be externalized from code or decoupled from the rest of the system
Single-implementation interfaces are legitimate when they isolate frequently-changing modules from the rest of the system
Hand-written mocks are preferable to mocking frameworks because they can be given meaningful names and don't pollute test setup
Strategic decoupling in moderation is valuable, but gratuitous decoupling should be avoided
money_quotes: Any module that changes frequently should be decoupled from the rest of the system.
Interfaces should not be actively avoided. Being restrained is not the same as being repelled.
The goal is not to 'Future Proof' your code. The future will always find a way to thwart you.
Decoupling shouldn't be gratuitous; but it's not something to actively avoid. Indeed, strategic decoupling in moderation is a very, very good thing.
Hand written mocks don't pollute your setups with random sequences of dots and parentheses.
I have worked on systems where the impact of certain changes was so prohibitive that they were delayed for years.
posts.key_insight: The goal isn't to prevent all future changes, but to strategically decouple the parts of your system that change frequently so that modifications remain cheap and localized.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob embarks on a humorous investigation into whether the first or second number in a multiplication expression is the multiplicand or multiplier. After discovering that authoritative sources completely disagree on the convention, he applies linguistic analysis to the phrase 'five times three.' He realizes 'times' is a preposition (not a verb), making the phrase order-independent. By reformulating it as a proper sentence ('Five is multiplied by three'), he concludes that the first number is indeed the multiplicand—vindicating his third-grade teacher Mrs. Moe.
key_points: Internet sources and authorities completely contradict each other on which operand is the multiplicand vs multiplier
The word 'times' is a preposition, not a verb, making 'five times three' a prepositional phrase rather than a sentence
By converting to a proper sentence ('Five is multiplied by three'), the grammatical roles become clear
The Yoda test for sentence parsing helps clarify subject-object relationships
Mathematical notation's ambiguity reflects deeper linguistic ambiguity in how we describe operations
money_quotes: The implications of this are no less than galactolyptic.
In the statement 5 X 3 we don't know which number is the multiplier and which is the multiplicand!
Great heavens above, The Internet did not agree!
Oh what a dry and colorless place this world would be without the joyful melody, the childlike impudence, of 'multiplier' and 'multiplicand'.
The word 'times' is not a verb. It's a preposition – a goddam preposition!
A pre position. A position before any other positions. What kind of nonsense is that?
When multiplying five by three, three shalt be the number of thy multiplying, and the number of they multiplying shalt be three.
posts.key_insight: Linguistic precision matters even for concepts we take for granted, and sometimes the only way to resolve ambiguity is to reformulate the question entirely.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob presents a formal oath for programmers, modeled after professional oaths like the Hippocratic Oath for doctors. The oath consists of nine promises covering code quality, testing, collaboration, honesty, and continuous improvement. It frames programming as a profession with ethical obligations rather than merely a job. The oath emphasizes that programmers have a responsibility to defend the honor of their craft through disciplined practice.
key_points: Programming should be treated as a profession with formal ethical commitments, not just a technical job
Code quality is a moral obligation - programmers must never knowingly allow defective code to accumulate
Every release requires proof that the code works (implicit advocacy for TDD and automated testing)
Professional programmers have obligations to their teammates - covering for each other and not impeding progress
Honest estimation without false certainty is an ethical requirement of the profession
money_quotes: I will not produce harmful code.
I will not knowingly allow code that is defective either in behavior or structure to accumulate.
I will produce, with each release, a quick, sure, and repeatable proof that every element of the code works as it should.
I will fearlessly and relentlessly improve my creations at every opportunity. I will never degrade them.
I will do all that I can to keep the productivity of myself, and others, as high as possible.
I will produce estimates that are honest both in magnitude and precision. I will not make promises without certainty.
I will never stop learning and improving my craft.
posts.key_insight: Programming is a profession that demands formal ethical commitments to quality, honesty, and continuous improvement - not merely technical competence.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that programmers must establish themselves as a formal profession with shared ethics before society forces regulation upon them. He traces the evolution of programmers in public consciousness from obscure lab workers in the 1950s to today's role models, villains, and scapegoats. The VW emissions scandal exemplifies how programmers can be blamed for ethical failures. Society's complete dependence on software—nothing works without it—means programmers effectively 'rule the world' by writing the rules machines follow. Yet programmers lack the binding ethics, disciplines, and standards that define a profession. The proposed Programmer's Oath is a first step toward self-regulation before external forces impose their own definitions.
key_points: Society's perception of programmers has evolved from irrelevant lab workers to recognized heroes, villains, and scapegoats
Modern civilization has complete, life-or-death dependence on software for every function
Programmers effectively rule the world by writing the actual rules that make machines work
Without shared ethics, disciplines, and standards, programmers lack a true profession
If programmers don't self-organize as a profession, society will impose regulation upon them
money_quotes: To preserve our freedom of action, our independence, our authority and self direction, there are certain promises we need to make to society at large.
Nothing happens in our society unless it is mediated by software.
Without software, quite frankly, and quite literally, we all die.
We rule the world. We write the rules that make the whole world work.
Other people think they write the rules; but then they hand those rules to us, and we actually write the rules that make the machines work.
We are viewed, by our employers, as laborers. We are tools for others to command and use. We have no profession.
If we do not form a profession on our own, then society will force it upon us – and define it for us.
posts.key_insight: Programmers wield enormous power over society but lack the professional framework of shared ethics and standards—and must establish one themselves before external regulation is imposed upon them.

posts.summary: Through a Socratic dialogue between a young developer and a mentor, Uncle Bob argues that the truly important architectural decisions are those that allow you to defer choices about databases, frameworks, and web servers—not the choices themselves. He demonstrates how dependency inversion and interface segregation principles enable business rules to remain independent of infrastructure concerns. The mentor shows that by having low-level components depend on interfaces owned by high-level business rules, teams gain flexibility to delay technology decisions until they have sufficient information. The post challenges the common belief that databases are 'the heart of the system' and reframes them as mere IO devices ancillary to core business logic.
key_points: The database is merely an IO device, not the heart of the system—business rules should not depend on specific database implementations
Dependency Inversion Principle: low-level policies should depend upon high-level policies, not the reverse
Interface Segregation Principle: each business rule should define interfaces for only the data access it specifically needs
The important architectural decisions are those that allow you to NOT make premature decisions about databases, frameworks, and web servers
Good architecture enables teams to defer technology choices until they have enough information to make them wisely
money_quotes: The Database isn't an important decision? — Too much probably. And, no; the database is not one of the most important decisions.
The database is merely an IO device.
I didn't say they don't use the tools of the database; I said they shouldn't depend upon them.
Senders own the interfaces that the receivers must implement.
The important decisions that a Software Architect makes are the ones that allow you to NOT make the decisions about the database, and the webserver, and the frameworks.
Woe is the architect who prematurely decides on a database, and then finds that flat files would have been sufficient.
Blessed is the team whose architects have so isolated them from slow and resource hungry IO devices and frameworks that they can create fast and lightweight test environments.
Blessed is the team whose architects care about what really matters, and defer those things that don't.
posts.key_insight: A software architect's most important decisions are those that preserve the team's ability to defer technology choices, not the technology choices themselves.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that stabilization phases—periods where teams let systems run before release to build confidence—are fundamentally a symptom of uncertainty and produce only false confidence. Since production data is entirely new, stabilization behavior has little bearing on production behavior. He contends that teams should attack their uncertainty directly through comprehensive testing strategies including unit tests, acceptance tests, integration tests, and thread-safety measures, progressively reducing stabilization phases until they can be eliminated entirely.
key_points: Stabilization phases exist because development teams have produced code they are not certain of, creating a CYA strategy rather than actual quality assurance
Time running a system is unrelated to quality—it only raises false confidence since production data may drive entirely new code pathways
The logical flaw is believing that execution time indicates quality, when production behavior will differ from stabilization behavior
Teams should proactively attack uncertainty through comprehensive automated testing: unit tests near 100% coverage, acceptance tests, integration tests, and thread-safety strategies
The goal should be progressively decreasing stabilization phases from months to weeks to days to hours, until they can be eliminated entirely
money_quotes: We run them because we are afraid. We are afraid because we are uncertain what the system will do.
There is a certain dissonance between calling ourselves professionals, and being so uncertain about what we have created that we fear what it might do.
The behavior of the system in the stabilization phase has little to no bearing on the behavior of the system in production; because the data coming into the system in production is entirely new data.
Time simply raises false confidence.
So stabilization phases are really just about creating false confidence. They are a CYA strategy.
It will be a long time before I trust software to drive car I am in. Because I – know.
The salesman said: 'Trust the car.' And I thought: 'The hell I will!' And I applied the brakes.
posts.key_insight: Stabilization phases are passive admissions of uncertainty that should be replaced with proactive testing strategies that attack the root causes of that uncertainty.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob recounts his shock upon learning from astronomer Dr. Pamela Gay's podcast that sexual harassment is a commonplace, everyday occurrence for women in science and tech fields. He conducted research and found extensive documentation of harassment in the software community, including at conferences he attended. He connects this behavior to the dramatic decline of women in programming since 1985, suggesting harassment may explain why women left the field in droves. The post concludes with an uncompromising professional standard: never touch anyone without explicit consent, always intervene when witnessing harassment, and terminate offenders immediately regardless of their perceived indispensability.
key_points: Sexual harassment in tech is not anomalous behavior by a few bad actors but a commonplace, regular occurrence that women have come to expect as normal
Women programmers started leaving the field dramatically around 1985, coinciding with the rise of 'brogrammer' culture and programmers gaining significant status and power
The demand for programmers and resulting perks have created a sense of impunity among some who believe their skills make them untouchable
Many harassment accounts have been voluntarily deleted by victims, suggesting the trauma is so significant they cannot bear to leave records intact
Professional programmers must never touch anyone without explicit consent, must intervene when witnessing harassment, and employers must terminate offenders regardless of their perceived value
money_quotes: I've always thought that sexual harassment was anomalous behavior perpetrated by a few disgusting, arrogant men in positions of power. It never occurred to me that sexual harassment was an everyday, commonplace, run-of-the-mill, what-else-is-new occurrence.
Who does something like that? What kind of low-life, scumbag, knuckle-dragging, troglodyte, behaves that way? Drunk or not? Didn't their fathers ever teach them about respect?
I can't imagine living like that – having to tolerate being manhandled – having to always be on guard against it.

No wonder women avoid the field! No wonder at all. I imagine that they find out, very early on, that to stay in this field is to have to submit to being regularly manhandled by too many of their co-workers and bosses.
Power has a gender, and that gender is male.
You never lay your hands on someone with sexual intent without their explicit permission. It does not matter how drunk you are.
No one is so indispensable that you should tolerate, or cover up, this kind of behavior.
If your employers do not act decisively in such matters, quit!
posts.key_insight: Sexual harassment in tech is not a rare aberration but a systemic, everyday reality that has driven women from the field, and true professionalism demands zero tolerance and active intervention.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to Ian Sommerville's blog post about abandoning TDD after a few months of experimentation. He argues that Sommerville gave up too quickly, experiencing classic novice symptoms like the Fragile Test Problem rather than fundamental flaws in TDD itself. Martin contends that these problems stem from insufficient test design and coupling issues, not from TDD as a practice. He emphasizes that TDD requires proper design thinking—tests need well-designed APIs just like production code. The post concludes by dismissing the complaint that TDD isn't a 'magic bullet' as irrelevant, since TDD never promised to solve problems it wasn't designed to address.
key_points: The Fragile Test Problem is a test design problem, not a TDD problem—tests that break constantly are over-coupled to production code
TDD does not eliminate the need for design; practitioners must still think about the big picture while working on details
Being a novice with any discipline temporarily crowds out other concerns like design thinking, which is why mentorship matters
Anything that is hard to test is badly designed—testability is a fundamental quality attribute
TDD never promised to protect against unanticipated problems; criticizing it for this is a straw man argument
money_quotes: We are programmers! We design! We create structures with high cohesion and low coupling. We manage dependencies. We isolate modules. WE. DESIGN.
Something that is hard to test is badly designed.
Any design that is hard to test is crap. Pure crap. Why? Because if it's hard to test, you aren't going to test it well enough.
Act locally. Think Globally.
If you have one part of your system that breaks whenever you change another part of your system, what can you conclude about the design of that system?
It takes time and experience with the discipline to get past these hurtles. More time than I believe Sommerville gave it. I think he just gave up too soon.
Deal with it. There will always be risk. Don't blame TDD, and don't give up.
posts.key_insight: The problems novices experience with TDD—fragile tests, lost big-picture thinking, hard-to-test designs—are symptoms of inadequate design skills and inexperience, not inherent flaws in the discipline itself.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob traces the history of type systems from Frege's mathematical logic through the evolution of programming languages, framing it as a decades-long 'Type Wars' between static and dynamic typing camps. He recounts his personal journey from assembly to C to C++, and describes how IBM's Smalltalk lost to Sun's Java despite Smalltalk programmers being more productive. However, those Smalltalk programmers introduced TDD to the Java world, which became the Trojan horse that enabled the resurgence of dynamic languages. He predicts that as TDD becomes standard professional practice, dynamic languages will ultimately prevail because comprehensive test coverage eliminates the need for static type checking.
key_points: The debate over types predates computers, originating with Frege's logic and Russell's paradox in the late 1800s
C won over Pascal in the language wars of the late 70s, but C++ later won programmers back to static typing after they experienced the pain of unchecked types at scale
Smalltalk programmers were 2-5X more productive than C++ programmers according to Capers-Jones research, yet Java defeated Smalltalk using the 'missile argument' about type safety
TDD was invented by Smalltalk programmers to solve the runtime type error problem, and they spread it to Java developers as a form of 'subversion'
With 100% unit test coverage, static type checking becomes redundant, which is why dynamic languages are gaining ground despite new statically-typed languages like Swift and Go emerging
money_quotes: You don't need static type checking if you have 100% unit test coverage.

When a Java programmer gets used to TDD, they start asking themselves a very important question: 'Why am I wasting time satisfying the type constraints of Java when my unit tests are already checking everything?'
We C programmers, combat hardened and war weary, swore we'd never go back to the careless days of unenforced types.
When Smalltalk died, a fifth column of Smalltalk programmers infiltrated the ranks of the Java programmers and began to teach TDD. Their goal: subversion.
Real programmers wrote assembler – or so I thought.
I was also glad that this was a C derivative, so that I didn't have to admit to the Pascal weenies that they'd been right all along!
The Smalltalkers will, eventually, win.
posts.key_insight: TDD renders static type checking redundant, and as test-driven development becomes the professional norm, dynamically typed languages will ultimately triumph over their statically typed counterparts.

posts.summary: Martin argues that the productivity gains from programming languages have hit diminishing returns. While the jump from binary to assembler provided a 10x improvement, subsequent language improvements (Fortran, C, C++, Java, Ruby) each offered progressively smaller gains—often just 5-7%. The key insight is that short edit/compile/test cycles have become the dominant factor in productivity, effectively erasing measurable differences between modern languages. As long as programmers maintain short-cycle discipline, the choice of language matters far less than commonly believed.
key_points: Assembler provided roughly 10x productivity over binary machine language—the largest single improvement in programming history
Each subsequent language generation (Fortran→C→C++→Java→Ruby) provided diminishing returns, typically 5-20% improvements
Short edit/compile/test cycle discipline has become the dominant productivity factor, dwarfing language differences
Modern hardware has eliminated compile-time advantages that dynamic languages once held over statically-typed languages
The quest for new programming languages is likened to searching for the Holy Grail—driven more by preference than measurable productivity gains
money_quotes: No future language will give us the factor of 10 advantage that assembler gave us over binary.
The short-cycle discipline has reduced the differences to virtual immeasurability.
So long as you work in short cycles, it hardly matters what modern language you use.
I'm saying that we've passed the point of diminishing returns.
When your cycles are extremely short, the clerical overhead in each cycle is very small. Your brain has less to keep track of.
No future language will give us 50%, or 20%, or even 10% reduction in workload over current languages.
It's a quest for the Holy Grail.
Clerical overhead grows out of proportion to the cycle time.
posts.key_insight: Programming language choice matters far less than maintaining a disciplined short edit/compile/test cycle—we've passed the point of diminishing returns where language improvements provide meaningful productivity gains.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explores mutation testing as a powerful technique for validating the quality of test suites beyond simple code coverage. He explains that while coverage tools show which lines are executed, mutation testing verifies that tests actually detect semantic changes to the code. Tools like pitest systematically modify code (inverting conditionals, removing function calls) and check if tests fail—surviving mutants indicate gaps in test coverage. Most provocatively, he argues that mutation testing could validate test-after approaches, potentially weakening the case for strict test-first TDD discipline.
key_points: Code coverage only proves execution, not testing—remove all asserts and coverage stays the same
Mutation testing creates 'mutants' by making semantic changes to code and verifying tests fail
100% line and branch coverage should be an asymptotic goal we always push toward
A semantically stable test suite that catches all code changes is the ultimate measure of test quality
Mutation testing could legitimize test-after approaches if teams verify semantic stability
money_quotes: Testing shows the presence, not the absence of bugs.
If experiments are good enough for science, why aren't unit tests good enough for software?
So what should your goal be? The question is absurd. There is no justifiable goal other than 100%.
Pull out all the asserts from your tests, and your coverage remains unchanged!
Of course your test suite should fail if you make a semantic change to your production code. Does anybody realistically doubt that?
A surviving (green) mutant might be the result of tests that have been @ignored, or commented out, or when asserts have been removed, or never added.
The cleaner the code the longer it's useful lifetime.
If I saw a team using mutation testing to guarantee the semantic stability of a test-after suite; I would smile, and nod, and consider them to be highly professional.
posts.key_insight: The true measure of a test suite isn't coverage metrics but semantic stability—whether any meaningful code change causes a test to fail.

posts.summary: Martin argues that the software industry is caught in wasteful 'churn' - constantly abandoning proven technologies for new ones despite diminishing returns. He contends that software progress follows a logarithmic curve and we've essentially hit the asymptote, making further technological switching counterproductive. The piece pushes back against those declaring OO 'dead' in favor of functional programming, arguing these are complementary techniques, not competitors. Martin concludes that professionals should stop chasing shiny new things and instead master a small set of languages and tools to become a true profession.
key_points: Software progress follows a logarithmic curve - early gains were dramatic, but we're now near the asymptote with diminishing returns
OO and functional programming are orthogonal techniques that work together, not mutually exclusive paradigms where one replaces the other
Switching languages constantly wastes the investment in powerful tooling like IntelliJ and Eclipse that took years to build
The constant pursuit of new languages, frameworks, and paradigms has become unprofessional
The industry needs to stabilize on a small set of languages and tools to mature into a real profession
money_quotes: OO isn't dead. OO was never alive. OO is a technique; and a good one.
Functional Programming is not 'better' than Object Oriented programming. Functional Programming is a technique, and a good one, that can be used alongside Object Oriented programming.
Every year, though we apply massive effort, we make less progress than the year before; because every year we get closer and closer to the asymptote.
New languages aren't better; they are just shiny.
The search for the golden fleece of a new language, or a new framework, or a new paradigm, or a new process has reached the point of being unprofessional.
We need to choose a language, or two, or three. A small set of simple frameworks. Build up our tools. Solidify our processes. And become a goddam profession.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's obsession with constantly adopting new technologies represents unprofessional waste rather than progress, as we've already reached the point of diminishing returns.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reconciles his previous 'Churn' essay with the advice to learn new languages yearly, arguing that learning languages helps developers recognize the limited underlying patterns in language design. He contends that while we've largely explored the language and framework domain, vast territories remain unexplored: concurrency, communication protocols, classic computer science texts, algorithms, and foundational languages. The essay is a call to stop chasing 'shiny objects' and instead pursue deeper knowledge in the fundamentals of computing.
key_points: Learning new languages reveals that most are recombinations of a small set of underlying patterns
The software industry has neglected deep technical domains like concurrency in favor of chasing new frameworks
Professionals should master fundamentals: concurrency primitives, communication protocols, graph algorithms, queueing theory
Reading classic texts (Knuth, Dijkstra, SICP) provides foundational knowledge most developers lack
Learning historically significant languages (Fortran, Lisp, Prolog, Forth) matters more than learning the latest trendy language
money_quotes: It has often been said (by me) that a professional software developer never stops learning.
You'll begin to see the patterns behind all these languages, and recognize that those patterns are relatively few in number.
The fact that we've explored one small aspect of our profession does not mean that there aren't other things to explore and learn.
Let's talk about concurrency. This is something we suck at!
Our field is a rich one. We've barely scratched the surface.
It would be a shame if we never truly explored those depths because we kept on being distracted by shiny objects.
Perhaps one of those new languages could be new – to you; but old and significant to our profession.
posts.key_insight: True professional growth comes not from chasing new languages and frameworks, but from mastering the deep fundamentals of computing that our industry has neglected.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explores whether Test-Driven Development can guide a programmer to discover Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm through a sequence of increasingly complex test cases. Starting with degenerate cases (empty graphs, single nodes), he incrementally builds up the solution, making design decisions along the way about input/output formats and API structure. The journey is 'jerky' rather than smooth—several tentative algorithms are abandoned as tests expose their weaknesses. Ultimately, he arrives at a working implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm, demonstrating that TDD can lead to complex algorithms even if the path isn't direct or elegant.
key_points: TDD can be used to incrementally discover complex algorithms like Dijkstra's, though the process may be 'jerky' rather than smooth
The 'generality rule' states that as tests get more specific, production code must get more generic—you can't add test-specific expressions to production code
Composed asserts—test helper methods that translate readable assertions into API calls—help keep tests decoupled from implementation details
Test coupling should be minimized; changes to production code shouldn't break dozens of tests
Each failing test exposes weaknesses in the current implementation that can be corrected in a relatively straightforward manner
money_quotes: As the tests get more specific, the code gets more generic.
We don't want the tests to become strongly coupled to the production code. The more the coupling the more fragile the tests become.
I think it's interesting how much thought and effort have been put into the structure of this problem; for just the first degenerate test case.
Did you like that change? && to ||. Yeah, pretty clever. It'll only work for two consecutive edges. The assumptions are mounting to the sky!
Those first few tests drove me through several tentative algorithms that did not neatly evolve into one another.
Is there a better sequence of tests that would lead more directly to Dijkstra's algorithm, without the tentative jerkiness? Perhaps; but if so, I haven't found them.
posts.key_insight: TDD can guide you to complex algorithmic solutions, but the journey may involve false starts and abandoned approaches rather than a smooth, direct path—and that's acceptable.

posts.summary: Martin responds to a study claiming TDD doesn't work by engaging in a Socratic dialogue that dissects the study's methodology. He reveals that the study compared TDD to Test-Last Development where participants worked in 'small chunks' - effectively forcing TLD practitioners to mentally do TDD anyway. The study's attempt to isolate the variable of test ordering inadvertently eliminated all meaningful differences between groups. Martin concludes that the real value of TDD lies in short cycles and discipline, not strictly in test-first ordering, and that the study actually demonstrates the importance of reading studies before drawing conclusions from them.
key_points: The study forced TLD participants to work in 'small chunks', which required them to mentally plan tests before writing code - essentially doing TDD in their heads
TDD's primary benefit is disciplinary: it enforces short cycles and high coverage over long periods, not just test-first ordering
When you eliminate the cycle-length difference between TDD and TLD, you eliminate the meaningful distinction between them
The study used a small three-hour problem, too short to reveal the long-term discipline benefits of test-first development
Headlines and blog summaries about studies often misrepresent what the research actually demonstrated
money_quotes: The TLDers were doing TDD in their heads, and then just reversing the order.
In their effort to reduce the number of variables they inadvertently eliminated them all.
The really effective part of TDD is the size of the cycle, not so much whether you write the test first.
The benefit of writing the tests first is disciplinary. Writing the test first keeps your cycles short; and keeps your coverage high, over long periods of time.
They found no difference, so I guess that's right.
I think it showed that you can't interpret the conclusions of a study without reading the study.
posts.key_insight: TDD's value lies not in the strict ordering of tests before code, but in the discipline of short cycles - and studies that control for cycle length inadvertently prove this by finding no difference.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob demonstrates how to test-drive the Diamond-Square terrain generation algorithm, addressing the challenge of testing algorithms that produce complex multi-dimensional outputs. Rather than testing raw output values which would be tedious and force writing the entire algorithm at once, he advocates testing the internal coordinate calculations using spy test doubles that record the sequence of operations. This approach allows incremental development while ensuring the algorithm's structure is correct, deferring simple calculations like averages until the coordinate logic is proven sound.
key_points: Testing algorithms with large output arrays (9+ values) by predicting results leads to data overload and forces writing the whole algorithm at once - a pattern called 'Getting Stuck'
Despite being a Classical School TDD adherent who normally tests outputs, algorithmic concerns sometimes warrant inspecting internal values - specifically the coordinates being manipulated
Spy test doubles that build action strings recording operation sequences provide better insight into algorithm correctness than checking raw output values
Once coordinate calculations are proven correct, average calculations become trivial to test separately with real values
The approach enables incremental test-driving of a complex algorithm by separating structural concerns (coordinates, repetition) from computational concerns (averages, randomness)
money_quotes: Trying to write a comprehensive set of tests, even for the 3X3 case, would be tedious at best; and very difficult for someone else to understand.
Test-driving from the raw results forces us to write the whole algorithm very early in the testing process. There's no way to proceed incrementally.
Those tests don't prove the algorithm is working the way it is supposed to. They just prove that the resulting values are right.

I'm a strong adherent of the Classical School of TDD, and I tend not to use my tests to inspect the internal workings of the software I am writing. The output values are usually good enough. In this case, however, the algorithm is the overriding concern.

Once I know that those coordinates are calculated properly, I don't really care what the values of the cells are, or whether the average is calculated properly. I mean, averages are pretty trivial to test.

This is a rather different slant on a spy test double. Rather than giving us booleans and flags to inspect about the calls of individual functions, this spy is going to load the actions string with the sequence of things that happened as the algorithm proceeded.
posts.key_insight: When test-driving algorithms with complex outputs, focus tests on verifying the structural correctness of coordinate and sequencing logic through action-recording spies, then separately test the trivial computational pieces with real values.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Swift and Kotlin represent a troubling trend of languages attempting to prevent bugs through increasingly rigid type systems and compiler constraints. He contends that features like mandatory exception handling, closed-by-default classes, and nullable types shift responsibility from programmers to languages—a misguided approach. The real problem, he asserts, is that programmers don't test their code adequately. These strict type systems punish developers who don't have perfect knowledge upfront, forcing them to either design everything in advance or override all the safety features, ultimately defeating the purpose.
key_points: Swift and Kotlin's deep static typing attempts to plug every possible type hole, creating increasingly complex and constraining languages
Language safety features presume programmers have perfect knowledge of the system before writing it
When safety constraints become too burdensome, programmers simply override them all, defeating their purpose
Defects are never the fault of languages—they are the fault of programmers who don't test
The real solution to preventing bugs is comprehensive testing, not language constraints
money_quotes: Defects are never the fault of our languages. Defects are the fault of programmers.
These languages are like the little Dutch boy sticking his fingers in the dike.
The question is: Whose job is it to manage that risk? Is it the language's job? Or is it the programmer's job?
To become an expert in these languages, you must become a language lawyer.
Why are these languages adopting all these features? Because programmers are not testing their code.
They punish you when you are wrong. They force you to go back and change massive amounts of code.
Why did the nuclear plant at Chernobyl catch fire, melt down, destroy a small city, and leave a large area uninhabitable? They overrode all the safeties.
And so you will declare all your classes and all your functions open. You will never use exceptions.
posts.key_insight: Language safety features are a misguided substitute for programmer discipline and testing—when constraints become too burdensome, developers simply override them, making the cure worse than the disease.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob clarifies his previous 'Dark Path' blog, explaining that his concern is not with static typing itself but with how far languages like Swift and Kotlin have pushed type constraints. He argues that types check internal consistency of program text, not behavior, making them fundamentally different from tests. While some type safety provides valuable low-hanging fruit, excessive type constraints lead to Open-Closed Principle violations and force Big Design Up Front. Languages like Java and C# hover near a reasonable balance point, while Swift and Kotlin have crossed into diminishing returns territory.
key_points: Types constrain program text and check internal consistency, while tests verify external behavior - these are orthogonal concerns
Every increase in type safety increases notation complexity and the risk of Open-Closed Principle violations
Excessive type constraints force programmers to get their type models right up front because changing them later becomes too expensive
Java and C# hover near the balance point of useful type safety, while Swift and Kotlin have gone too far
Type systems cannot replace testing - you still must test every bit of behavior regardless of how strong the type system is
money_quotes: No, types are not tests. Type systems are not tests. Type checking is not testing.
So what the type system is checking is not the external behavior of the program. It is checking only the internal consistency of the program text.
Behavior and self-consistency are orthogonal concepts.
Every step down that path forces users of the language to get their type models 'right' up front; because changing them later is too expensive.
Every step down that path forces us back into the regime of Big Design Up Front.
So, a little type safety, like a little salt, is a good thing. Too much, on the other hand, can have unfortunate consequences.
Type models do not specify behavior. The correctness of your type model has no bearing on the correctness of the behavior you have specified.
posts.key_insight: Types verify internal textual consistency while tests verify external behavior - conflating them leads to both over-constrained code and undertested systems.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob, known for advocating minimal comments in code, describes a situation where comments proved genuinely necessary. While implementing a caching feature that throttles long-running algorithms (which he calls 'choking'), he and his team found the concept too difficult to explain through code alone. They added explanatory comments describing the choking behavior and created an ASCII timing diagram to document their unit tests. This experience reinforced that while comments should be a last resort, some situations genuinely require them to preserve intent and rationale.
key_points: Good code typically doesn't need many comments, and each comment represents a failure to make code self-explanatory
Some concepts, like the 'choking' pattern for throttling long-running functions, are inherently difficult to express through code alone
Visual diagrams embedded in comments can be essential for understanding complex timing-dependent behavior
