Test names can be simplified to references (test1, test2) when accompanied by explanatory diagrams
The need for extensive comments is rare but real, and when it occurs, well-written comments are invaluable
money_quotes: I have often suggested that every comment represents a failure to make the code self explanatory.
I have advised programmers to consider comments as a last resort.
I recently came across that last resort.
We realized that nobody else would understand our tests unless they could see that timing diagram as well.
Had we left the comments out of these modules, we would have lost the intent and the rationale behind what we did.
This doesn't happen all the time. Indeed, I have found this kind of thing to be relatively rare. But it does happen.
When it does nothing can be more helpful than a well written, well thought through, comment.
posts.key_insight: Even the strongest advocate for self-documenting code must acknowledge that some concepts—particularly those involving timing, concurrency, or complex behavioral contracts—genuinely require thoughtful comments to preserve intent.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob addresses the criticism that TDD damages design and architecture, arguing that the problem lies not with TDD itself but with how programmers apply it. He explains that tests, like any code, must be designed with proper decoupling principles. The common mistake is creating a one-to-one correspondence between test classes and production classes, which creates tight coupling and fragile tests. The solution is to let tests and production code evolve in opposite directions—tests becoming more specific while production code becomes more generic—with an API emerging to bridge them. Architecture doesn't emerge from TDD, but lower-level designs do when programmers apply proper design principles during refactoring.
key_points: A one-to-one correspondence between test code and production code implies extremely tight coupling, leading to fragile and rigid tests
Tests are part of the system and must be designed with the same principles (OCP, DIP) as production code
As tests get more specific, production code should get more generic—this opposition is why TDD works
High-level architecture does not emerge from TDD and must be established beforehand; only lower-level designs emerge
The fragile test problem and test-induced rigidity are symptoms of poor test design, not inherent flaws in TDD
money_quotes: A one-to-one correspondence implies extremely tight coupling.
Tests need to be designed. Principles of design apply to tests just as much as they apply to regular code.
As the tests get more specific, the production code gets more generic.
It is not TDD that creates bad designs. It is not TDD that creates good designs. It's you.
TDD is a discipline, not a solution.
It is only programmers, not TDD, that can do harm to designs and architectures.
The idea that the high level design and architecture of a system emerge from TDD is, frankly, absurd.
posts.key_insight: TDD doesn't harm architecture—poor test design does; tests must be decoupled from production code structure, evolving toward specificity while production code evolves toward generality.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to DHH's post about testing practices, offering point-by-point agreement and disagreement with DHH's seven testing guidelines. He pushes back on DHH's recommendation against 100% coverage, arguing it should remain an asymptotic goal, and disagrees with DHH's code-to-test ratios, preferring 1:1. He agrees that testing frameworks is usually unnecessary, that integration tests should be reserved for integration issues, and that obviously trivial code may not need tests. The post ends with a correction noting DHH's TSA post actually predated his 'TDD is Dead' post.
key_points: 100% test coverage should be treated as an asymptotic goal - no other number makes a reasonable target
Code-to-test ratio should be approximately 1:1 (20K lines of code = 20K lines of tests)
Writing tests with TDD requires 'negative time' - it saves time rather than consuming it
Don't test your framework unless you have reason not to trust it
Keep tests focused and close to the code - avoid testing through UIs or web servers
money_quotes: Aim as high as you can. Treat 100% as an asymptotic goal. No other number makes a reasonable goal.
Testing should take none of your time. Writing tests, with TDD, requires negative time (i.e. it saves you a boatload).
Every worthwhile test you don't write costs you time.
There's generally no reason to test your framework; so long as you trust it.
Keep your tests focused. Don't test through UIs. Don't test through web servers. Test as close to the code as you can.
Be careful. Sometimes one line of code has 20 lines of semantics.
posts.key_insight: Writing tests with TDD doesn't cost time - it saves time, and every worthwhile test you skip will ultimately cost you more than writing it would have.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Test-Driven Development is the programmer's equivalent of double-entry bookkeeping, a discipline that ensures accuracy through redundancy and immediate feedback. He explores the parallels between the two disciplines: both operate at fine granularity, provide immediate error detection, and create two complementary pathways that must reconcile. However, he identifies a crucial asymmetry—unlike accounting where debits and credits are reversible, tests and production code have a one-way relationship. Through logical analysis, he demonstrates that the three laws of TDD cannot be inverted, concluding that test-first is the only valid discipline because tests serve as 'assembly instructions' for production code, a trap-door function that works in only one direction.
key_points: TDD and double-entry bookkeeping share key properties: fine granularity, immediate feedback, and dual pathways that must reconcile to zero errors
Unlike accounting's symmetric debits/credits, the relationship between tests and production code is fundamentally asymmetric—tests depend on production code but not vice versa
The three laws of TDD cannot be logically inverted because there's no natural constraint on granularity when production code comes first
Tests form independent assertions without system structure, while production code forms an interconnected system—this structural difference makes the conversion a one-way 'trap-door function'
Writing comprehensive fine-grained tests after production code is impractical because you must understand the entire codebase and it must already be decoupled
money_quotes: The stakes are enormous. Accuracy is essential. Millions wait to be lost or gained based upon our rare and esoteric skills.
This asymmetry leads to the inescapable conclusion that the equivalence to double entry bookkeeping only works if the tests are written first.
When tests are written first, granularity and decoupling are trivial to achieve. When tests follow production code, decoupling and granularity are much more difficult to achieve.
The tests, and their ordering, form the assembly instructions for the production code.
Tests can trivially drive production code; but production code cannot practicably drive the equivalent test suite.
There is a well defined discipline of test-first that is equivalent to double-entry bookkeeping; but there is no such discipline for test-after.
If accountants can do it, can't we?
posts.key_insight: Test-first is not merely a preference but a logical necessity—like assembly instructions, tests can trivially generate production code, but the reverse is a trap-door function that cannot be practically executed.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explores whether training neural networks constitutes programming in the traditional sense, concluding it does not. He establishes that traditional programming is the enumeration of finite state machine transitions, demonstrated through the equivalence of BDD's Given/When/Then and TDD's Arrange/Act/Assert patterns. He then argues that neural networks operate on statistical inferences rather than explicit state transitions, making their training a 'black art' rather than rigorous programming. The post concludes with mathematical comparisons between human brains and computers, questioning whether current hardware can ever achieve true autonomous driving without human-brain-level processing power.
key_points: All computer programming reduces to defining finite state machine transitions - states, events, and behaviors
TDD and BDD are simply methods for enumerating FSM transitions using Given/When/Then or Arrange/Act/Assert patterns
Neural network training is fundamentally different from programming - it's attempting to make the network infer states and transitions from statistical patterns
The human brain has roughly 10,000 times the processing power of a modern laptop, with 10 petabytes of RAM-equivalent memory
Current neural network technology may be insufficient for truly safe autonomous driving - we might need human-brain-level capabilities
money_quotes: What programmers do is to define the necessary states, events, and behaviors. Every computer program, no matter how simple or complex, is nothing more than a network of states, transitions, and behaviors.
Training neural nets is something of a black art. There may be a science behind it; but that science is not particularly rigorous yet.
It is your job, as the neural net in charge of that car, to extract and express order from chaos. No programmer could write the FSM to do this.
The human brain is not a computer in any sense that we would recognize the word.
So, no. We are not going to see Hal 9000 any time soon.
Do you believe you could train your cat to drive you from place to place? Safely?
posts.key_insight: Training neural networks is fundamentally different from programming because it attempts to infer statistical patterns rather than enumerate explicit state transitions, and our current hardware is orders of magnitude too weak to replicate human-level neural processing.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to a blog post where the author abandoned unit testing due to fragile, over-mocked tests. He argues the problem isn't unit testing itself but poorly written tests that are too tightly coupled to implementation. He provides clear definitions for various test types (unit, acceptance, integration, system, micro-test, functional) and emphasizes that all tests should be decoupled from production code using proper design techniques. The solution is treating tests as first-class citizens with the same quality standards as production code, not abandoning them entirely.
key_points: Over-mocking creates fragile tests coupled to implementation details, not a failure of unit testing itself
Tests should be decoupled from production code using OO design, dependency inversion, and design patterns like Facade and Strategy
Writing test doubles manually rather than using mocking frameworks encourages more thoughtful, less coupled test design
15-minute test runs are far too slow for the rapid feedback TDD requires
When programmers abandon unit tests, it's typically because they treated tests as 'junk code' rather than first-class citizens
money_quotes: Someone who treats their tests like first class citizens will take great pains to ensure that those tests are not strongly coupled to the production code.
Too many programmers think that the rules for unit tests are different – that they can be 'junk code' written using any ad hoc style that they find convenient.

Too many programmers have read the books on mocking, and have bought in to the notion that mocking tools are an intrinsic, and necessary, part of unit testing. Nothing could be further from the truth.
Writing the Test Double myself encourages me not to write tests with Test Doubles, unless it is really necessary.
They have allowed the tests to rot, to become coupled, to become rigid, and fragile, and slow. And then, in frustration, they give up on the tests.
Keep your tests clean. Treat them as first-class citizens of the system.
posts.key_insight: When unit tests become fragile and burdensome, the solution is not to abandon them but to apply the same design discipline and quality standards to test code that you apply to production code.

posts.summary: Martin argues that while the expected proliferation of processor cores that originally motivated interest in functional programming hasn't materialized as dramatically as predicted, FP remains essential for software development. He contends that FP's benefits extend far beyond concurrency—it makes code easier to write, read, test, and understand because programmers don't need to track mutable state. Martin recommends Clojure specifically for its simplicity, JVM interoperability, and compatibility with object-oriented features like dynamic polymorphism, directly refuting the notion that FP and OO are incompatible.
key_points: Moore's Law plateaued around 2000, leading to multi-core processors and renewed interest in FP for handling concurrency safely
The expected explosion of cores (to 1024+) hasn't happened—we've plateaued at 4 cores for years—but FP remains valuable
FP's real benefit is cognitive simplicity: immutable state means fewer balls to juggle, making code easier to write, read, and test
FP and OO are not mutually incompatible—Clojure provides full access to JVM's dynamic polymorphism while maintaining functional purity
Clojure's homoiconicity (code as data) and absurdly simple syntax make it an ideal language for learning FP
money_quotes: If you can't change the state of a variable, you can't have a race condition.
FP is easier to write, easier to read, easier to test, and easier to understand.
When you push an element onto a stack in an FP language, you get a new stack, you don't change the old one.
The programmer has to juggle fewer balls in the air at the same time. There's less to remember. Less to keep track of.
FP and OO are mutually incompatible! Who told you that? That's nonsense!
Just as pushing an integer onto a stack gives you a new stack, when you call a method that adjusts the value of an object, you get a new object instead of changing the old one.
The code is data that the program can manipulate.
Functional programming is important. You should learn it.
posts.key_insight: Functional programming's enduring value lies not in solving the anticipated multi-core crisis, but in the cognitive simplicity that comes from eliminating mutable state—making code fundamentally easier to reason about.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob uses IBM Watson's infamous 'Toronto' gaffe from Jeopardy to challenge the optimistic predictions about driverless cars. While Watson dramatically outperformed humans overall, it made a fundamentally inhuman error by naming Toronto as a US city—a mistake no reasonably educated person would make. He argues that driverless cars will similarly outperform humans statistically while inevitably making catastrophic errors that no human would make. The post culminates in a hypothetical courtroom scene where a computer must explain why it killed a child, with the chilling implication that its 'reason' may be as incomprehensible as Watson's Toronto answer.
key_points: AI systems can dramatically outperform humans on aggregate metrics while making errors that are fundamentally alien and incomprehensible to human reasoning
Computers always have discrete, unambiguous reasons for their decisions, but those reasons may be utterly divorced from human logic or common sense
The inevitability of driverless car tragedies will demand explanations that computers cannot provide in humanly satisfying terms
Statistical safety improvements don't address the problem of individually inexplicable catastrophic failures
No human would insist Toronto is a US city—such categorical errors reveal a fundamental gap in machine 'understanding'
money_quotes: I have one word for all these predictions: Toronto!
The mistakes that Watson made were not the kind of mistakes that a human would make.
Watson was, after all, a computer; and computers always have absolutely discrete and unambiguous reasons for what they do.
Whatever the reasons, the answer that Watson gave was supremely stupid.
No human of moderate intelligence and education would have made that mistake.
Indeed, any human who insisted on that answer, as Watson surely would have, might very well be deemed legally insane.
The car will have had a good reason to kill that child.
The reason was: Toronto.
posts.key_insight: AI systems that statistically outperform humans can still make catastrophically stupid errors that no human would make, and when those errors kill people, the computer's 'reasons' will be as incomprehensible as naming Toronto a US city.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that the human brain's computational complexity vastly exceeds anything we can currently build or simulate. He describes the brain as an analog computer of 100 billion neurons, each itself a sophisticated information processor driven by tens of thousands of digital processors (DNA machinery). The interconnections are physical, dedicated, and operate through both electrical and chemical signaling at molecular speeds. He concludes that comparing our internet infrastructure to a single brain is absurd, and artificial sentience remains far beyond our reach.
key_points: The brain is fundamentally an analog computer where information is encoded in firing rates, not discrete pulses
Each of the 100 billion neurons is itself a vastly powerful information processor, superior to a modern laptop
Neurons contain tens of thousands of digital processors (DNA/protein machinery) controlled by analog chemical signals
Cellular communication occurs through both local chemical signals and bloodstream-mediated long-distance signaling
Our entire internet infrastructure is computationally trivial compared to a single human brain
money_quotes: Your brain is a highly efficient analog computer composed of a deeply interconnected array of one hundred billion analog processors of immense computational power, each driven by tens of thousands of digital processors that are monitored and controlled through interactions both from within and without.
The raw information processing power of a single cell is vastly superior to a modern laptop.
Virtually every molecule, if not every atom, in your brain is an active component in the information processing going on between your ears.

The idea that our meager internet, which indirectly interconnects only a trifling few hundreds of millions of pitifully weak serial processors, could process the information of a single brain is absurd.
No, we will not be achieving artificial sentience any time soon.
Information is contained in the rate at which neurons fire. It is the pulse rate, not the pulses themselves, that contain and transmit information.
posts.key_insight: The human brain's computational architecture—analog, massively parallel, and operating at molecular scales—represents a complexity so far beyond our digital systems that artificial sentience remains a distant fantasy.

posts.summary: Martin uses a thinly-veiled allegory about 'escalator operators' and 'height discrimination' to comment on a workplace firing over expressed ideas. He argues that regardless of whether Edward's ideas were wrong or offensive, firing someone for expressing ideas creates a monoculture that kills creativity and vitality. Martin contends that bad ideas should be countered with better ideas, not punishment. He goes so far as to say that in such a situation, the people who fired Edward should themselves be immediately terminated to save the company.
key_points: Firing people for expressing disagreeable ideas—even wrong ones—creates a dangerous monoculture
When expressing ideas becomes intolerable, creativity, imagination, and risk-taking are suppressed
The proper response to bad ideas is better ideas, not punishment
Companies that punish idea expression are dying organizations
Leadership should protect the freedom to express ideas, even unpopular ones
money_quotes: Fire people who express ideas you disagree with.
A company who fires people for expressing ideas is dying.
If the people in power fire those who express bad ideas then no one other than the people in power will ever express an idea.
You never punish bad ideas.
Instead, you counter bad ideas with better ideas.
All creativity, all imagination, all risk taking, all the things that make a company, a community, and a society, vital is being suppressed.
Fire the people who fired Edward. I would Fire them with extreme prejudice.
posts.key_insight: Punishing people for expressing ideas—even wrong or offensive ones—destroys the intellectual vitality that organizations need to survive and innovate.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on the responses to a controversial blog post, categorizing his critics into four groups: outraged haters, condescending elitists, unprepared true believers, and rational disagreers. He expresses appreciation for those who engaged thoughtfully but notes they were vanishingly rare. The majority fell into camps that either hurled insults without reasoning or argued points irrelevant to the actual topic. He finds the prevalence of hatred and knee-jerk reactions discouraging, while acknowledging the positive responses he did receive.
key_points: Rational, engaged disagreement that adds to conversation is rare but valuable
The largest group of critics argued irrelevant points based on secondhand information
Outraged responses filled with profanity offer no reasoning or rationale
Condescending elitism in discourse is thankfully uncommon
Online discussions often generate more heat than light
money_quotes: I appreciate the last group. They engaged in a reasonable discussion. They added to the conversation. They made a difference.
Unfortunately the last group was vanishingly small. There were only half a dozen, or less, out of the hundreds of responses overall.
They had read something about what someone else had written about what someone else had said… They had formulated their conclusions. They knew the truth!
The number of Fs was impressive. It's a word they seem to know so well, and use so frequently that one wonders if they know many others.
They simply dumped their truckload of hate and moved on.
posts.key_insight: Genuine rational discourse is increasingly rare, with most online responses falling into camps of outrage, irrelevance, or condescension rather than thoughtful engagement.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on his early career when women made up a third of programmers and were treated as equals, noting the decline to roughly 10% over subsequent decades. He describes becoming aware of gender issues in tech only after his controversial 2009 RailsConf joke, acknowledging subsequent missteps and apologies. The post pivots to defending James Damore's firing as a mistake and criticizing what he perceives as character assassination attempts against himself on Twitter. He concludes by asserting he is not a misogynist while acknowledging his generational blind spots, questioning whether such tactics help women in tech.
key_points: In 1969, roughly a third of programmers were women and gender was not a discussed issue - it was simply normal
The proportion of women in programming declined significantly from the 1970s through 1980s, a change he only recognizes in hindsight
He remained unaware of women in tech issues until his controversial 2009 RailsConf keynote forced him to confront the topic
He believes Google's firing of James Damore was wrong and that companies dependent on innovation cannot stifle creative disagreement
He distinguishes between legitimate debate and what he characterizes as character assassination campaigns on social media
money_quotes: The code in those systems had no gender bias. You could not look at a source file and tell whether a man or a woman wrote it.
A company that depends upon innovation and creative thinking cannot survive if it stifles creative thought by firing people who disagree.
I am a 64 year old white male who grew up with Bewitched, Father Knows Best, and Petticoat Junction and this has certainly colored my sense of humor and outlook. I am working on that.
Nobody was talking about the problem of Women in Tech back then. The situation just was what it was. I took it to be completely normal.
Is character assassination really the strategy that is most likely to help women in tech? I don't think so.
It is difficult to defend yourself against a frothy mob.
posts.key_insight: Uncle Bob uses his personal history of working alongside women programmers in the 1960s-90s to establish credibility while defending himself against accusations of misogyny stemming from his defense of the Damore memo.

posts.summary: Martin uses the Volkswagen emissions scandal as a cautionary tale for software engineers about the limits of 'just following orders.' He describes the likely scene where engineers were asked to defeat emissions tests and probably found the technical challenge intellectually appealing. The post notes that engineer James Liang received 40 months in prison and $200,000 in fines despite arguing he was following orders out of loyalty to his employer. Martin concludes that engineers will be held to high ethical standards by courts regardless of employer pressure.
key_points: The VW emissions defeat device was likely seen as a 'cool problem' by the engineers who built it
The 'just following orders' defense failed - James Liang received near-maximum sentencing
Courts imposed 10x the requested fine specifically as a deterrent to other engineers
Your employer cannot protect you from legal consequences of unethical work
Engineers are held to high ethical standards whether their employers uphold those standards or not
money_quotes: What a cool problem to have to solve? No, really! Imagine how much fun it would be to figure out some sneaky way to bypass the emission test.
Their defense, of course, was that they were just following orders. They were just protecting their jobs – making sure they could feed their families. But that defense didn't fly.
Engineers take note: Your employer can't cover for you.
Doing your job does not mean that you just follow orders.
The courts are going to hold you to a high ethical standard, even if your employer does not.
posts.key_insight: Engineers bear personal legal and ethical responsibility for the code they write, and 'just following orders' provides no protection when that code causes harm.

posts.summary: Martin defends Douglas Crockford after his disinvitation from Nodevember, framing it as an example of what he calls 'SJF' (Skulking Juvenile Fascist) behavior in the tech community. He argues that anonymous social media campaigns use fear and innuendo to pressure conference organizers into publicly shaming speakers over minor perceived offenses. Martin characterizes these tactics as cowardly, anti-social, and unjust, contrasting them with direct confrontation and debate. He proposes 'illumination' as the cure - forwarding critical tweets directly to their targets so they can respond. The post is a combative defense of free expression and a critique of what Martin sees as cancel culture in tech.
key_points: Conference organizers who capitulate to social media pressure become 'meat' for further campaigns, not allies
The tactics Martin criticizes involve indirect pressure on employers and associates rather than direct confrontation with the person
Martin sees these campaigns as fundamentally opposed to dialogue and understanding - 'They understand already!'
Transparency and direct communication are proposed as countermeasures to anonymous criticism
Martin frames himself as unbowed by similar threats, noting no 'dearth in invitations'
money_quotes: They won't confront you if you offend them. Oh no. They have no need to talk, debate, or try to come to an understanding. They understand already!
By that action, the organizers of Nodevember became SJF meat. The SJFs ate them for lunch.
Two words that they have no right to use, because their methods are both anti-social and unjust.
If you happen to come across the slime trail of an SJF, shine a light on it.
It works because cowards abound.
I've actually had a few SJFs make the same threat about me. So far I haven't noticed any dearth in invitations.
posts.key_insight: Anonymous social media campaigns that pressure institutions rather than engaging individuals directly are fundamentally incompatible with honest discourse and justice.

posts.summary: Martin argues that the principle 'the ends justify the means' is fundamentally unscrupulous and dishonorable, using the goal of increasing women in programming as his central example. He contrasts honorable approaches (education, addressing prejudice, public relations) with what he considers dishonorable tactics (hiring quotas, social pressure campaigns, boycotts, and attacks). He criticizes recent incidents including Doug Crockford's disinvitation from a conference, a DDoS attack on his own site, and James Damore's firing as examples of this unscrupulous meme in action. Martin concludes that the honorable response to bad ideas is to present better ideas, not to suppress speech through mob tactics.
key_points: The concept that 'ends justify means' is by definition unscrupulous - having scruples means choosing honorable means to achieve ends
Honorable approaches to increasing diversity include education, addressing prejudice, and public relations - not quotas or social pressure
Organized boycotts and disinvitation campaigns are dishonorable because they are 'unilateral and asymmetric' - a few acting in secret as judge, jury, and executioner
The honorable response to speech you disagree with is to present better ideas, not suppress the original
Merit-based hiring is the only honorable hiring policy regardless of diversity goals
money_quotes: When a person has scruples it means that they choose honorable means to achieve their ends.
The only honorable hiring policy is one that is entirely merit based.
Such means are not honorable because they are unilateral and asymmetric. They are done in relative secrecy by a few, but they affect many.
The few set themselves up as the judge, jury, and executioner. The accused is tried in absentia, and in secret, without the ability to offer a defense.
The honorable way to deal with a bad idea is not to suppress that idea, but to present a better idea.
Anyone living in a free society should judge them to be abhorrent and reprehensible.
Have scruples. Be Honorable!
posts.key_insight: In a free society, the honorable response to ideas you find objectionable is to counter them with better ideas, not to suppress them through social pressure, boycotts, or attacks.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob addresses the 'Fragile Test Problem' where tests break during refactoring because they mirror production code structure. He argues that test structure should be contra-variant to production code structure, not covariant. As development proceeds, tests should become more specific while production code becomes more generic, moving in opposite directions along the generality axis. This decoupling of both structure and behavior allows true refactoring while maintaining test coverage.
key_points: Creating one test class per production class couples test structure to code structure, causing the Fragile Test Problem
Tests should have their own independent design, not mirror production code structure
As you extract private methods and discover new classes, the original test class can still cover all that code through the public API
Tests become more specific over time while production code becomes more generic - they move in opposite directions
Generalizing production code is itself an act of decoupling from the test specifications
money_quotes: If a small change in one module of a system causes large changes in many other modules of the system, the system has a design problem.
Their behavior is coupled; but their structure need not be coupled.
The act of generalizing is the act of decoupling. We decouple by generalizing!
The structure of the tests must not reflect the structure of the production code, because that much coupling makes the system fragile and obstructs refactoring.
We keep writing failing tests in order to drive the generality of the production code to a point where it becomes impossible to write another failing test.
Not everything you read and see is a rule.
No test suite can specify every required behavior. The production code must generalize the subset of behaviors specified by the tests to all the behaviors required of the system.
posts.key_insight: Tests and production code should evolve in opposite directions - tests becoming more specific while production code becomes more generic - decoupling both their structure and behavior to enable true refactoring.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to an Atlantic article about the 'software apocalypse' that blamed software failures on inadequate tools rather than programmer discipline. He criticizes the article for interviewing only tool inventors while ignoring thought leaders like Kent Beck and Martin Fowler who focus on craftsmanship. Using a scene from the TV show 'Wisdom of the Crowd' as an illustration, he argues that the real cause of software disasters is programmers taking shortcuts under pressure and others providing cover for sloppy work. He dismisses the idea that better REPLs, model-driven engineering, or other tools will solve the problem, emphasizing instead that disciplined testing practices and professional accountability are the true solutions.
key_points: Tools like Light Table, Model Driven Engineering, and TLA+ are useful but cannot solve the fundamental problem of undisciplined programming
The software apocalypse stems from two causes: programmers taking sloppy shortcuts under schedule pressure, and other programmers providing cover for that behavior
REPLs allow you to 'try' things but not 'test' them—tests are repeatable, reviewable documents that can be enhanced and added to suites
The Atlantic article failed to even consider that programmers doing a better job might prevent catastrophic bugs
In a room full of programmers, fewer than one in twenty regularly write unit tests
money_quotes: Tools are fine; but the solution to the software apocalypse is not more tools. The solution is better programming discipline.
The author completely avoided the notion that the software apocalypse might be avoided if programmers just did a better job.
The author seemed convinced that the solution to the software apocalypse – the solution to bad code – is more code.
I knew I shouldn't have reused that code, but we were in a rush.
You don't test things in the REPL; you try things in the REPL. A test is much more formal than a trial.
A test can be repeated. A test can be enhanced. You can add to a test. You can review a test. A test is a document. A test is a program.
The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, But in ourselves…
How many of you write unit tests on a regular basis? Not one in twenty raised their hands.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's pursuit of better tools distracts from the real solution to catastrophic bugs: raising the level of programmer discipline and refusing to excuse sloppy work done under schedule pressure.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob examines the persistent gender gap in programming, noting that despite high demand and growing salaries, women represent only about 5% of developers even though they earn 65% of college degrees. He traces potential 'shunts' in the pipeline from childhood play preferences through high school, college, and careers. After dismissing hiring discrimination and college bias as unlikely causes given market pressures, he argues that quota programs would be counterproductive—they wouldn't increase overall female employment (demand is already high) and would create environments where women are systematically outperformed by male colleagues who faced higher hiring standards.
key_points: The programmer shortage is acute (1 hire per 3 job postings) yet the industry effectively ignores half the population, with women representing only ~6% of developers
The 'pipeline' has potential shunts at every stage—from childhood play preferences favoring boys' engagement with computers, through the 'high school trap,' to career attrition after graduation
Market forces make active hiring discrimination unlikely: companies starved for talent can't afford to filter on anything but merit

Quota programs would not increase total female employment (demand already exceeds supply) but would redirect top female talent to quota-setting companies while lowering average female performance relative to male colleagues everywhere
The real solution lies in upstream interventions: encouraging girls' interest in programming through education and outreach programs starting in childhood
money_quotes: So five times as many men than women graduate with BSCS degrees. Then, nearly two out of three of those women do not pursue a programming career.
Businesses that are starved for programmers can't afford to filter based on gender. They can't afford to filter based on anything except merit, and they'll have to compete for that.
If a plurality women students expressed interest in computer science, the universities would bend over backwards to accommodate them.
There seems to me to be little downside in encouraging young girls and young women to consider programming as an entertaining activity, something fun to learn, and eventually a good career.
No more women get hired overall, because demand is too high to allow any candidate, male or female, to go unhired.
What quotas will do, however, is to redirect women into those companies that employ quotas, and redirect men into those companies that do not.
They must also face the ethical question of bypassing more qualified candidates in favor of gender – which is clearly discriminatory.
posts.key_insight: The programming gender gap stems from pipeline problems beginning in early childhood, not hiring discrimination, and quota programs would paradoxically worsen women's relative position by creating systematic performance disparities while doing nothing to increase overall female employment.

posts.summary: Martin argues that programming language evolution has been driven primarily by Moore's Law - the exponential growth in computing power that allowed us to move from assembly to interpreted languages. With clock speeds plateauing around 2010 and core density growth slowing, he suggests we may have entered a stable era where language proliferation will slow. The anticipated functional programming revolution, driven by fears of many-core processors, may never fully materialize since we're still waiting for 8+ core laptops. Martin welcomes this stability as a transition from the 'adolescence' of software development to an era of professional craftsmanship with a settled complement of languages and tools.
key_points: Moore's Law drove language evolution from assembly to interpreted languages by making computational resources effectively infinite
Clock speeds plateaued at ~2.8GHz over a decade ago, and core density growth has slowed to incremental rather than exponential
The anticipated many-core processor revolution that would have demanded functional programming hasn't materialized
The 'crisis' driving functional language adoption may not occur, meaning Java/C#, Ruby/Python, and Go may remain sufficient
The industry may be entering a stable 'adult' period with fewer new languages and more focus on craftsmanship
money_quotes: We, who used to worry about microseconds, now cavalierly push systems to production written in languages that execute in textual interpreters!
Most of us work in an environment of virtual infinities. There may be some constraints out there somewhere, but most of us know we will never bump into them.
That's the way it is with limits – often you don't see them coming until you hit them.
If Moore's law was the driver of our language evolution, what will drive it now?
I, for one, look forward to the end of the barnstorming era and the onset of the era of professional, and ethical craftsmanship.
An era where we discard the detritus of the excesses of our youth, and settle upon a small complement of languages, platforms, and frameworks.
posts.key_insight: The end of Moore's Law may herald a welcome stabilization in software development, shifting focus from chasing new languages to mastering professional craftsmanship with proven tools.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that SQL's fundamental design as a textual query language makes it inherently dangerous, as it can pass through user interfaces and provide unauthorized data access. Despite decades of awareness since the first SQL injection was documented in 1998, breaches continue because it's unreasonable to expect every programmer to correctly apply protection mechanisms every time. He contends that frameworks don't solve the problem—programmers do—and they've consistently failed. The only real solution is to eliminate SQL entirely and replace it with non-textual APIs using data structures and function calls.
key_points: SQL injection has been a known vulnerability since 1998, yet continues to cause massive breaches at companies like Equifax and Yahoo
Relying on frameworks or programmer discipline to prevent SQLi is fundamentally unreliable given the sheer number of query statements in typical applications
The subtle syntax differences between safe and unsafe SQL statements (question marks, hash marks, parentheses) make mistakes inevitable
Even with good architecture, there are too many 'absolutelys' required to guarantee safety
The only reliable solution is replacing SQL with non-textual APIs that use data structures and function calls
money_quotes: SQL is demon spawn, and no self-respecting software developer should ever use it.
The instant that article was published every single programmer in the world should have ceased to use SQL on the spot!
Frameworks don't handle the issue; programmers do! And programmers haven't been handling that issue all that well; have they?

SQL is the ultimate security breech. SQL is a portable, universal, textual language that can be transmitted through the user interface of a system and, if passed to the SQL engine, can provide absolute access and control to all the data in the system.
The very idea that SQL statements might come in through the user interface and be held in RAM ought to fill you with unmitigated terror!
You never know when some 22 year old programmer, working at 3AM under a horrific schedule pressure, will forget to use just the right ? and # in just the right positions.
If there is no SQL engine, then there can be no SQLi attacks.
posts.key_insight: The only way to truly eliminate SQL injection vulnerabilities is to eliminate SQL itself, replacing textual query languages with type-safe APIs.

posts.summary: Martin traces the evolution of data storage from punch cards through magnetic tape, spinning disks, and now SSDs, noting how each technological shift abstracted away physical limitations while introducing new access paradigms. He explains how punch cards imposed 80-byte records, tape removed that limit but kept serial access, disks enabled random access via cylinder-head-record addressing, file systems abstracted physical disk structure, and relational databases provided organized access to fixed-size records. With SSDs eliminating rotational latency and seek time, Martin poses an open question: what new abstraction or access paradigm will emerge now that access time is essentially uniform across all data?
key_points: Each storage technology transition abstracted away a physical limitation while enabling new ways of organizing and accessing data
Early programmers formatted disks with fixed formal arrangements specific to each application, manually managing cylinder-head-record addressing
File systems decoupled the structure of data from the physical structure of the disk, allowing named arrays of bytes
Relational databases provide random access to fixed-size records, abstracting both physical storage and indexing complexity
SSDs eliminate the time constraints of spinning media, raising the question of what new abstraction this enables
money_quotes: Memory was always the problem.
The structure of the disk had been entirely abstracted away and decoupled from the structure of the data.
Every time our technology has changed, from Cards, to Tape, to Disk, new access methods were invented. The older technology fell away, and newer modes of access were adopted.
With each new technology a fact that we considered essential was abstracted away.
The one big thing that has changed is time. Accessing SSD is fast. There is no rotational latency. There is no seek time.
SSD makes everything faster but changed the way we viewed _____. Fill in that blank.
posts.key_insight: Each revolution in storage technology abstracts away a physical constraint and spawns new data access paradigms, and SSDs—by eliminating access time as a variable—may enable yet another fundamental shift in how we think about data organization.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob draws an extended parallel between Test Driven Development and double entry bookkeeping, arguing that both are disciplines used by professionals who manipulate complex symbolic documents where correctness is critical. He notes that accountants face the same deadline pressures as programmers yet would never abandon their discipline, while programmers routinely make excuses to avoid TDD. The post satirically rewrites common anti-TDD arguments as if they were directed at double entry bookkeeping, exposing their absurdity. He concludes by noting that double entry bookkeeping took centuries to become universal, hoping TDD won't face the same slow adoption.
key_points: TDD and double entry bookkeeping are structurally identical: both involve representing work in two complementary forms and checking balance after each small change
Accountants face the same deadline pressures as programmers but would never suggest skipping half their process to meet a deadline
The excuses programmers make against TDD sound absurd when applied to the universally accepted practice of double entry bookkeeping
Double entry bookkeeping took 500+ years to become a global standard despite its obvious benefits
No other discipline provides the same confidence that complex code will avoid significant negative consequences
money_quotes: Can you imagine a group of accountants saying to each other: 'Guys, we're really under the gun. We've got to finish these accounts by Friday. So, do all the Assets and Equities. We'll come back and do the Liabilities later.'
No. You *can't* imagine that. Or, at least, you shouldn't. Accountants can go to jail for doing things like that.

Both allow those of us who maintain complex documents full of arcane symbols that must be absolutely correct under pain of severe consequences, to be confident that those consequences will not be encountered.
I'm going to go out on a limb here and declare with brutal honesty that it's *literally* a ritualistic waste of time.

TDD is a good discipline for ensuring that the complex documents, full of arcane symbols, are crafted in such a way so as to avoid significant negative consequences. I know of no other discipline that comes close.
Let's hope it doesn't take 500 years for a discipline of testing to becomes the standard for software developers.
posts.key_insight: The excuses programmers make to avoid TDD would be laughable if applied to double entry bookkeeping, revealing that resistance to testing discipline is about attitude, not practicality.

posts.summary: Martin draws an extended analogy between aviation physics and software development, comparing 'induced drag' from raising an airplane's nose to the drag created by messy code. Just as pilots operating 'behind the power curve' need more power to go slower, software teams with rotten code expend enormous effort for minimal progress. The solution in both cases is the same: lower the nose (clean up the code) to let thrust dominate again. He criticizes startup culture for believing that abandoning discipline leads to speed, when it actually creates the drag that slows them down.
key_points: Rotten code creates 'induced drag' that cancels out team effort, requiring massive power expenditure for minimal forward progress
Velocity charts serve as the software equivalent of an airspeed indicator, warning teams when they're falling behind the power curve
The solution to being behind the power curve is counterintuitive: slow down to clean up messes rather than pushing harder
Startup culture mistakenly believes high quality means low speed, leading them to abandon discipline and create the very drag that slows them down
Good Agile teams monitor velocity and increase refactoring when speed drops to eliminate induced drag
money_quotes: Too many software teams operate behind the power curve all the time. Rotten code is induced drag.
These teams have created so much induced drag that it takes a huge effort to make any forward progress. The team mushes forward at full power, barely making any headway.
Indeed, many teams have maxed out their power and are in a slow uncontrollable descent.
When the velocity starts going down, good agile teams increase their refactoring to eliminate the induced drag.
Startup culture in the U.S. believes in operating behind the power curve. That's where they think they want to be.
They are so focussed on fast progress, and so convinced that high quality means low speed, that they abandon discipline and principles for the sake of the goal.
They don't understand that when you make a mess, you induce drag, and you cancel out your power.
posts.key_insight: Messy code doesn't just slow you down—it creates a vicious cycle where the harder you push, the less progress you make, and the only escape is to stop and clean up.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob critiques his friend GeePaw Hill's assertion that TDD is purely about 'money' and speed, not about citizenship or ethics. While agreeing with Hill's five premises about TDD's benefits (faster value delivery, good judgement, internal quality, efficiency, external quality), Martin argues that Hill contradicts himself by claiming TDD isn't about quality or craftsmanship while simultaneously touting these benefits. Martin's central argument is that any practice helping programmers deliver value faster, use good judgement, and improve quality is inherently about professional ethics and good citizenship in the software community.
key_points: Hill's five TDD premises (faster value, judgement, internal quality, efficiency, external quality) are praised as solid and convincing
Hill's claim that TDD is 'not about citizenship or morality' directly contradicts his own arguments about quality and judgement
The five goals themselves define good moral citizenship; TDD is one valid path toward them
A programmer who neglects best practices for quality and efficiency cannot claim to be a good moral citizen
TDD may not be the only path to professional ethics, but it is a legitimate path, making it inherently about ethics
money_quotes: How can programmers be good moral citizens if they are not using the best means at their disposal to: deliver value faster, use good judgement, improve internal quality, increase efficiency, and improve external quality?

Is the author suggesting that a programmer who, out of negligence, goes slow, uses bad judgement, makes an internal mess, create gross inefficiencies, and delivers bad features is being a good moral citizen? I think that's absurd.
The fact that TDD helps with those five goals means that TDD is about good moral citizenship.
Striving to achieve those five goals is what makes you a good moral citizen of the software community. TDD is merely one of the paths that assist in that striving.
TDD is, in fact, about being a good moral citizen of the software community. TDD is about professional ethics.
Lastly, let me say that I eagerly await a better path to those goals. So far, I haven't found one.
posts.key_insight: Any practice that helps programmers deliver value, exercise judgement, and improve quality is inherently a matter of professional ethics—you cannot separate the pragmatic benefits from the moral obligation to pursue them.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob announces a new initiative to fly his personal airplane to clients within 500 nautical miles of Chicago, offering his talks and training at half price. The catch is flexibility on scheduling due to weather dependencies and his preference to return home each night. He frames this as both an opportunity to enjoy his new pilot's license and a way to serve the underserved 'fly-over country' that rarely gets visits from industry speakers and authors.
key_points: Offering half-price talks and training to anyone within 500 nautical miles of Chicago
Scheduling flexibility required due to weather-dependent flying conditions
Prefers day trips to be home with family each evening
Sees the Midwest as underserved by tech industry speakers and wants to change that
Suggests local airports as potential venues for user group workshops
money_quotes: I just got my pilot's license. That means I can fly! So now I want to fly – to you!
Sorry, I'm not flying through thunder storms to get to you. No. Not going to happen. Deal with it!
I love my wife! I love my family! What can I say? I want to be home.
Well, the sky's the limit!
Flying is a hoot! I enjoy the hell out of it.
I live smack in the middle of fly-over country. This, it seems to me, is an area of the country that is badly underserved by speakers like me.
There are lots of programmers inside that circle who never see authors, or speakers, or industry experts. I'd like to do my part to change that.
posts.key_insight: Uncle Bob sees an opportunity to combine his love of flying with serving programmers in the underserved Midwest who rarely get access to industry thought leaders.

posts.summary: Martin reflects on programmers' dual responsibility by juxtaposing two events: SpaceX's Falcon Heavy launch that sent a Tesla to the asteroid belt, and the death of Elaine Herzberg, killed by an Uber self-driving car. He argues that programmers enabled both the triumph and the tragedy through their code. The post challenges the notion that ethics should be separate from programming practices, asserting that the profession's impact on society makes moral considerations intrinsic to the work. Martin concludes that programmers can no longer isolate themselves in 'techie bubbles' and must recognize that every line of code carries ethical weight.
key_points: Software permeates every aspect of modern achievements - from CAD/CAM to simulations to communications, billions of lines of code enabled SpaceX's historic launch
Programmers bear collective responsibility for harms caused by their code, even when no individual acted maliciously or negligently
Machine learning systems are 'insidiously difficult to understand,' making it potentially impossible to identify why autonomous systems fail
The sentiment that ethics should be excluded from discussions of programming practices is disturbing given software's life-and-death consequences
Code now affects everything from baby monitors to nuclear weapons policy, making programmer isolation from societal concerns untenable
money_quotes: Every minute step along the pathway from the dream, to the realization, was lubricated, enabled, enhanced, and simplified by software.
It was a message that we sent to ourselves, and to the universe at large, saying that we are coming, and nothing in this universe will stop us.
Elaine Herzberg is dead. She was struck by a 'self-driving' car while walking her bicycle across the road. And we programmers killed her.
Perhaps there was an IF statement somewhere in that code that, had the boolean predicate been in the opposite state, would have prevented the collision.
Machine learning neural networks are insidiously difficult to understand.
It seems to me that ethics and morality have become intrinsic to everything we programmers do; because so very much depends upon the quality of our work.
We programmers must no longer hide in our little techie bubbles.
Every line of code represents an ethical and moral decision.
posts.key_insight: Programmers can no longer treat their work as purely technical because the ubiquity of software in modern life makes every line of code an ethical and moral decision with real consequences for human lives.

posts.summary: Martin argues that 'Agile in the Large' is a contradiction in terms because Agile was specifically designed to solve small-project problems, not large ones. He traces software development from the 1960s through waterfall's necessity in the 70s-80s (due to long feedback loops) to Agile's emergence when Moore's law enabled sub-second turnarounds. The key insight is that humans have always known how to execute large projects through hierarchy and command-and-control—what we lacked was an effective approach for small teams. Agile solved the small problem; large projects are solved by breaking them into many small Agile teams coordinated through traditional management structures.
key_points: Agile was a 'turning of the wheel' back to 1960s-style short feedback cycles, but with added disciplines like testing, refactoring, and automation
Waterfall emerged from necessity—when edit/compile/test loops took 24 hours, extensive planning was required
Humans have always excelled at large projects (Apollo, D-Day, global economy)—the 'big' part was never the problem we needed to solve
Agile teams are the lowest level of command and control—'the squad'—above which traditional hierarchy becomes necessary
Agile in the Large approaches are really traditional project management with Agile vocabulary and flavor
money_quotes: Agile was a revolution in how relatively small teams can develop relatively small software projects. Note the emphasis on the word small.
We've always known how to do big projects. You do big projects by breaking them up into a bunch of small projects. Agile solved the small part of that.
Agile was never about egalitarianism. Agile was never about the rejection of plans, or the rejection of command and control.
Indeed, Agile was the embodiment of the lowest level unit of command and control: The squad.
Once you are doing something big, you have left the realm of Agile.
We went from 24 hour turnarounds in 1970, to 60 minute turnarounds in 1980, to ten minute turnarounds in 1990, to ten second turnarounds in 2000.
You don't design, build, produce and sell hundreds of millions of iPhones without an awful lot of command and control.
posts.key_insight: Agile was designed to solve small-team, small-project problems—large projects require traditional command-and-control structures, and calling those structures 'Agile' is just adding flavor to millennia-old human organizational patterns.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Functional Programming and Object-Oriented Programming are not opposing paradigms but orthogonal concepts that work well together. He reduces OO to its essential characteristic: dynamic polymorphism that eliminates source code dependencies between caller and callee. Similarly, he reduces FP to referential transparency—the guarantee that functions always return the same output for the same input. Since these two concepts are independent dimensions, they can and should be combined to create systems that are both decoupled (via polymorphism) and predictable (via immutability).
key_points: OO's essential characteristic is polymorphism that breaks source code dependencies, not encapsulation or inheritance
FP's essential characteristic is referential transparency—no reassignment of values
The two paradigms are orthogonal (independent) but not mutually exclusive
Combining OO and FP yields systems that are decoupled, testable, predictable, and resistant to race conditions
The anti-OO bias in the FP community stems from a natural but mistaken tendency to reject old techniques when learning new ones
money_quotes: OO without polymorphism is not OO.
The mechanism of polymorphism must not create a source code dependency from the caller to the callee.
In a functional program, every time you call a particular function with a particular value, you will get the same result; no matter how long the program has been executing.
Orthogonality does not imply mutual exclusion.
There is no FP vs OO.
A system that is built on both OO and FP principles will maximize flexibility, maintainability, testability, simplicity, and robustness.
Excluding one in favor of the other can only weaken the structure of a system.
posts.key_insight: FP and OO are orthogonal rather than opposing paradigms—polymorphism for decoupling and referential transparency for predictability are independent virtues that strengthen each other when combined.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explains his decision to change how he uses gendered language around the term 'Craftsman' after a conversation with Liz Keogh, a software craftswoman he deeply respects. She explained that seeing 'Craftsman' in formal writing, even when used neutrally, made her feel 'set apart' and excluded. He acknowledges his surprise at this effect and commits to changing his usage, providing a detailed algorithm for when to use Craftsman, Craftswoman, Craftsperson, and various plural forms. The post reflects his characteristic programmer's approach to solving problems—creating explicit rules and conditional logic for his own behavior.
key_points: Liz Keogh, whom Uncle Bob considers more skilled than himself, explained that 'Craftsman' in formal writing made her feel excluded
He commits to using gender-specific terms when referring to individuals and balanced constructions for neutral cases
He rejects alternative terms like Artisan or Professional because they don't carry the same 'tone and weight'
He reverses gender order between singular and plural forms to avoid appearing to prefer one gender
He acknowledges his discomfort with 'Craftsperson' but is willing to let time determine if that wanes
money_quotes: She graciously, and patiently, explained to me that the appearance of the word 'Craftsman' in written formal text, even when used in the gender neutral case, made her feel 'set apart'.
I want nothing I say or write to make a woman like Liz Keogh – or any other woman for that matter – feel excluded.
I'm a programmer. I need a formula – an algorithm – I need to know how to write the if statements in my brain.
Call me dense if you like (I've been called worse) but I did not realize that the usage of the word was having this effect on her.
This ought to keep the order balanced, so that it does not appear that I am preferring one gender over another.
posts.key_insight: When someone you respect tells you your language makes them feel excluded, the right response is to change your language, not to defend your intent.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that Gherkin's GIVEN/WHEN/THEN syntax has become so effective for specifying requirements because it implicitly describes finite state machine transitions. He draws a parallel between Gherkin (GWT) and unit test patterns (Arrange/Act/Assert), showing they are isomorphic—both describe state transitions. The post demonstrates how viewing requirements as state machines helps identify missing edge cases by systematically exploring all possible state-event pairs. Uncle Bob concludes that Gherkin succeeds because it bridges specification, testing, and programming disciplines with a common language rooted in formal state machine notation.
key_points: Gherkin's GIVEN/WHEN/THEN triplet is fundamentally a state machine transition specification (current state, event, next state)
The Arrange/Act/Assert pattern in unit tests is isomorphic to GIVEN/WHEN/THEN—both describe state transitions
A complete suite of Gherkin scenarios combined with TDD unit tests specifies the complete finite state machine of system behavior
Viewing systems as state machines makes it trivial to find missing state-event pairs that cause bugs
Gherkin succeeds because it speaks equally to specification, testing, and programming disciplines
money_quotes: This means that a suite of Gherkin scenarios is a specification of a finite state machine.
Gherkin is a language that sits perfectly between, and speaks equally to, the three disciplines of specification, testing, and programming.
How often have you found, and fixed, bugs in systems that were due to some event happening in a state that didn't expect it? If you are like me, it happens all the time.
When a system is described in state transition form, and when the states and transitions are well identified, then the hunt for the missing {state, event} pairs becomes trivial.
The formalism that business analysts find most conducive to specifying system requirements just happens to be the same formalism that programmers frequently use to specify system behavior.
There is no better mechanism for exploring the behavior of a system than a well specified state machine.
posts.key_insight: Gherkin's effectiveness stems from its implicit description of finite state machines, which provides a formal bridge between business requirements, tests, and code while making it easy to discover missing edge cases.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob uses a Socratic dialogue to draw a fascinating connection between Diophantine equations and software estimation. He explains that digital computers fundamentally operate on integers, making every program specification effectively a Diophantine equation. He then invokes Church and Turing's proof that decidability (determining whether a Diophantine equation has solutions) cannot be guaranteed by any finite algorithm. The ironic conclusion: the founding documents of computer science prove that software estimates cannot be universally guaranteed, since estimation is a form of deciding whether a solution exists.
key_points: Digital computers compute exclusively with integers, making every program input and output integral
A program specification is essentially a Diophantine equation, and the program finds solutions to that equation
Church and Turing proved there is no general finite algorithm to decide if a Diophantine equation has solutions
Software estimation is fundamentally a decidability problem - asking whether a program can be written
While most specifications are estimable in practice, there is no universal guarantee that any program can be estimated
money_quotes: A digital computer is a computer that computes with integers. Nothing but integers.
The specification of a program is a great big Diophantine equation in a bazillion unknowns, and the program that meets that specification finds solutions to that ginormous equation.
A digital computer program, therefore, represents a Diophantine equation.
The founding documents of computer science are documents that prove that there is no finite mechanism for deciding if a program can even be written.
An estimate is a decision.
The founding of computer science was based on the proof that estimates were not guaranteed.
I think it's deliciously ironic that it was the proof of NOESTIMATES that founded computer science.
posts.key_insight: The mathematical foundations of computer science—specifically Church and Turing's proofs on decidability—demonstrate that software estimation is fundamentally unprovable in the general case, making #NoEstimates ironically baked into the field's origins.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on Sarah Mei's 'Livable Code' talk and the provocative idea that code can be too clean. He uses his own office as a metaphor: it has clear organization that makes things findable, but also deliberate messiness that provides convenience for transient needs. He argues that software systems benefit from the same balance—rigorous cleanliness with small, intentional rule-breakages that create useful affordances. The key is maintaining diligence about cleanup while accepting that lived-in code is not shameful.
key_points: The craftsmanship movement has always focused on building professional and ethical teams, not just individual behavior
Software systems should be 'livable'—neither a tangled mess nor antiseptically clean
Small amounts of mess provide 'affordances for transient issues' that make systems more practical to work in
Techniques like temporarily merging separated code to get things working, then re-separating, embody 'Worse is Better'
Diligence about cleanup is essential—the mess must not spin out of control
money_quotes: Can a system be too clean? Is it possible to focus so much on cleanliness that no one can practically work in the system?
The cleanliness and organization of the office makes it useable. I know where things are. I know how to access them.
The slight messiness of the office is convenient. The mess is there because of transient issues.
The act of using the office would reintroduce the mess!
When I write code I fight very hard to keep it clean. But there are also little places where I break the rules specifically because those breakages create affordances for transient issues.
We should not be ashamed if our code looks a little bit lived in.
On the other hand, we need to be diligent about cleaning up after ourselves; and not let the mess spin out of control.
posts.key_insight: Perfect cleanliness can be as impractical as chaos—livable code balances rigorous organization with intentional affordances for the messy reality of ongoing work.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that the Agile movement betrayed its original purpose by becoming a project-management certification business, pushing out the programmers who founded it. He contends that Software Craftsmanship is not a splinter movement but rather the continuation of Agile's true values after the 'Agile Industrial Complex' abandoned them. The piece responds to Martin Fowler calling the Craftsmanship movement 'tragic,' reframing the tragedy as Agile's corruption rather than Craftsmanship's formation. Uncle Bob maintains that Agile was never about project management—it was about programmers working well, collaborating with customers, and delivering value.
key_points: The Agile movement was started by programmers but was taken over by project managers focused on certifications, conferences, and 'the next big thing'
Software Craftsmanship is not a breakaway movement—it's programmers staying the course while the Agile movement moved away from them
Agile and project management are 'utterly orthogonal'—Agile is values and disciplines for building software, not a way to manage projects
The proof that Agile abandoned programmers: programmers started Agile conferences but no longer attend them
A two-day Craftsmanship certification would be 'laughed out of the room'—the very idea exposes how certifications trivialize what matters
money_quotes: Craftsmanship is the Agile, that the Agile movement left behind.
They pushed so many project managers in, they pushed the programmers out.
Anyone who suggested a two-day course to certify Craftsmanship would be laughed out of the room, laughed out of the town, and laughed out of the state.
Agile was never about project management; but that's what they've turned it into.
An Agile team will be Agile no matter how the project is managed. On the other hand, a team that is not Agile will not become Agile simply by virtue of a new and fancy project management strategy.
Thinking that a team can become Agile simply because a Certified Scrum Master is the project manager is a pipe dream.
The Agile movement does not heal the divide between programmers and managers; it exacerbates it.
posts.key_insight: Software Craftsmanship isn't a retreat from Agile—it's the continuation of Agile's original programmer-centric values after the Agile movement abandoned them to become a project management certification industry.

posts.summary: Martin argues that software social justice advocates have progressively abandoned legitimate concerns in favor of increasingly absurd complaints. He traces a progression from reasonable objections about gendered language in 'Software Craftsmanship' (which the community accommodated) to claims that Agile practices are ineffective because they were created by white men, to the final absurdity of claiming 'DDD' is offensive because it's a bra size. He concludes that these advocates are now pursuing outrage for its own sake rather than genuine social justice, and have 'jumped the shark' into irrelevance.
key_points: The software community initially accommodated concerns about gendered language like 'craftsmanship' by adopting alternatives like 'craftspeople'

Claims that Agile/XP practices don't work for diverse teams because they were 'developed by white dudes' represent a logical absurdity that would invalidate most of Western civilization's achievements
The complaint that 'DDD' is exclusionary because it's a bra size represents peak absurdity and loss of credibility
What may have started as legitimate advocacy has devolved into outrage for outrage's sake
The pursuit of things to be offended by has replaced genuine concern for the disadvantaged
money_quotes: Perhaps we should have realized, then and there, that the software SJWs were heading over a cliff in their aimless pursuit of: something to be outraged about.
Perhaps, then and there, we should have realized that the true goal of the software SJWs was outrage – for the sake of outrage – and was no longer, truly, social justice.

I mean if the race and gender of the authors was the sole determinant of effectiveness, then one would have to condemn the Declaration of Independence, and The Magna Carta, the works of Shakespear, and The Cat in the Hat as being ineffective for the same reason.
The software Social Justice Warriors have Jumped the Shark.
Perhaps, when they started, their goals were laudable. Perhaps they really wanted to help folks who were disadvantaged in some way.
posts.key_insight: Legitimate advocacy movements lose credibility when they abandon substantive concerns in favor of increasingly trivial grievances pursued for the sake of outrage itself.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob presents a manifesto for 'The Unoffended' - a philosophical stance advocating for radical tolerance in free society. The document declares that members of a free society should strive to remain unoffended by others' speech, identity, beliefs, and actions. The only exception is intentional or negligent harm done to others. The piece argues that all ideas must be heard and evaluated on their merits for the best ideas to rise, and that ideas themselves are not harmful unless they specifically incite harmful actions.
key_points: A free society depends on members' tolerance and forbearance toward each other and each other's ideas
Being 'unoffended' means accepting disagreement as a gift rather than an attack
All ideas must be heard and evaluated on their merits for the best ones to rise to the top
Ideas themselves are not harmful unless they specifically incite harmful actions
The only thing worth being intolerant of is intentional or negligent harm to others
money_quotes: We therefore strive to remain unoffended by the freedom of others to speak and act according to their identity, politics, ambitions, and desires.
We are intolerant only of intentional or negligent harm done to others.
Your willingness to share disagreeing thoughts and ideas is a gift that we value and cherish.
We believe that, in order for the best ideas to rise to the top, all ideas must be heard and evaluated on their merits.
We believe that ideas are not harmful, so long as they do not specifically incite harmful actions.
Celebrate, support, and defend your right to enjoy the personal dignity of your identity, your beliefs, and your choices.
posts.key_insight: Tolerance of disagreement and uncomfortable ideas is not weakness but the foundation of a free society and intellectual progress.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explores the differences between OO and functional approaches to processing game state, specifically updating lists of shots and targets in a Spacewar game. The OO approach mutates state directly, while the functional approach must create new instances of everything, tracking both changed and unchanged elements. He initially viewed the functional requirement to separate and recombine unchanged elements as wasteful extra work. However, he arrives at an insight comparing it to selecting bicycles from a garage for a race—you must separate them, modify them, and return them to the collection. He notes that functional languages use clever persistent data structures to avoid actual copying by sharing unchanged elements.
key_points: OO programming allows direct mutation of world state, making updates straightforward but impure
Functional programming requires creating entirely new instances rather than modifying existing state
Tail call optimization enables recursive world transformation without stack overflow
The functional approach requires explicitly tracking both changed AND unchanged elements to reconstruct state
Persistent data structures eliminate the performance cost of functional immutability by sharing unchanged elements
money_quotes: That's what functional programming is all about. You aren't allowed to change the value of any existing variables.
In order to build a new instance of the world, I must keep track of those parts of the world that changed, and all the parts of the world that did not change.
I must separate them. Make the modifications. Then add them back together again. I can't just reach into the world and make specific changes.
At first I thought that this was extra work. It seemed wasteful to me that I had to separate and keep track of the unchanged elements. However, then I had a flash of insight.

Imagine that you have a dozen bicycles in your garage. You and your partner are about to enter a race, and you need to select and prepare two bicycles. You must separate those two bicycles from the rest, prepare them, race them, and then put them back into the garage with the rest of the bicycles.
posts.key_insight: Functional programming's requirement to explicitly track unchanged elements alongside changes mirrors how we naturally handle physical objects—separating, modifying, and recombining—and persistent data structures make this efficient.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob analyzes the 737 Max 8 crashes through the lens of a pilot and software engineer, concluding that software literally killed everyone aboard by wresting control from pilots and plunging the aircraft into the ground. He absolves Boeing of ill intent while cataloging their critical software mistakes: trusting a single sensor without cross-checking, giving software power to override pilots, and ignoring pilot counteractions. He compares these failures to NASA's Apollo 1 and Challenger disasters—catastrophic errors made by smart people who didn't think they were taking risks. His ultimate message is that programmers must accept personal responsibility for the harm their code can cause.
key_points: The MCAS software trusted a single AOA sensor without cross-checking against other instruments—a fundamental violation of instrument flying discipline
Software was given power to override and overpower pilots, violating the core aviation principle that pilots must maintain ultimate control
