Boeing's fatal mistake was treating MCAS as a 'minor tweak' rather than flight-critical software, infecting everyone's attitude toward proper care
Smart people at respected organizations (NASA, Boeing) can make catastrophic errors without ill intent through groupthink, overconfidence, and mission focus
Programmers bear personal responsibility for harm caused by their code and must have the courage to say 'No' when deployment risk is too high
money_quotes: The software executing within those planes killed everyone aboard.
Programmers must not be treated as requirement robots. Rather, programmers must have intimate knowledge of the domain they are programming in.
This evokes the terrifying mental image of a pilot hauling back on the yoke for all he's worth while the airplane inexorably plunges headlong into the ground.
We, programmers, are killing people. Our errors cause loss, injury, and death. It's our fingers on the keyboards. It's our code running in the machines.
If our code might cause injury, loss, or death, it is up to us to foresee, and then prevent, that harm from occurring.
We need to have the courage to say 'No' when we assess that the risk of deploying our code is too high.
This is not a responsibility we can shirk. It's on us; because it's our code.
posts.key_insight: When software has the power to cause death, programmers cannot hide behind requirements or organizational decisions—they bear personal moral responsibility for understanding the domain and preventing foreseeable harm.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to a Twitter debate with Mark Seemann about whether static typing reduces the number of tests needed. He demonstrates his argument by implementing a random element selection function in Clojure using strict TDD, contrasting it with Mark's Haskell implementation. Bob argues that tests describe correct behavior, and if all system components are tested properly, invalid states will never occur regardless of type system. He concludes that the number of tests required for practical correctness is the same whether using static or dynamic typing, since disciplined TDD ensures invalid arguments are never passed in the first place.
key_points: Static typing does not reduce the number of behavioral tests needed because tests verify behavior, not types
The TDD strategy of 'Don't go for the Gold' means gradually increasing test complexity by handling degenerate and trivial cases before tackling the core algorithm
If every component is written with proper tests, invalid states will never be represented, making defensive tests for nil/invalid inputs unnecessary
Mark Seemann's type-focused approach consumed thought on type structure, while TDD focuses purely on desired behavior stepped forward incrementally
The debate centers on whether making illegal states 'unrepresentable' via types is better than making them 'unrepresented' via disciplined testing
money_quotes: Static typing does not reduce that number of tests, because those tests are behavioral and empirical.
Don't go for the Gold. Gradually increase the complexity of your tests by staying away from the center of the algorithm for as long as possible.
I don't need to make states unrepresentable, if I know those states will never be represented.
If the behavior of each element of the system is correct, then no element of the system will pass invalid arguments to any other.
Testing shows the presence, not the absence of bugs.
This is a dynamically typed language. You get exceptions when you mess up the types.

If you call this function from a module that you wrote with tests, using something as effective as the TDD discipline, you won't be passing nil or negative numbers, or any other form of invalid arguments.
posts.key_insight: The discipline of comprehensive TDD makes defensive type checking redundant because well-tested code never passes invalid arguments, rendering the static-vs-dynamic typing debate moot for test count.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob distinguishes between objects and data structures as complementary opposites: objects are functions operating on implied data, while data structures are data operated upon by implied functions. This distinction has profound implications for software design, particularly regarding ORMs (which transfer data between structures, not map to objects), the object/relational impedance mismatch, and how we add types versus functions. The post demonstrates that classes make adding new types easy but functions hard, while data structures make adding functions easy but types hard. Additionally, data structures create source code dependencies that flow toward implementations (causing widespread recompilation), while classes invert these dependencies, isolating callers from changes.
key_points: Objects and data structures are complements, not similar entities - objects hide data behind functions, data structures expose data with implied functions
ORMs don't map objects to tables; they transfer data between data structures because database schemas and object models serve different purposes
Adding types is easy with classes but hard with data structures; adding functions is easy with data structures but hard with classes
Classes enable dependency inversion - implementation source files depend on interfaces, not vice versa - isolating callers from recompilation
The object/relational impedance mismatch isn't solved by ORMs because objects and data structures are opposites constrained by different forces
money_quotes: An Object is a set of functions that operate upon implied data elements. A Data Structure is a set of data elements operated upon by implied functions.
Objects are the opposite of data structures.
There is no such thing as an Object Relational Mapper; because there is no mapping between database tables and objects.
Business objects define the structure of the business behavior. Database schemas define the structure of the business data. Those two structures are constrained by very different forces.
There is no impedance mismatch because objects and data structures are complementary, not isomorphic.
Adding new functions to a set of classes is hard, you have to change each class. Adding new types to a set of data structures is hard, you have to change each function.
Classes isolate callers from recompilation and redeployment.
posts.key_insight: Objects and data structures are not interchangeable but complementary opposites, and understanding this distinction is essential for making informed design decisions about extensibility, dependencies, and system architecture.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob recounts a frustrating restaurant experience where a POS terminal wouldn't allow a waiter to order two of the same sausage type, illustrating how software often constrains rather than enables users. He argues that while early software's inflexibility was forgivable given the novelty of automation, modern users rightfully expect software to get out of their way. The post challenges programmers to take personal responsibility for usability, not hiding behind UX teams or specifications. Developers should actively play with their software, invent use cases, and find constraints before users do.
key_points: Software should be more flexible than the manual processes it replaces, not more restrictive
The era of forgiving inflexible software is over—users now expect technology to adapt to them
Programmers are stakeholders whose reputation rides on the product's success
Developers should actively test their software as users would, inventing edge cases rather than blindly implementing specs
UX is everyone's responsibility, regardless of job title
money_quotes: Why won't it let me…?
Software is supposed to make life easier. Software is supposed to ease and enable the job of users.
All too often, however, software is constraining and restrictive. If you don't do what the software expects, the software fights you and stops you.
Nowadays we expect software to get out of our way.
That POS terminal should not get in the way.
Remember that as a programmer you are also a stakeholder. Your reputation is on the line.
Yes, I know, UX is not your job. But do it anyway; because your name is on that product.
posts.key_insight: Programmers must take personal ownership of usability because their name is on the product, regardless of whether UX is formally their job.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that after five decades of programming in dozens of languages, Clojure (a modern Lisp) has emerged as his favorite and the language he believes will eventually become standard. His singular reason is 'economy of expression' - Clojure's near-absence of syntax and grammar allows programmers to express problems clearly with minimal effort. He demonstrates this by showing how the same program requires far less code in Clojure than Java, while addressing common objections about parentheses, performance, dynamic typing, and tooling. He concludes that Lisp's minimal syntax, functional nature, and remarkable persistence over 60+ years make it uniquely suited for building large systems.
key_points: Clojure's power comes from one thing: economy of expression enabled by almost no syntax or grammar
Despite initial skepticism, minimal syntax proved far more conducive to building large systems than heavyweight languages like C++ or Java
Dynamic typing concerns can be addressed through tests and clojure/spec, and the syntax savings are worth the tradeoff
Lisp has survived for 60+ years despite repeated attempts to kill it, outlasting languages like Smalltalk and Forth
New languages offer nothing revolutionary - they're just recombinations of old ideas with extra syntax baggage
money_quotes: The language has almost no syntax or grammar.

I've come to a conclusion. My favorite language of all, the language that I think will outlast all the others, the language that I believe will eventually become the standard language that all programmers use… is Lisp.
What I found, instead, was that the minimal syntax of Clojure is far more conducive to building large systems, than the heavier syntax of Java or C++.
OK, I get it. You like static typing. Fine. You use a nice statically typed language, and I'll use Clojure. And I'll be in Scotland before ye.

The thing about those new languages is that there's nothing new in any of them. They're just made up of the pieces of old languages that they cut up and shook in a jar and then dumped out in a game of language Yahtzee.
It's the language that refuses to die. We've tried to kill it many times. But like the annoying neighborhood stray cat it keeps … coming … back.
Finally, Lisp is functional. And the future is looking very functional to me.
posts.key_insight: A language's power lies not in its features but in its economy of expression - the less syntax standing between thought and code, the better equipped you are to build large, maintainable systems.

posts.summary: Martin uses a personal story about his wife reconnecting with a distant Mexican relative through 23andMe and old digitized photos to illustrate software's profound role in modern life. The chain of software tools—photo scanning apps, genetic analysis, email, FaceTime—enabled a deeply emotional family reunion across decades and borders. He argues that software has become the 'circulatory system of our civilization,' essential not just for survival but for human connection and joy. This recognition of software's importance leads him to call for greater discipline and professionalism in the industry, arguing that programmers must move beyond the 'wild west' mentality given how much now depends on their work.
key_points: A chain of software tools (23andMe, Photomyne, Photos, email, FaceTime) enabled an emotional family reunion spanning 50 years and international borders
Software is not merely utilitarian infrastructure but enables moments of human joy and connection
Programmers should feel pride in building systems that support both civilization's survival and its emotional life
The immense societal dependence on software demands that the industry adopt deeper discipline and professionalism
The 'wild west' era of software development must end because too much now depends on what programmers build
money_quotes: Software is the circulatory system of our civilization.
Software digests, filters, and sorts the constituents of the information stream.
Software is the heart, the lungs, the vessels, the liver and kidneys, and the digestive system of our civilization.
Nothing works anymore without software. Our civilization could no longer survive without software.
It was software that enabled the warm tears of recollection to flow.
It is things like this that make me proud to be a programmer.
Too much depends upon us now. We're going to have to leave the wild west of software behind and civilize ourselves, so that the civilization we support will continue to prosper.
posts.key_insight: Software has become civilization's circulatory system—enabling not just survival but human connection—which obligates programmers to embrace greater professionalism and discipline.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob traces the evolution of programmers' representation in popular culture from 1956 to 1999, examining how movies depicted both computers and their creators. In early films, computers were mysterious characters (often villains) while programmers were portrayed as mad scientists or emotionally damaged geniuses. Over time, the computer shifted from main character to mere tool, while programmers evolved from obscure figures to 'the guy next door' to messianic saviors. The essay concludes that programmers have now become so integral to filmmaking itself that they no longer need cinematic representation—they've transitioned from being subjects of movies to being essential creators of them.
key_points: Early sci-fi (1950s-60s) portrayed programmers as brilliant but emotionally unstable scientists in the Dr. Morbius mold
The 1980s-90s saw computers shift from villainous characters to neutral tools while programmers became more humanized
Jurassic Park (1993) marked the first truly 'ordinary' programmer character who could be your neighbor
The Matrix represents programmers as simultaneously mundane ('live next door') and messianic saviors
Modern filmmaking's dependence on programming has made explicit programmer characters unnecessary—we've become the industry rather than its subject
money_quotes: It's important to remember that prior to 1946 there were no programmers, that computers themselves were virtually unknown until the late '50s.
The implication is that programmers are so intelligent and driven that they eventually lose emotional stability.
Note that during this era it is the computer that is the character. The programmers, if mentioned at all, are ancillary.
This is our first real glimpse of a humanized programmer. Dennis Nedry is not a mad scientist, not a well respected researcher, he's just a common ordinary programmer.
Does society really think we are the folks who live next door who are simultaneously the hidden saviors?
I think we have transitioned off the screen and have become part of the movie industry.
So now they know us intimately. We do live next door. And they don't need to put us on the screen anymore.
posts.key_insight: Programmers have evolved in cultural perception from mysterious mad scientists to ordinary neighbors, and have become so essential to modern filmmaking that they no longer need fictional representation—they've become the industry itself.

posts.summary: Robert C. Martin writes an open letter criticizing the Linux Foundation's handling of a Code of Conduct case against Charles Max Wood, who was banned from KubeCon after a public Twitter complaint citing his MAGA hat and a request for civil dialogue. Martin argues that both the complaint and the Foundation's public response were themselves violations of proper conduct, as they caused harm to Wood without due process. He contends that Code of Conduct proceedings should be private and confidential, and that public complaints should be rejected as potential harassment. Martin demands the Linux Foundation explain their decision-making process, apologize to Wood, and establish transparent procedures for future cases.
key_points: Public Code of Conduct complaints should be rejected as potential harassment and violations themselves
The Linux Foundation's public response caused demonstrable harm to the accused without due process
Organizations need documented, transparent processes for accepting and adjudicating conduct complaints
Political affiliation should not be grounds for Code of Conduct violations
The accused should have rights of appeal and privacy protection
money_quotes: It seems to me that Code of Conduct complaints made in public must be immediately rejected and viewed as Code of Conduct violations in and of themselves.
Code of Conduct complaints should be submitted in private and remain private and confidential in order to prevent their use as a means of harassment.
Making them a public showcase is, simply, horrible.

Those of us who have watched this case may be forced to conclude that LF has no internal process, that no due diligence will be applied to Code of Conduct complaints and determinations, that the accused will have no rights either of appeal or privacy.
LF owes Mr. Wood, and the Software Community at large, a profound apology.
I find it highly problematic that the complaint and the decision were public.
posts.key_insight: Code of Conduct processes become instruments of harassment when conducted publicly, and organizations must protect the accused through private proceedings and documented due process.

posts.summary: Written as a fictional dialogue set in 2045 between a father and son discussing the COVID-19 pandemic, Martin argues that programmers were unsung heroes of the crisis. While acknowledging the frontline bravery of healthcare workers, grocery employees, and delivery drivers, he makes the case that software infrastructure—from internet communications to contactless payments to RNA sequencing—was essential to society's ability to shelter in place and eventually defeat the virus. The piece frames programming as a profession of quiet but profound societal importance.
key_points: Healthcare workers, grocery staff, and delivery drivers showed physical bravery by working despite infection risks
Programmers enabled remote work, communication, shopping, and entertainment that made sheltering at home viable
Software systems for contactless payment reduced virus transmission compared to paper money
RNA sequencing software gave researchers a head start on vaccine development
The pandemic was the first national emergency where instant digital communication reached almost everyone
money_quotes: They were as brave as any soldier going to war. Perhaps braver, because you couldn't see the enemy, and in those days you couldn't fight it.
Many of us did. We were the lucky ones.
Even though most people weren't working, the essential parts of our civilization had to be kept running.
It was the programmers who made it possible for people to work from home; because it was the programmers who built the software that made the internet possible.
This was the first full scale national emergency during which people had instant access to the news, to the government, and to each other.
Without the programmers who made those systems, people would have had a much harder time sheltering at home; and the pandemic would have been much worse.
It's an important job, Son. I never want to be anything else. Except, of course, your Dad.
posts.key_insight: While programmers weren't frontline heroes risking their lives, the digital infrastructure they built was essential to society's ability to survive the pandemic by enabling people to work, communicate, shop, and be entertained from the safety of their homes.

posts.summary: This is an introductory tutorial to Clojure syntax and programming fundamentals. Uncle Bob walks readers through the basics of lists, function calls, vectors, and function definitions in Clojure. He demonstrates how expressions are evaluated in the REPL, explains the difference between lists and vectors, and builds up to writing recursive functions like factorial and sum. The post emphasizes how minimal Clojure's syntax really is—by the end of the tutorial, readers have learned 'the vast majority of Clojure syntax.'
key_points: Clojure's syntax is remarkably minimal—function calls are just lists where the first element is the function and the rest are arguments
Lists are linked lists (good for stacks), vectors are growable arrays (good for queues and indexing)
The REPL evaluates any list as a function call, so you must quote lists to prevent evaluation
Clojure uses 64-bit integers by default but supports unlimited precision with the N notation
Higher-order functions like apply and reduce provide powerful abstractions for working with collections
money_quotes: A function call is really just a list. The function is the first element of the list, and the arguments are just the other elements of that list.
You now know the vast majority of Clojure syntax. There's more, of course, but you already know enough to write significant programs.
Lists make good stacks. Vectors make good queues.
If you just want the list (1 2) at the REPL you can convince the REPL not to call the list as a function by quoting it.
That works nicely, until we exceed 64 bits of precision. Clojure likes to use 64 bit integers for efficiency.
posts.key_insight: Clojure's elegance lies in its minimal syntax where everything is a list, and function calls are simply lists being evaluated.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob provides a gentle introduction to functional programming concepts in Clojure by walking through the implementation of a prime number finder. He demonstrates core functions like map, filter, range, and let bindings while building the solution incrementally. The key pedagogical point is the shift from procedural thinking (if statements, while loops) to dataflow thinking—envisioning data flowing through transformations. He playfully admits to testing with his eyes rather than formal TDD, and deliberately leaves a bug (1 appearing as prime, 2 missing) as an exercise for the reader.
key_points: Clojure's map and filter functions enable a declarative, dataflow approach to problem-solving
The let binding creates immutable local names, not traditional variables
Clojure seamlessly interoperates with Java libraries (demonstrated with Math/sqrt)
Functional programming replaces if statements and while loops with data flowing through filters and mappers
The mindset shift from procedural to dataflow thinking is fundamental to functional programming
money_quotes: No if statements. No while loops. Instead we envisioned lists of data flowing through filters and mappers.
The solution was almost more of a fluid dynamics problem than a software problem.
Instead of imagining a procedural solution, we imagine a data-flow solution.
Think hard on this – it is one of the keys to functional programming.
It allows you to create names that are bound to expressions... though the word 'variable' is not quite right because they cannot be reassigned. They are immutable.
I know. Blasphemy! So sue me. ;-)
posts.key_insight: Functional programming fundamentally shifts your mental model from step-by-step procedures to visualizing data flowing through transformations—this dataflow mindset is the key to thinking functionally.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob describes his experience using REPL Driven Design instead of TDD for a Clojure program that processes COVID-19 data. Initially, REPL-driven development felt faster and more satisfying, with quick feedback cycles and the ability to use real production data. However, when he needed to make structural changes, he found himself paralyzed by fear due to lack of test coverage, ultimately deploying a bug that went undetected for four days. He concludes that the decoupling TDD forces upon developers is precisely the design that enables flexibility and change.
key_points: REPL Driven Design offers satisfying quick feedback cycles that feel nearly as fast as TDD
Using real production data instead of mocks creates a false sense of speed and confidence
Without tests, structural changes become fear-inducing because there's no safety net to catch regressions
The design changes TDD forces (for mocking and isolation) are the same changes needed for flexibility
What feels easier in the short term often creates technical debt that surfaces during refactoring
money_quotes: And suddenly I found myself full of fear. I had no way to ensure that those design changes wouldn't leave the system broken in some way.
Had I stuck with the TDD discipline I would have automatically made that design change, and I would not have faced the fear, the delay, and the error.
The decoupling that TDD forces upon us in order to pass isolated inputs and gather isolated outputs is almost always the design that facilitates flexibility and promotes change.
REPL driven development feels easier and faster than TDD; but it is not.
So I postponed the change until I could muster the courage, and set aside the dedicated time it would require.
posts.key_insight: The discipline TDD imposes through mocking and isolation isn't overhead—it's precisely the architectural flexibility you'll need when requirements change.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob recounts being disinvited from a Chicago conference twelve days before the event because 'Code of Conduct' people and some speakers objected to his political opinions. He frames the situation in legal terms, arguing the conference breached their contract and the objecting speakers committed tortious interference. Rather than pursue legal action, he announces a competing free talk at the same time as the conference. The post serves as both a public airing of grievance and a warning to future conference organizers about the consequences of such disinvitations.
key_points: Conference organizers disinvited him 12 days before the event due to objections about his political opinions
He frames the disinvitation as breach of contract despite the pro-bono nature of the engagement
He accuses the objecting speakers of tortious interference for forcing his removal
His friend who organized the event admitted being 'scared to death' of the Code of Conduct enforcers
He announces a competing free talk at the same time as the conference as a form of retaliation
money_quotes: I'm scared to death of these people.
The conference organizers are in breach of contract.

Those speakers would not have been harmed by speaking in a virtual conference that I also spoke in. Their intent was to damage me by forcing the conference organizers to breach their contract with me.
Disinviting someone from a virtual conference who can draw a potentially large audience away from that virtual conference is not a particularly intelligent tactic.
Next time I do one of these pro-bono events I'll have the organizers agree to paying a hefty cancellation fee.
posts.key_insight: Uncle Bob views his disinvitation over political opinions as both a contractual wrong and a symptom of fear-driven conformity in the tech community, responding with a competitive counter-event rather than legal action.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that while codes of conduct at conferences are well-intentioned, they have become weaponized to exclude people based on political opinions or personal grudges rather than actual harassment. He recounts being disinvited from Chicago Cloud Conference after complaints were filed against him, allegedly related to Twitter activity, without being given any opportunity to defend himself or even know who accused him. He criticizes the overly broad language in codes of conduct that makes virtually any statement potentially actionable if someone finds it offensive. His solution is to refuse participation in any conference that publishes a code of conduct without also publishing a fair adjudication process that includes the right to face one's accusers and mount a defense.
key_points: Codes of conduct have evolved from protecting against genuine harassment to being used as weapons for excluding people based on political views or personal animosity
The author was disinvited from a conference based on complaints he was never informed about, with no opportunity to defend himself against unknown accusers
Overly broad conduct rules that prohibit anything 'offensive to anyone' are impossible standards that could apply to virtually any statement
Without published adjudication processes, conferences enable secret tribunals where small groups can expel attendees without accountability
Due process protections—knowing your accuser, being able to defend yourself—are essential safeguards that codes of conduct currently lack
money_quotes: As much as I think that codes of conduct are a good idea, we must not allow them to be weaponized.
If we are going to set up rules with consequences, then we also need to set up the the due processes by which those rules and consequences are adjudicated.
That's quite a standard. I don't think any of us could withstand it. We've all said or written things that have offended, or could offend someone.
In short this means that if someone doesn't like you, they can get you kicked out – and there's nothing you can do about it.
An individual, or a small group of people, whom you do not know, will vote in secret, without your knowledge, and without your input.

From now on I will not agree to attend, nor will I agree to speak at, any conference that publishes a code of conduct but does not have a published process for adjudicating code of conduct complaints.
posts.key_insight: Rules without due process aren't safeguards—they're weapons that can be turned against anyone by those willing to exploit them.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob explores the equivalence between nested loops and finite state machines through a series of refactorings of the Prime Factors Kata. Starting with a doubly nested loop in Java and a recursive solution in Clojure, he demonstrates how any nested loop structure can be transformed into a single loop with explicit state management. The journey reveals that functional programs naturally resemble finite state machines because they cannot save state within flow of control through variable mutation. He concludes by imagining a language with no loops at all, where programs are simply FSM-style switch statements executed repeatedly.
key_points: Any program with nested loops can be rewritten as a single loop with explicit state variables
The transformation from nested loops to a Moore-model finite state machine can be done through pure refactoring while keeping tests passing
Functional programs inherently look more like FSMs because they cannot mutate variables during loop execution
A language could theoretically eliminate all loops, replacing them with FSM-style switch statements executed repeatedly
The hidden state machine in imperative code becomes explicit when you cannot rely on mutable variables for flow control
money_quotes: The value, if any, is in the journey itself.
We have transformed the code from a doubly nested loop, to a single loop, without affecting the algorithm.
The Java program can save some state information within the flow of control, because it can mutate variables while the loops are in progress.
The Clojure program cannot save any state within the flow of control because no variables can be mutated at all.
Thus, functional programs tend to look much more like Finite State Machines than programs that are free to manipulate variables.
That means the loop knew nothing at all about the algorithm it was looping.
In effect the program would look like a tail-call-optimized recursive function.
posts.key_insight: Functional programs naturally reveal the finite state machine structure that imperative code hides within its mutable flow of control.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob defends the continued relevance of the SOLID principles against criticism that they are outdated in the age of microservices and reduced inheritance usage. He systematically addresses each principle, arguing that software fundamentals haven't changed since 1945 - it's still sequence, selection, and iteration. He responds specifically to Dan North's 'just write simple code' advice, contending that while the sentiment is correct, simplicity requires disciplined principles to achieve. The core argument is that each generation mistakenly believes their technical context is fundamentally different from their predecessors.
key_points: Software hasn't fundamentally changed since 1945 - it remains if statements, while loops, and assignments, so principles from decades ago still apply
Microservices don't eliminate the need for SRP; you can create tangled microservices just as easily as tangled monoliths
LSP is about sub-typing and interfaces, not just inheritance - all implementations of interfaces are subtypes that users must understand consistently
The advice to 'just write simple code' without guiding principles is how you get complicated messes
Every new generation thinks their world is vastly different; every generation is wrong about that
money_quotes: Software is still if statements, while loops, and assignment statements — Sequence, Selection, and Iteration.
Every new generation likes to think that their world is vastly different from the generation before. Every new generation is wrong about that.
You can create a tangled microservice, or a tangled set of microservices if you mix code that changes for different reasons.
Of all the principles, the idea that anyone would question this one fills me full of dread for the future of our industry.
Simple code is both open and closed.
This principle is about keeping abstractions crisp and well-defined. It is impossible to believe that this is an outmoded concept.
If the years have taught us anything it is that simplicity requires disciplines guided by principles.
The best way to make a complicated mess is to tell everyone to 'just be simple' and give them no further guidance.
posts.key_insight: Simplicity in software is not achieved by vague exhortations but by disciplined adherence to principles that define what simplicity means in practice.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob provides practical guidelines for when and how to use pair programming effectively. He argues that pairing works best for the 'vast middle' of programming tasks—non-trivial but not deeply complex problems, which constitute about 90% of programming work. He emphasizes that pairing should always be voluntary and informal, never mandated by management. Sessions should be kept short (20-40 minutes), and even mature teams might only pair about 50% of the time, with the benefits extending to non-paired code through knowledge sharing.
key_points: Deep problems requiring heavy concentration don't lend themselves to pairing, though starting with a pair can help identify if simpler solutions exist
Trivial tasks like fleshing out error messages don't warrant pairing's overhead
The 'vast middle' of non-trivial but not deep problems (90% of programming) is where pairing provides the most value
Pairing must be voluntary, never forced or tracked by management
Short sessions (20-40 minutes) work best, with benefits extending far beyond the code actively written together
money_quotes: Deep problems, that require much heavy thinking, do not often lend themselves to pairing. The interaction between the programmers tends to disrupt the necessary concentration.
It is not uncommon for programmers to get caught in a problem that they think is deep, but for which there is a much simpler solution that another programmer could quickly see.
Pairing should always be voluntary, never be forced, never be scheduled by a manager, and never tracked.
Some people can't, or won't do it. That's ok; but it may require that their participation in certain projects be curtailed.
During the pairing sessions, a large amount of code will be reviewed; far more than the pair is actively writing.
Don't be a jerk. Pair sometimes, don't pair other times.
Pair enough so that you have a good grasp of the overall system, and know enough of what your teammates are doing that you could step into their roles if the need arose.
posts.key_insight: Pair programming is most effective when applied voluntarily to the vast middle ground of non-trivial but not deeply complex problems, with benefits that extend well beyond the code written during pairing sessions.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that if/else/switch statements are problematic not just because they proliferate and become hard to maintain, but primarily because of their dependency structure. These statements create 'dependency magnets' where high-level modules transitively depend on low-level modules, binding systems into tight monolithic architectures. The solution is polymorphism: replace the switch with a base class interface, move the single remaining decision point to a factory in main, and let all dependencies point toward higher-level modules. This preserves the same runtime behavior while enabling independently deployable components.
key_points: Replicated if/else/switch statements across a codebase create fragility because changes easily miss some instances
The deeper problem is dependency structure: switch statements become 'dependency magnets' creating transitive dependencies from high-level to low-level modules
Polymorphism solves this by inverting dependencies - high-level modules use a base class interface while low-level details implement it
All three approaches (if/else, switch, polymorphic dispatch) use table lookups with similar runtime and memory characteristics
The factory containing the sole if/else/switch should live in main, using simple tokens (strings/integers) rather than type-safe enums to preserve component boundaries
money_quotes: The proliferation of if/else/switch statements is a common problem in software systems.

This turns the if/else/switch statements into dependency magnets that reach across large swathes of the system source code, binding the system into a tight monolithic architecture without a flexible component structure.
The solution to this problem is to break those outwards dependencies on the lower level modules. This can be done with simple polymorphism.
The decision about which case to follow must have been made before those high level policy modules invoked the base class interface.
It may not be type safe. Indeed, it cannot be type safe. But it will allow you to preserve the component structure of your architecture.
Isn't it fascinating how interesting a discussion of a simple if/else/switch can be?
posts.key_insight: The real problem with if/else/switch statements isn't code duplication but dependency structure - they become magnets that transitively bind high-level modules to low-level details, which polymorphism elegantly solves by inverting those dependencies.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob traces his 57-year programming journey through various type systems, from untyped assembler and C, through statically typed C++ and Java, to dynamically typed Ruby and finally Clojure. He initially rejected high-level languages as inferior to assembler, then embraced static typing after nightmare scenarios with C's type-free approach. After years with C++ and Java, he grew tired of rigid static type systems and found freedom in dynamic languages. He now settles on Clojure, where the clojure/spec library gives him control over type enforcement when and where he needs it, essentially enabling Design by Contract on his own terms.
key_points: His career followed a clear arc: untyped (assembler, C) → static (C++, Java) → dynamic (Python, Ruby, Clojure)
Early rejection of Pascal's type system as 'too constraining' foreshadowed his eventual preference for dynamic typing
C was effectively untyped despite having declared types because the compiler didn't enforce proper usage
Clojure's spec library provides strong typing controlled by the programmer rather than the compiler, enabling selective enforcement
After 11 years with Clojure and dabbling in Go, Kotlin, Haskell, Scala, and F#, he expects Clojure to be his final language
money_quotes: Real programmers programmed in assembler.
I found the type system far too constraining, and didn't trust all the magic behind the scenes.
Oh, that's not to say that C didn't have declared types. It's just that the compiler didn't bother to check that you were using those types properly.
I had overcome the feeling of being handcuffed by a strong type system and became quite adept at building type models.
I've even played with Scala and F#. I keep looking as new languages arise; but have found nothing that calls me to switch away from Clojure.
Instead of that typing being controlled by the compiler, it is controlled by me.
Static typing is not for me. I prefer the flexibility of dynamic typing, and the ability to enforce types if, and when, I need such enforcement.
posts.key_insight: The ideal type system is one where the programmer controls type enforcement rather than being controlled by the compiler—enabling flexibility by default with rigor on demand.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob demonstrates how he built a Turtle Graphics system in Clojure, using clojure/spec to define dynamic type constraints. He argues that dynamic typing with spec allows for more expressive type constraints than most static type systems can achieve, such as asserting relationships between field values. Combined with a comprehensive test suite, he contends this approach provides the same reliability as static typing while offering greater flexibility. The post concludes by reconciling the old TDD vs Design by Contract debate, suggesting both techniques complement each other well.
key_points: Dynamic type systems like clojure/spec can express constraints that are difficult or impossible in most static type systems, such as asserting that a heading must be between 0 and 360, or that an accounting balance sheet sums to zero
Post-conditions that assert type conformance at runtime, combined with exhaustive tests, provide the same safety guarantees as compile-time static type checking
The programmer controls when types are asserted, allowing violations during intermediate computations as long as the final result conforms
TDD and Design by Contract are not opposing philosophies but complementary tools that work well together
Type models should capture not just data structure but meaningful constraints on values and relationships between fields
money_quotes: Since those functions have post conditions that will check the types, and since my suite of tests is exhaustive, I am quite certain that there will be no runtime errors in production and that my dynamic type checking is as robust as any static type system.
The dynamic nature of the type checking allows me to assert type constraints that are very difficult, if not impossible, to assert at compile time.
Moreover, I get to control when types are asserted. It is up to me to decide if and when a certain type should be checked. This gives me a lot of power and flexibility.
It allows me to violate the type rules in the midst of computations, so long as the end result ends up conforming to the types.
What clojure/spec has taught me is that the two play very well together, and both should be in every programmer's toolkit.

The sum of the assets, liabilities and equities on the balance sheet must be zero. This is easy to assert in clojure/spec but is difficult, if not impossible, to assert in most statically typed languages.
posts.key_insight: Dynamic type checking with comprehensive tests offers the same safety as static typing while enabling more expressive constraints and greater programmer control over when types are validated.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob recounts his early programming experiences on the PDP-8, starting as a teenager visiting DEC's sales office with his father and later working professionally with the machine. Decades later, he built a PDP-8 emulator in Lua on his iPad using Codea, which he recently resurrected after Apple's policy changes. As a challenge, he decided to solve Project Euler Problem 4 (finding palindromic number factors) in PDP-8 assembly language using TDD. While initially nostalgic, the experience quickly became tedious grinding work, reinforcing how far programming tools have advanced.
key_points: Bob's father drove him 30 minutes each way on Saturdays to play with a PDP-8 at the DEC sales office - a formative experience that shaped his career
He built a working PDP-8 emulator in Lua on his iPad, which survived Apple's temporary lockdown of code-sharing in Codea
The PDP-8 was extremely primitive - it could only add, not even subtract (requiring two's complement tricks)
Using TDD to develop assembly subroutines for multiplication and division brought back old instincts but became tedious work
The nostalgic appeal of retro programming quickly gives way to appreciation for modern tools - working on a PDP-8 is a 'PITA'
money_quotes: Down the rathole I went.

Was this fun? Yes, at first it was kinda cool to reminisce, and to feel all the old knowledge and instincts come flooding back into my brain. But once the 'novelty' wore off, it stopped being fun, and just turned into work -- grinding, tedious, work.
Working on a PDP-8 is a PITA, even when with all the cheats I supply in my Emulator.
The poor PDP-8 could only add. It couldn't even subtract. Subtraction was accomplished by using twos-complement addition (let the reader understand;-)
This code solves the problem; but I'm quite sure it has some really nasty bugs anyway. I am in no way proud of this code. I'm just not willing to improve it.
Anyway, be careful. The lure of the rathole is very compelling.
posts.key_insight: Nostalgia for early computing is compelling but deceptive - the primitive tools that shaped us were genuinely painful to use, and we shouldn't romanticize the struggle.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob recounts discovering a subtle bug in his functional Space War game where bases were being duplicated, causing crashes. Despite disciplined TDD and clean code practices, the bug eluded his tests because it involved multiple Klingons stealing from the same base simultaneously. In functional programming, updating immutable objects requires creating new versions, and when two thieves targeted one victim, two separate updated copies of the base were created and both added back to the world. The fix required restructuring the algorithm using hashmaps, with existing unit tests providing safety during the refactoring.
key_points: TDD and clean code don't guarantee bug-free software—edge cases can still slip through when tests don't cover all scenarios
Functional programming's immutability creates a specific class of bugs: when updating m elements from n, you must carefully partition and recombine to avoid duplication
The bug was hard to find because it required specific game conditions (multiple Klingons near one base) that took 20+ minutes to occur naturally
Debugging required adding detection functions throughout the code to narrow down where duplication occurred
Existing unit tests provided a safety net when refactoring the algorithm to fix the bug
money_quotes: I wrote this with TDD, and I was very disciplined about the cleanliness of the code, and the test coverage. So this was unexpected.
Unfortunately being able to detect that the duplication occurred did not tell me where it occurred.
In a purely functional program you cannot update the status of an object. Instead you transform old objects into new objects.
If you are writing functional programs, you will face this issue, and you'll likely face it a lot.
Dealing with immutable lists of objects means that when you update such a list you must recreate it.
posts.key_insight: Functional programming's immutability requires careful handling when multiple operations target the same object, as each creates a separate copy that must be properly merged rather than naively concatenated.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob reflects on returning to his Space War game project after three years away, using it as an example of how functional programming and good practices make code maintainable. He contrasts his 1978 character-graphics version with his modern Clojure/Quil implementation. The key revelation is that pure functional code—with no mutable state—eliminated the usual anxiety of revisiting old code. Combined with comprehensive tests and clojure/spec type descriptions, he was able to make progress quickly and confidently without the typical 'wading through legacy code' feeling.
key_points: Pure functional programming (no mutation) made the codebase easy to understand after three years because functions had no hidden side effects
A comprehensive test suite covering game logic provided confidence when making changes
clojure/spec declarations served as living documentation for data structures, providing type-like benefits in a dynamic language
The relief of returning to well-written functional code was 'palpable' compared to typical legacy code anxiety
The Clojure codebase was so clean it converted to ClojureScript with minimal changes, demonstrating portability
money_quotes: No variables were mutated, anywhere in the code. This meant that every function did exactly what it said it did; and left no detritus around to confound other functions.
No function could be impacted by the state of the system because the system did not have 'a state'.
The relief I experienced coming back into this code base after three years of not touching it, and knowing it was functional, was palpable.
I never had that feeling of wading through bogs of legacy code.
I'm sure you've had that feeling of trepidation when you pick up a code base that you haven't seen in three years.
These kinds of clojure/spec descriptions gave me the documentation I needed to reaquaint myself with the critical data structures of the system.
posts.key_insight: Pure functional programming eliminates the anxiety of returning to old code because immutability guarantees that functions do exactly what they say with no hidden state to confound your understanding.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob argues that functional programming languages should still use class-like structures to organize code, even without explicit class keywords. He defines a class broadly as a cohesive group of functions operating on a shared data structure. Using his Clojure project 'more-speech' as an example, he demonstrates how extracting a 'relay' abstraction from a bloated 'protocol' module improved his design. The post critiques Java's overreach in forcing everything into classes and making methods polymorphic by default, while maintaining that the fundamental principle of cohesive partitioning transcends language paradigms.
key_points: A class is fundamentally a cohesive batch of functions operating on shared data, not a language keyword
Java and C# overreached by forcing everything into classes and making polymorphism the default
Functional programming's lack of class enforcement can lead to lazy, ad-hoc module organization
The principle of loose coupling and internal cohesion applies regardless of programming paradigm
Objects exist in all programs and should be properly classified, even in functional languages
money_quotes: A class is a group of cohesive and narrowly defined functions that operate on an encapsulated data structure.
The rules don't change just because you've chosen to use immutable data structures.
Polymorphism is a tool, not a rule.
Abstraction is the amplification of the essential, and the elimination of the irrelevant.
A place for everything, and everything in its place.
Objects exist in programs, and they can, and should, be classified.
The essential characteristic of these elements, these batches of functions, is that they are internally cohesive.
posts.key_insight: The principles of cohesive organization and proper abstraction through classification apply to all programming paradigms—functional programmers should embrace class-like structures even without language enforcement.

posts.summary: Uncle Bob responds to confusion and criticism from his previous post about classes in functional programming by providing a concrete Clojure code example. Using a 'Dilithium Cloud' class from his spacewar game, he demonstrates that classes can exist in functional programming without keywords, polymorphism, or inheritance. He defines a class as 'an intentionally named abstraction that consists of a set of narrowly cohesive functions that operate over an internally defined data structure.' The post playfully mocks static typing purists while showing how dynamic type checking, constructors, and methods can all exist in a functional paradigm.
key_points: A class doesn't require the class keyword, polymorphic dispatch, or inheritance - it's simply a description of an object
Types can be defined dynamically in Clojure using specs, with validation functions for testing
Functional methods differ from traditional OO methods by operating on collections, avoiding polymorphism, and returning new immutable objects
Cohesive functions operating over an internal data structure within a named abstraction constitute a class regardless of paradigm
The OO vs FP distinction is less fundamental than purists on either side suggest
money_quotes: A class is an intentionally named abstraction that consists of a set of narrowly cohesive functions that operate over an internally defined data structure.
We do not need the class keyword. Nor do we need polymorphic dispatch. Nor do we need inheritance. A class is just a description, whether in full or in part, of an object.

To all the adherents of the Statically Typed Functional Programming religion: I know that you believe that Static Typing is an essential aspect of Functional Programming and that no mere dynamically typed language could ever begin to approach the heights and glory of The One True and Holy TYPED Functional Apotheotic Paradigm.
So are these really methods of the cloud class? Sure! Why not? They are a set of narrowly cohesive functions that manipulate an internal data structure within an intentionally named abstraction.
posts.key_insight: The concept of a class transcends language paradigms - it's fundamentally about organizing cohesive functions around a data structure, whether you call it OO or functional programming.
